He uses the same word of condemnation — ‘Woe!’ — that he has pronounced six times against the people. He is not above them; he is one of them: ‘a man of unclean lips among a people of unclean lips.’

questions.header: 6:5–7: The Sinfulness of the Prophet
questions.question: What does the Lord do? What implication does this have for the people of God as a whole?
questions.answer: The seraph touches Isaiah’s lips with a burning coal from the altar. The Lord takes away his guilt and atones for his sin. Isaiah was unclean among an unclean people — which implies forgiveness is possible for the people too. The means of cleansing is the altar: sacrifice and atonement. A powerful pointer toward what Christ will ultimately provide.

questions.header: 6:8–10: The Hardness of the People
questions.question: What commission does the Lord give Isaiah? What will be the result of his ministry?
questions.answer: To go and speak to the people. But the result will be that they are hardened — deaf, blind, unwilling to repent — leading to judgment. The word of God does not leave people where they are: it either softens or hardens. This is not God causing hardening maliciously, but the word confirming the response of hearts already turning away.

questions.header: 6:11–13: The Triumph of God’s Grace
questions.question: What hope are we left with?
questions.answer: Despite utter destruction, the Lord will save a remnant — and that remnant leads ultimately to the Messiah. The ‘stump’ imagery is key: despite Judah being cut down, a stump remains. Cf. 11:1 for the Messianic implications. Death and judgment do not have the last word; the grace of God does.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: What response did these chapters call from Judah? What do they demand of us today?
questions.answer:
For Judah: recognise the grace of God — ‘what more could have been done?’; repent of greed, self-indulgence, and self-importance; fear the Lord more than the Assyrians; recognise their own sinfulness and the glorious holiness of the Lord; hope in the Messiah from the stump.

For us: the holiness of the Lord confronts us with our own sin. We, like Isaiah, are people of unclean lips. But the coal from the altar points us to Christ. The warning about hardening is real — we cannot hear the word of God and remain unmoved.
questions.leaderNote: John 15:1–17 is helpful for applying the vineyard imagery. Note also Jesus’s Parable of the Tenants (Matthew 21:33–46; Mark 12:1–12; Luke 20:9–19). For the hardening and its NT application, see Mark 4:12; Acts 28:26–27; 2 Corinthians 2:15–16.

chapters.title: Isaiah 7:1–9:7 — Trust in the Lord Alone, for Immanuel is Coming
chapters.bigIdea: Trust in the Lord alone, for Immanuel — God’s for ever king — will bring judgment and eternal light, peace, and righteousness.

background.body:
This session sits at the heart of chapters 6–12, the section focused on the theme of the King. It begins with a specific historical crisis — the Syro-Ephraimite War of 735 BC — and uses it as the occasion for one of Isaiah’s most theologically significant prophecies.

The Syro-Ephraimite crisis (7:1–2). Rezin king of Aram (Syria) and Pekah king of Israel (Ephraim, the northern kingdom) had formed an anti-Assyrian coalition and were attacking Jerusalem, apparently trying to force Ahaz to join them or replace him with a compliant king. Ahaz was terrified (v2). His strategy was to buy Assyrian protection (2 Kings 16) — which the Lord explicitly tells him not to do.

Ahaz versus Isaiah. The contrast between Ahaz and Isaiah is the theological spine of this section. Ahaz refuses the sign, refuses to trust, and turns to Assyria. Isaiah and his family stand as a ‘remnant’ of true faith in the middle of faithless Judah. The names of Isaiah’s sons function as living prophetic messages: Shear-Jashub (‘a remnant will return’) and Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz (‘quick to the plunder, swift to the spoil’).

The double fulfilment of Immanuel (7:14). The sign has a near fulfilment in events of Isaiah’s own day (see 8:1–4: before the child is old enough to know right from wrong, the Syro-Ephraimite threat will be gone) and an ultimate fulfilment in Jesus (Matthew 1:22–23). ‘Immanuel — God with us’ is the key: the presence of God with his people in a time of crisis.

The child of 9:6–7. The poem of 9:1–7 is one of the most majestic in Isaiah. Its four titles for the coming king — Wonderful Counsellor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace — go far beyond anything that could be said of an ordinary Davidic king. The identification of the child as ‘Mighty God’ (a title used of the Lord himself in Isaiah 10:21) makes the divine claim explicit. This child fulfils the promise to David of an eternal kingdom (2 Samuel 7:12–16).

keyPoints.body:
The Lord’s extraordinary invitation to trust is met by Ahaz’s polite refusal. Ahaz wraps his rejection in pious language but his actions reveal the truth: he has already decided to trust Assyria (2 Kings 16:7–8). The offer of any sign he chooses is extraordinary grace — and his refusal is one of the most revealing moments of unbelief in the Old Testament.

Immanuel is the Lord’s answer to Ahaz’s unbelief. The sign simultaneously brings judgment (the land will be devastated before the child is grown) and hope (God is present, God is acting, the threat will pass).

Isaiah himself models the faith Ahaz refuses. In 8:12–18, Isaiah fears the Lord rather than the Assyrians and trusts the Lord’s word rather than seeking guidance from mediums. Isaiah and his children are ‘signs and symbols in Israel from the Lord’ (8:18) — a living demonstration that it is possible to trust the Lord in crisis.

The light of 9:1–7 follows the darkness of 8:19–22. The sequence is deliberate: chapter 8 ends in utter darkness; then 9:1: ‘the people walking in darkness have seen a great light.’ The contrast is total. And the light comes through a birth.

The child’s titles in 9:6 demand more than a human king. ‘Mighty God’ and ‘Everlasting Father’ cannot be applied to a mere human being. The New Testament’s identification of Jesus as this child is not a reinterpretation but a recognition.

exegetical.body:
‘The upper pool of the aqueduct’ (7:3). The same location appears in 36:2 (the Assyrian field commander confronting Hezekiah’s officials). The repetition is likely deliberate — reminding the reader of the earlier offer of trust and the contrasting responses of Ahaz and Hezekiah.

‘Curds and honey’ (7:15, 22). A sign of poverty and desolation — the sparse diet of a land devastated and left to wild cattle. Not a sign of blessing but of judgment.

‘The gently flowing waters of Shiloah’ (8:6). Jerusalem’s modest, reliable water supply — a picture of the Lord’s quiet, faithful provision. The people have rejected this in favour of the ‘flooding’ of the Assyrian empire, and thereby invited the very flood they were trying to avoid.

The stone/rock of stumbling (8:14). The Lord himself will be a sanctuary for those who fear him but a stone of stumbling for those who do not. Picked up in Romans 9:33 and 1 Peter 2:8 as applying to Jesus.

Galilee of the nations (9:1). The regions mentioned were most exposed to Assyrian attack and most despised as ‘mixed’ territory. That salvation should begin here rather than in Jerusalem is a deliberate reversal. Matthew 4:12–17 identifies Jesus’s Galilean ministry as the fulfilment of this prophecy.

pastorals.body:
The Immanuel prophecy (7:14) is familiar from Christmas readings. The challenge is to help the group engage with the original context before moving to the fulfilment. The sign was given to Ahaz in a moment of political crisis and his refusal to trust the Lord, despite an extraordinary offer, is the pastoral heart of the session. Don’t rush past Ahaz to get to Matthew 1.

The titles of 9:6 are well-known to Christians and can be handled too quickly as a result. Spend time on each title, particularly ‘Mighty God’ and ‘Everlasting Father’, and help the group feel their weight.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group moving too quickly to Matthew 1 and away from the weight of the original context. The Immanuel sign was given to a king who refused to trust — and who paid the consequences. That is as much part of the passage’s message as the joy of 9:6–7.

questions.header: 7:1–17: Who do you trust?
questions.question: What problem is Ahaz and the people of Judah facing (v1)? How are they responding to it? (v2)
questions.answer: An alliance of Aram and Israel (Ephraim) has come against Jerusalem — the Syro-Ephraimite War, approximately 735 BC. The people are utterly terrified: hearts shaking like trees in the wind. Ahaz appears to have been seeking Assyrian protection rather than trusting the Lord.

questions.header: 7:1–17: Who do you trust?
questions.question: What kind of king was Ahaz? (See 2 Kings 16; 2 Chronicles 28.) How does the Lord show him grace at his point of crisis? (vv2–9)
questions.answer: Ahaz was a bad king who promoted idolatry and did not trust the Lord. Despite this, the Lord graciously sends Isaiah with encouragement. He sends Isaiah along with Shear-Jashub — whose name (‘a remnant will return’) is itself an encouragement that the Lord will not utterly abandon his people. The Lord asks Ahaz to remain calm, noting his enemies are ‘just smouldering stubs of firewood’ — nothing before the Lord.

questions.header: 7:1–17: Who do you trust?
questions.question: What message does the Lord give Isaiah for Ahaz?
questions.answer: ‘Be careful, keep calm, and don’t be afraid. Do not lose heart’ (v4). The Lord assures Ahaz the plans of Rezin and Pekah will fail. The condition is faith: ‘If you do not stand firm in your faith, you will not stand at all’ (v9). The Lord even offers Ahaz a confirming sign of any kind he chooses — an extraordinary offer of grace to an unbelieving king.

questions.header: 7:1–17: Who do you trust?
questions.question: What response does the Lord ask of Ahaz and what response does Ahaz make? (Cf. also 2 Kings 16.)
questions.answer: The Lord asks Ahaz to trust him and offers any sign he chooses. Ahaz refuses — cloaked in pious language (‘I will not put the Lord to the test’) but revealing the true state of his heart. 2 Kings 16 shows he had already decided: he sent silver and gold from the temple to Tiglath-Pileser and invited him to act on his behalf. The piety is a mask over unbelief.

questions.header: 7:1–17: Who do you trust?
questions.question: The Lord responds to Ahaz’s lack of trust by giving his own sign. What is it and what does it refer to?
questions.answer: The sign of Immanuel: a virgin will conceive and give birth to a son called ‘God with us’. The near fulfilment: before the child is old enough to know right from wrong, the Syro-Ephraimite threat will be gone (v16; cf. 8:1–4). The ultimate fulfilment: Matthew 1:22–23 identifies Jesus as the Immanuel of Isaiah’s prophecy.
questions.leaderNote: The Hebrew word almah means ‘young woman of marriageable age’ and strongly implies but doesn’t exclusively specify virginity. The LXX used parthenos (virgin) and Matthew follows this. The double fulfilment — near and ultimate — is the key to interpreting the passage rightly.

questions.header: 7:17–25: That day will bring judgment
questions.question: How will the Lord respond to his people’s sin and lack of faith?
questions.answer: He will judge them by sending the king of Assyria. Ahaz turned to trust Assyria, but the Assyrians are actually under the Lord’s control — used to bring judgment on his disobedient people. The very tool Ahaz runs to becomes the instrument of judgment against him.

questions.header: 7:17–25: That day will bring judgment
questions.question: Isaiah speaks of God’s judgment with four references to ‘in that day’. What will ‘that day’ bring? (vv18–25)
questions.answer: Overwhelming, humiliation, impoverishment, agricultural ruin. Note again the Lord ‘whistling’ for the Assyrians (like 5:26) — they are at his beck and call, using them to judge his own people.

questions.header: 8:1–4: Partial fulfilment of the Immanuel sign
questions.question: How do 8:1–4 expand on and partially fulfil the sign promised in 7:14–15? (Note any repetition of words or ideas.)
questions.answer: ‘Before the boy knows how to say “My father” or “My mother”’ (v4) — the idea is that judgment will come while the baby is still very young, as in 7:16. This partly fulfils the sign the Lord gave. The name Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz (‘quick to the plunder, swift to the spoil’) emphasises the speed of judgment.

questions.header: 8:5–8: How will the Lord respond to his people’s rejection?
questions.question: How will the Lord respond to his people’s rejection of him?
questions.answer: They have rejected the ‘gently flowing waters of Shiloah’ — the Lord’s quiet, faithful provision — in favour of the flooding Assyrian empire. So the Lord will send the Assyrians to almost overwhelm them: the imagery is of a flood barely below the chin. They have invited the flood they were trying to avoid.

questions.header: 8:5–8: Immanuel in comfort and judgment
questions.question: Why do you think v8 ends with addressing ‘Immanuel’?
questions.answer: Even the land being overwhelmed by the Assyrian flood is addressed as ‘Immanuel’ — God with us. The promise of 7:14 is present even in the midst of judgment. The land being overrun is still God’s land. Judgment is not the final word: the presence of God with his people is not cancelled by their punishment.

questions.header: 8:9–18: Immanuel in comfort and judgment
questions.question: How is Isaiah himself to respond to the military threat? How is he a contrast to Ahaz and Judah? (vv9–18)
questions.answer: Where Ahaz dreaded Rezin and Remaliah, Isaiah is to fear the Lord. He is to trust the Lord (v17) and with his family be signs for Israel (v18). The Lord is either a sanctuary or a stumbling block — for Ahaz he will cause stumbling, but for Isaiah he is a holy place. Isaiah is a ‘remnant’ of the true people of God.

questions.header: 8:19–22: Where are the people tempted to turn?
questions.question: Where are the people tempted to turn for comfort and what will it lead to? (vv19–22)
questions.answer: Turning to mediums and spiritists rather than the living God (v19). But they will find only distress and utter darkness (vv21–22). Only the word of the Lord can bring light. Those who reject his word are left with no word — and no light.

questions.header: 9:1–7: The child who is God’s chosen King brings light in darkness
questions.question: How is the Lord going to burst into the darkness? What is the significance of the place names in v1? (Cf. Matthew 4:12–17.)
questions.answer: He is going to come with light — the contrast between 8:22 and 9:1 is total. The places named (Zebulun and Naphtali, Galilee of the nations) were most exposed to Assyrian attack and most despised as ‘mixed’ territory. Matthew 4:12–17 identifies Jesus’s Galilean ministry as the fulfilment of this prophecy. The great light dawns precisely where the darkness was deepest.

questions.header: 9:1–7: The child who is God’s chosen King brings light in darkness
questions.question: What is the Lord going to do?
questions.answer: He is going to bring light, blessing, joy, freedom from oppression and from war. The images accumulate — harvest joy, dividing plunder, burning of soldiers’ boots and bloodstained garments. Pause to let the group feel the cumulative richness before moving to v6.

questions.header: 9:1–7: The child who is God’s chosen King brings light in darkness
questions.question: What is the surprise of v6? How does it connect to and expand upon 7:14?
questions.answer: All this — the light, joy, freedom, peace — will come through the birth of a child. And the birth of the child was the promised sign of the presence of God (Immanuel) among his people. Not a great military leader but a child. The sign of 7:14 is expanded: Immanuel has a throne.

questions.header: 9:1–7: The child who is God’s chosen King brings light in darkness
questions.question: What do vv6–7 tell us about the child? What will he be like and what will he bring?
questions.answer:
Wonderful Counsellor — surpassing wisdom.
Mighty God — a title used of the Lord himself (cf. Isaiah 10:21); the child is divine.
Everlasting Father — eternal fatherly care for his people.
Prince of Peace — rules in shalom.

He will rule for ever on David’s throne (fulfilling 2 Samuel 7:12–16), characterised by justice and righteousness. The Lord will accomplish this.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: What did the sign of Immanuel mean for the people of Isaiah’s day? How did it respond to both their sin and their fear? Now think about how it was fulfilled in Jesus. How does the original context help us understand the coming of Jesus better?
questions.answer: The sign came in a moment of political crisis and unbelief, offered to a king who refused to trust the Lord. God with us answers both Ahaz’s fear (his enemies are nothing before the Lord) and his sin (God has not abandoned his people). The fulfilment in Jesus is the answer to the deepest human crisis: not merely the Assyrians but sin and death. Understanding the original context — a sign given in darkness, pointing to a presence that will not abandon — makes the incarnation more profound, not less.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: How did Ahaz and Isaiah respond differently to the Lord and his word? What does this teach us about the nature of faith and the temptation to reject the Lord? (Look again at 8:12–15 and its NT applications.) What does it look like for us to put our faith in the Lord like Isaiah?
questions.answer: Ahaz faced the same Lord and the same crisis as Isaiah — but chose to manage the situation in his own way. Isaiah chose to fear the Lord rather than the Assyrians. The NT applications of 8:14 (Romans 9:33; 1 Peter 2:8) show the Lord remains a stone of stumbling or cornerstone depending on how one responds. Faith like Isaiah’s: taking the Lord’s word seriously, refusing security in what the world offers, willing to stand out from the crowd.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: Spend time reflecting on 9:1–7. What has the coming of Immanuel achieved? What difference does Jesus make? We don’t yet see all of this in completion, so what difference do these verses make for how we live now?
questions.answer: Open reflection question. The government is on his shoulders — not ours. The light has come; darkness does not have the last word. The joy is not yet complete but it is real and coming. The titles of v6 tell us who bears the weight of history. We live in the tension between the dawning of the light and its full brightness.
questions.leaderNote: Allow time for this final question — opportunity for genuine worship, not just intellectual discussion. The titles of 9:6 are worth dwelling on one at a time. Don’t rush to the end of the session here.

chapters.title: Isaiah 9:8–10:34 — Assyria is Only the Lord’s Tool
chapters.bigIdea: Assyria is only the Lord’s tool and will one day come under his judgment, so submit to the Lord’s discipline and return to him.

background.body:
This session covers 9:8–10:34 and should be read alongside chapter 11, which is studied in the following session. The notes suggest reading all the way to the end of chapter 11 before the session so the Messianic promise of 11:1–16 is in view even as the group works through the darkness of 9:8–10:34.

The fourfold refrain (9:8–10:4). The section is structured around a repeated refrain: ‘Yet for all this, his anger is not turned away, his hand is still upraised.’ This divides the passage into four distinct indictments, each describing a different aspect of Israel’s sin and the corresponding judgment. The cumulative effect is important — Israel’s sin is stubborn and God’s response is patient but unrelenting.

The Assyrian paradox (10:5–19). One of the most theologically sophisticated passages in Isaiah. The Assyrians are simultaneously the Lord’s instrument of judgment and themselves guilty of pride and overreach. The Lord has ‘sent’ them (v6) but the Assyrians’ intentions are entirely their own (v7) — they are not aware they are serving his purposes. The axe and woodsman metaphor (v15) makes the point starkly. This paradox — human agency fully exercised within divine sovereignty — is one of the deepest theological threads in Isaiah and Scripture as a whole.

The remnant returns (10:20–34). Shear-Jashub (‘a remnant will return’ — the name of Isaiah’s son in 7:3) is explicitly echoed in 10:21–22. The promise has been hanging in the air since chapter 7 and here it is fulfilled, at least in promise: there will be survivors who truly trust in the Lord.

Midian and Egypt (10:26). The references to Gideon’s victory over Midian (Judges 7) and the deliverance from Egypt (Exodus 14) are typological, not nostalgic. Both were victories against overwhelming odds, entirely the Lord’s doing. The Lord has done this before and can do it again: Assyria may seem unstoppable, but the Lord is King.

keyPoints.body:
The fourfold refrain creates a cumulative sense of both sin and patience. ‘His hand is still upraised’ — the Lord has not yet struck, but judgment is coming. Each section shows Israel digging deeper into rebellion while the Lord’s discipline fails to provoke repentance.

The Lord uses pagan powers to discipline his people — but he does not endorse them. The Assyrians do what they want — conquering, plundering, building empire. The Lord is using what they do for his purposes. Their pride, which leads them to think they operate independently of any god, is precisely what will bring them under judgment. The axe cannot claim to be greater than the woodsman.

Pride is the common thread between Israel and Assyria. Israel’s sin in 9:8–10:4 is repeatedly characterised by arrogance — ‘We will rebuild with dressed stone’ (9:10): self-confident recovery rather than repentance. The Assyrians’ sin (10:7–14) is the same: ‘By the strength of my hand I have done this’ (10:13). Both are claiming for themselves what belongs to God alone.

The remnant promise is alive even in the darkest passages. Shear-Jashub has been hanging in the air since 7:3. Now in 10:20–22 it is fulfilled in promise: a remnant will return. They will stop relying on human powers and truly trust in the Lord.

Why chapter 11 matters after 9:8–10:34. The darkness is real and sustained. Chapter 11 is the answer. Don’t end the session without pointing ahead — the stump of 6:13 and the cutting down of 10:33–34 are the necessary prelude to the shoot of 11:1.

exegetical.body:
9:10: ‘We will rebuild with dressed stone.’ The response to discipline is not repentance but defiant self-improvement. The means of their false security (allies, enemies) become the instruments of judgment.

9:13–17: Unrepentant leadership. ‘The people have not returned to him who struck them’ (v13). The leaders are specifically indicted for misleading the people. Poor leadership is both judgment on the people and itself a sin.

9:18–21: Wickedness consuming itself. The self-consuming nature of sin is graphically described: they turn on each other and on Judah. Sin does not simply damage others — it ultimately destroys the sinner.

10:1–4: Injustice and the reversal of roles. Those who made others vulnerable will themselves become vulnerable. The question of v3 — ‘Where will you flee for help?’ — is devastating.

10:5: ‘Woe to the Assyrian, the rod of my anger.’ A startling statement. The Lord himself calls Assyria his ‘rod’ and ‘club’. This is not an endorsement of Assyrian policy but a statement of divine sovereignty: even pagan empire-building serves the Lord’s purposes without the empire-builders knowing it.

10:15: The axe metaphor. ‘Does the axe raise itself above the person who swings it?’ One of the clearest expressions of divine sovereignty and human accountability held together in Isaiah. The Assyrians think they are agents of history; they are the tools. The Lord is the woodsman.

10:26: Midian and Egypt. Gideon’s rout of the Midianites (Judges 7:19–25) and the crossing of the Red Sea (Exodus 14:15–31) were victories against overwhelming odds where only the Lord could be credited. When the Lord acts, military superiority is irrelevant.

pastorals.body:
The fourfold ‘his hand is still upraised’ can feel relentless and hopeless if the group doesn’t keep the remnant promise in view. The Lord’s anger is real, his patience is long, and the remnant will return. The sustained darkness makes the light of chapter 11 more brilliant.

The Assyrian paradox (human agency within divine sovereignty) is theologically rich but can be confusing. Don’t try to resolve the tension — it is one of the most consistent patterns in Scripture (cf. Genesis 50:20; Acts 2:23). The goal is to help the group hold both truths simultaneously: the Assyrians are fully responsible for their pride, and the Lord is using them for his purposes.

The question about seemingly unstoppable powers today is one of the most pastorally relevant in this session. The point is not to identify modern Assyrias with confidence but to trust the Lord’s sovereignty even when human power seems overwhelming.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group getting stuck in the detail of the four indictments and losing the shape of the whole. Aim to land the main insight from each section and keep moving. Save time for the Assyrian paradox (10:5–19), which is the theological heart of the session.

questions.header: 9:8–10:4: The Lord’s hand is still upraised
questions.question: This section is in four parts, each ending with ‘Yet for all this, his anger is not turned away, his hand is still upraised.’ In groups, go through each part and discuss what Israel’s sin is, how the Lord will judge them, and how his judgment fits their sin.
questions.answer:
9:8–12: Pride and arrogance — self-confident rebuilding rather than repentance. The Lord uses their allies and enemies (in whom they trust) to destroy them.

9:13–17: Unrepentant in the face of judgment; ungodly leaders and lying prophets. The Lord takes no pleasure in them and does not pity them — all parts of society are guilty, including the leaders who should know better.

9:18–21: Wickedness so rife it is consuming everything. The Lord’s judgment is the real fire beneath it. They turn even on each other.

10:1–4: Unjust laws and oppression of the vulnerable. The Lord will make them vulnerable in turn — they will have nowhere to flee.
questions.leaderNote: Works well as a group exercise: four sub-groups, one per section, 5–7 minutes, then bring together. The key insight to draw out: each judgment fits the sin — a form of poetic justice reflecting the Lord’s character as both holy and fair.

questions.header: 10:5–19: Does the axe raise itself above the person who swings it?
questions.question: How does the Lord see Assyria? What is Assyria’s divine purpose? (vv5–6)
questions.answer: Assyria is nothing more than the Lord’s tool of discipline — a ‘rod’ and a ‘club’. He is the one dispatching the Assyrians. Their power over Israel is entirely derived from and dependent on the Lord’s commission.

questions.header: 10:5–19: Does the axe raise itself above the person who swings it?
questions.question: How are the Assyrians’ intentions different from the Lord’s? How do the Assyrians see themselves? (vv7–14) Why is their pride such a big problem? (v15)
questions.answer: The Assyrians intend to destroy many nations and make themselves great. They are hugely self-confident, pointing to their long list of conquered nations as proof (vv9–11, 13–14). But this pride is precisely the problem (v15): the axe does not get to claim credit for what the woodsman does. The tool that claims to be greater than its user has confused itself about the nature of reality.

questions.header: 10:5–19: Does the axe raise itself above the person who swings it?
questions.question: What will the Lord do? (vv12, 16–19)
questions.answer: The Lord will punish the king of Assyria for his wilful pride (v12), after finishing using them to discipline Jerusalem. He will consume them and make them weak and few (vv16–19). The reversal is pointed: they seemed all-powerful and uncountable, but the Lord will bring them low. This actually happened historically when the Assyrian army was destroyed outside Jerusalem (37:36).

questions.header: 10:20–34: A remnant will return
questions.question: What will be the result of the Lord’s judgment on Israel?
questions.answer: Devastating destruction, but a small remnant will return to the Lord. They will stop relying on human powers — specifically Assyria, the very thing they had been running to — and truly trust in the Lord. The name Shear-Jashub (‘a remnant will return’ — Isaiah’s son in 7:3) is echoed explicitly in vv21–22. There is hope beyond judgment.

questions.header: 10:20–34: A remnant will return
questions.question: What is the experience of God’s people with the Assyrians? (vv24, 28–34)
questions.answer: They are afraid and oppressed as the Lord uses the Assyrians to judge Israel. Verses 28–34 give a terrifying march southward toward Jerusalem, town by town — a sense of mounting dread graphically narrated.

questions.header: 10:20–34: A remnant will return
questions.question: How does the Lord change their perspective on their situation? (vv25–27)
questions.answer: The Lord puts a limit on it. His anger against his people will be finished, and he will then punish the Assyrians. He will remove the burden of Assyrian rule. The very yoke that seemed permanent will be broken.

questions.header: 10:20–34: A remnant will return
questions.question: What is the significance of referring to Midian and Egypt in v26?
questions.answer: Both were victories against great odds — Gideon’s rout of the Midianites (Judges 7:19–25) and the crossing of the Red Sea (Exodus 14:15–31). In both cases only the Lord could take the credit. The point: Assyria might seem unstoppable but the Lord is King, and he has done this before.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: What does today’s passage teach us about what the Lord values in his people?
questions.answer: He values repentance, humility, and genuine trust — and is grieved by stubborn self-confidence that treats his discipline as an inconvenience to manage rather than a call to return. The fourfold refrain is not a picture of a God who enjoys judgment but of a God whose patience is being tested by a people who refuse to return.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: What does today’s passage teach us about the Lord’s promises?
questions.answer: Even in the darkest judgment, the remnant promise holds. Shear-Jashub has been waiting since 7:3 and here it is fulfilled. The Lord does not abandon his purposes even when his people abandon him. His promises outlast their failures.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: What does today’s passage teach us about the Lord’s sovereignty? How does this help us think about seemingly unstoppable and scary powers today?
questions.answer: The most powerful empire of the ancient world is, in the Lord’s hands, just a rod. The Assyrians do not know they are serving his purposes; that does not change the fact that they are. This gives the people of God a radically different perspective on terrifying human powers: not denial of their danger, but confidence that they operate within the Lord’s purposes and cannot ultimately prevail against his plans.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: Why do you think chapter 11 is so significant after what we have seen in chapters 9–10?
questions.answer: Chapters 9–10 have described the cutting down of Israel, Assyria, and all proud powers. Chapter 10 ends with the Lord himself lopping off the tall trees (vv33–34). Then 11:1: ‘A shoot will come up from the stump of Jesse.’ Out of total apparent death comes new life. The Branch does not come from the impressive cedars of human power but from the humblest point possible: a stump. This is the shape of the gospel.
questions.leaderNote: If the group is reading ahead to chapter 11, use this question to preview the next session. The shoot from the stump of Jesse is the answer to everything chapters 9–10 have described.

chapters.title: Isaiah 11–12 — Praise the Lord for His Righteous Branch
chapters.bigIdea: Praise the Lord because his righteous Branch will rule in peace and gather his people home.

background.body:
Chapters 11–12 form the climax of the first major section of Isaiah (chs 6–12, the ‘Book of the King’) and function as a resolution of everything that has preceded. The darkness of chapters 9–10 finds its answer not in political success or military reversal but in the coming of the Branch from Jesse’s stump.

The Branch (11:1). The Hebrew choter (shoot) and netser (Branch) convey new life emerging from apparent death. The stump of Jesse — not of David the king but of Jesse his father, the original family before the royal line — suggests a return to the very roots, a new beginning from the most basic point. This echoes the cutting down of 6:13 and 10:33–34.

The Spirit (11:2). The seven-fold description of the Spirit is one of the richest pneumatological texts in the OT. In Jewish tradition these seven gifts are associated with the Messianic age. Isaiah 42:1 and 61:1 return to the Spirit resting on the Servant/Anointed One. The NT identifies Jesus’s baptism as the fulfilment of this anointing (Matthew 3:16–17; Luke 4:18).

The new creation (11:6–9). The vision of predator and prey at peace deliberately echoes and reverses the Fall. The passage is closely linked to Isaiah 65:17–25 and Revelation 21–22.

The second Exodus (11:10–16). The gathering of the scattered people of God from the four corners of the earth is described as a new Exodus, vastly expanded: not just Israel from Egypt but scattered exiles from Assyria, Egypt, Cush, Elam, Babylonia, Hamath, and the islands of the sea. The NT applies this to the gathering of the nations through Christ.

The song of chapter 12. Chapter 12 is a short psalm of thanksgiving — two stanzas that bookend the first major section of Isaiah. It echoes the Song of the Sea (Exodus 15) and looks ahead to the songs of the redeemed in Revelation 5:9 and 15:3. The right response to everything in chapters 1–11 is praise.

keyPoints.body:
The Branch is the opposite of Assyria in every way. Chapter 10 described Assyria as lofty, tall, arrogant, and destructive. The Branch comes from a stump, is filled with the Spirit, and judges by righteousness not appearances. Everything Assyria is, the Branch is not; everything Assyria lacks, the Branch has.

The Spirit equips the Branch for everything the leaders of Israel lacked. The catalogue of 11:2–3 reads like a systematic answer to everything wrong with Israel’s leadership in chapters 1–10: wisdom where there was folly, understanding where there was blindness, knowledge and fear of the Lord where there was pride and idolatry.

The Branch’s rule overturns the Fall. The vision of 11:6–9 is a theological statement about the scope of redemption: not just political restoration but the healing of creation itself. The root of all evil is ignorance of the Lord; the earth being filled with his knowledge (v9) addresses the root.

The gathering is for all nations, not just Israel. The nations themselves will rally to the Branch (v10). This is the fulfilment of the promise of 2:1–5 that opened this section. The study has come full circle.

The right response is praise. Chapter 12 gives the group the words. Don’t rush past it — it is the conclusion and the response, and it deserves to be read aloud.

exegetical.body:
‘Jesse’ rather than ‘David’ (11:1). By the time of the Messiah’s coming, the royal Davidic line will have been so reduced that it is back to Jesse — the original family before the glory days. The Messiah does not come from a lineage at the height of its power but from a stump. This is the theology of the cross in seed form.

‘He will not judge by what he sees with his eyes’ (11:3). A direct contrast to the leaders of Israel who have judged by appearances — favouring the rich, oppressing the poor, calling good evil. The Branch’s justice is based on truth, not social status or political expediency.

‘The wolf will live with the lamb’ (11:6). Functions on multiple levels. Literally: the reversal of natural hostility. Politically: the great predator nations and vulnerable prey will no longer relate in those terms. The categories of power and weakness will be transformed under the Branch’s rule.

‘The root of Jesse’ (11:10). Slightly different from the ‘shoot from the stump of Jesse’ in v1 — here the root itself that the nations seek. Paul quotes this in Romans 15:12 as the basis for the Gentile mission: ‘The Root of Jesse will spring up, one who will arise to rule over the nations; in him the Gentiles will hope.’

‘The wells of salvation’ (12:3). Water drawn freely from a source that does not run dry. John 4:14 (the woman at the well) and John 7:37–38 (streams of living water) both draw on this imagery. Salvation is not a scarce resource but an inexhaustible spring.

pastorals.body:
This is one of the most joyful sessions in the whole study. After the sustained darkness of chapters 9–10, the group has earned the right to dwell in chapters 11–12 with delight. Some of the best responses to passages like 11:6–9 and 12:1–6 are simply silence, reading aloud together, or quiet prayer.

The new creation imagery of 11:6–9 can raise questions about whether this is literal or figurative. It is not necessary to resolve this — the theological point is clear regardless: the Branch’s redemption extends to the whole created order. Help the group feel the breadth of that vision.

The application questions at the end ask the group to reflect on the whole of chapters 1–12. This is a good opportunity to step back and see the arc: from the devastating indictment of chapter 1 to the song of praise in chapter 12. Allow time for that bigger reflection.

watch.body: 👁️ Watch for the group treating chapter 12 as an epilogue rather than a conclusion. It is the right response to everything in chapters 1–11 — the answer to ‘so what do we do with all of this?’ is praise and proclamation. If time is short, make sure chapter 12 gets its own space.

questions.header: 11:1–5: The Branch’s character
questions.question: How does the Branch contrast with the way the Assyrians are described in chapter 10?
questions.answer: The Assyrians are lofty, tall, impressive trees that the Lord cuts down (10:33–34). The Branch comes from a stump: humble, apparently lifeless, the least promising origin imaginable. Everything the Assyrians claimed for themselves — power, wisdom, victory — the Branch actually possesses, but through the Spirit rather than through pride.

questions.header: 11:1–5: The Branch’s character
questions.question: What will the Branch be like? How is he different from Assyria and from the leaders of Israel and Judah?
questions.answer: From David’s line (Jesse); full of the Spirit of the Lord; wisdom and understanding; counsel and might; knowledge and fear of the Lord — delighting in the fear of the Lord; judging righteously not superficially; bringing justice to the poor; striking down the wicked; characterised by righteousness and faithfulness. Every quality is the direct opposite of what the Assyrians claimed and what Israel’s leaders demonstrated in chapters 1–10.

questions.header: 11:1–5: The Branch’s character
questions.question: How are vv1–5 fulfilled in Jesus?
questions.answer: Jesus is the Davidic Messiah, anointed with the Spirit at his baptism (Matthew 3:16–17; cf. Luke 4:18, quoting Isaiah 61:1). Everything about his ministry — his wisdom in teaching, his righteous judgment, his care for the poor, his confrontation of the wicked — can be described in the categories of these verses. Invite the group to give specific NT examples for each characteristic.

questions.header: 11:6–9: The Branch’s rule
questions.question: What will the rule of the Branch be like?
questions.answer: Characterised by the global peace and harmony of creation: wolf and lamb, leopard and goat, calf and lion at peace. A little child will lead them. Nothing will harm or destroy on the Lord’s holy mountain because the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the Lord as the waters cover the sea.

questions.header: 11:6–9: The Branch’s rule
questions.question: How does the Branch’s rule fit into the overall story of salvation history?
questions.answer: This is the ultimate overturning of the Fall. The enmity, pain, and death that came into creation in Genesis 3 are reversed. It points forward to Revelation 21 — the new creation where there is no more death, mourning, crying, or pain. The Branch’s reign is the healing of everything that went wrong at the beginning.

questions.header: 11:10–16: The Branch’s people
questions.question: What will the Branch do for the Lord’s people?
questions.answer: Gather them from across the earth — from Assyria, Egypt, Cush, Elam, Babylonia, and the islands of the sea — and destroy any opposition. It will be like a new and greater Exodus: the Lord will dry up the gulf of the Egyptian sea and provide a highway for the remnant.

questions.header: 11:10–16: The Branch’s people
questions.question: How does this point us forward to the ministry of Jesus?
questions.answer: Christ gathers the people of God from across all nations. Note that the Branch gathers not just scattered Israelite exiles but the nations themselves (v10 — ‘the nations will rally to him’). This points forward to the work of Jesus when people from every language, tribe, and nation will be gathered to him, and nothing will be able to stop him building his church.
questions.leaderNote: Paul quotes v10 in Romans 15:12 to justify his Gentile mission. It is worth pointing the group to this — it shows that the universal scope of the Branch’s gathering is not a NT invention but the fulfilment of what Isaiah promised.

questions.header: 12:1–6: The song of the people of God
questions.question: How are the people of God to respond to all this?
questions.answer: With praise and proclamation. The song of chapter 12 is both a response to what the Lord has done and a commission to tell others about it. ‘Give praise to the Lord, proclaim his name; make known among the nations what he has done’ (v4).

questions.header: 12:1–6: The song of the people of God
questions.question: What is to be the focus of their praise and proclamation?
questions.answer: The salvation of the Lord. His anger turned away. His protection. The joyful proclamation of glorious things he has done for his people — not as private spiritual experience but as news to be made known among the nations. The image of drawing water from the wells of salvation (v3) speaks of abundance freely received: salvation is not scarce but an inexhaustible source.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: This ends the first main section in Isaiah. How has what we have learned help us understand the ministry of Jesus better? How does it help us understand the story of salvation history?
questions.answer: Open reflection question — invite the group to name what has struck them most from the first six sessions. The arc from chapter 1 (devastating indictment) through the Immanuel promise (ch7), the darkness of chapters 9–10, to the Branch and the song of chapter 12 is itself a miniature of the whole gospel story: diagnosis, promise, darkness, fulfilment, praise.
questions.leaderNote: A good moment to step back from detailed exegesis and invite broader reflection. What has changed in how the group reads the OT? What understanding of Jesus has deepened? What have they learned about the character of God? Allow at least 5–7 minutes.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: How can we now look differently at our world? What difference does it make to see what the Branch will do ‘on that day’?
questions.answer: Knowing that the Branch’s rule will ultimately cover the earth as the waters cover the sea gives the people of God a radically different perspective on the darkness and brokenness of the present. We live between the first coming of the Branch and his final coming. That in-between position shapes everything about how we face the present.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: How can we respond in praise and proclamation to the Lord?
questions.answer: Open application question. The pattern of chapter 12: receive the Lord’s salvation → respond in praise → proclaim to others. Help the group think concretely about what ‘making known among the nations what he has done’ looks like in their specific context.
questions.leaderNote: Consider closing the session by reading chapter 12 aloud together — perhaps alternating verses or reading responsively. After six sessions of sustained engagement with judgment and hope, the song of chapter 12 deserves to be heard as a song, not just studied as a text.

chapters.title: Isaiah 13–14 — The Day of the Lord on Babylon
chapters.bigIdea: The day is coming when the Lord will judge the earth and its arrogant kings and will show compassion to his people, so find refuge in the Lord.

background.body:
Chapters 13–23 form a section of oracles against surrounding nations. The Lord’s sovereignty is not limited to Israel and Judah — he is Lord of the whole earth, and the nations that oppress his people will themselves come under his judgment. This section was a source of enormous comfort to God’s people as they faced judgment and exile.

Babylon in Scripture. Babylon barely existed as a major power in Isaiah’s day. But in Scripture it carries a significance far beyond its historical moment. From the Tower of Babel (Genesis 11) to the whore of Babylon in Revelation 17–18, Babylon represents humanity united in arrogant rebellion against the Lord. The prophecy is simultaneously historical and cosmic.

The Day of the Lord. This phrase is one of Isaiah’s key theological categories — referring to any occasion when the Lord intervenes in history in judgment and/or salvation, and ultimately to the final intervention at the end of history. Temporal judgments prefigure and point toward the final Day. Help the group hold both levels together.

The fall of Babylon to the Medes (13:17; cf. 539 BC). The Medes (and Persians) under Cyrus overthrew Babylon in 539 BC — the historical fulfilment Isaiah points toward. The naming of the Medes over 150 years before this event is significant testimony to the Lord’s foreknowledge of history.

The king of Babylon (14:4–21). The taunt song against the king of Babylon is one of the most dramatic passages in Isaiah. The primary reference is to the pride of Babylon’s human king and the pride of all human powers that exalt themselves above God.

keyPoints.body:
The Day of the Lord goes beyond Babylon. Chapter 13 barely mentions Babylon by name. The cosmic scope — stars darkened, heavens shaken, the world punished (vv10–11) — signals that more is in view than the fall of one city. Earthly judgments are previews and patterns of the final judgment.

The Lord’s judgment is the humbling of human pride. The reasons for judgment — sin, evil, wickedness, arrogance, haughty pride, ruthlessness (v11) — are the same as those given for the judgment of God’s own people in chapters 1–12. The Lord will not tolerate pride anywhere.

The Lord’s judgment is good news for his people. Chapter 14:1–2 opens with the Lord showing compassion and restoring their fortunes. The judgment of their oppressors is the flip side of their liberation.

No earthly power escapes the grave. The taunt song of 14:4–21 is devastating in its irony: the king who made the earth tremble ends up laid low in the pit, food for maggots (vv9–11). The Lord alone endures.

The Lord’s plan extends to the whole world. Verses 26–27 make explicit that this is not a one-off but a pattern: ‘This is the plan determined for the whole world; this is the hand stretched out over all nations.’ No power in history is outside the Lord’s purposes.

exegetical.body:
‘The Day of the Lord’ (13:6, 9). It carries the sense of the Lord’s decisive intervention in history — always involving both judgment on the wicked and vindication of the righteous. In its ultimate form it is the last judgment; in its penultimate forms it is historical catastrophes that prefigure it.

Cosmic imagery (13:10, 13). The darkening of stars and shaking of the heavens is stock imagery for cataclysmic divine judgment (cf. Joel 2:10–11; Matthew 24:29). It signals the totality of the Lord’s judgment rather than requiring a literal astronomical event.

Sodom and Gomorrah (13:19). Babylon will be overthrown as Sodom and Gomorrah were — total, permanent, uninhabited desolation. Historically, ancient Babylon was indeed eventually abandoned and became ruins.

‘How you have fallen from heaven, morning star, son of the dawn!’ (14:12). The Latin Vulgate translated ‘morning star’ as Lucifer, which became the traditional name for Satan in Western Christianity. The primary reference is to the king of Babylon’s pride and aspiration to ascend above God (vv13–14). Jesus’s words in Luke 10:18 may echo this passage.

The Philistines (14:28–32). The closing verse (32) is striking: ‘The afflicted of his people will find refuge in him’ — amid all the oracles of judgment, this is the destination for God’s people.

pastorals.body:
The oracles can feel remote and even morally troubling. Help the group hold two things: (1) the Lord’s judgment is the response to specific sins described in the text, not arbitrary; (2) temporal judgments point beyond themselves to the final judgment, where all injustice will be reckoned with. The oracles are not a celebration of violence but a testimony to the Lord’s moral seriousness.

The taunt song of 14:4–21 is designed to be heard, not just studied. Read it aloud in the session if possible — the irony of the great king brought low to the pit is both sobering and strangely comforting.

watch.body: Watch for the group getting so absorbed in historical background (who are the Medes? what happened to Babylon?) that they miss the theological point. The main thing is the pattern: the Lord judges arrogant human power, shows compassion to his people, and his plans for the whole world cannot be thwarted.

questions.header: 13:1–16: The Day of the Lord
questions.question: What will the ‘Day of the Lord’ be like?
questions.answer: Gathering armies summoned by the Lord to carry out his judgment. Terror before his coming. Earthly and cosmic desolation that shakes the earth and punishes evil. Nowhere for sinners to hide. The description goes beyond any single historical event in its scope.

questions.header: 13:1–16: The Day of the Lord
questions.question: What clues are there that the judgment described here goes further than simply judgment on Babylon?
questions.answer: Babylon is barely mentioned by name. The scope is cosmic — ‘the world’ is being judged (v11). In Biblical terms Babylon represents far more than a geographical place — from Babel to Revelation it represents humanity united in wickedness against the Lord. The cosmic disruption language points beyond any single historical catastrophe.

questions.header: 13:1–16: The Day of the Lord
questions.question: What reasons are given for the Lord’s judgment here?
questions.answer: Sin, evil, wickedness, arrogance, haughty pride, ruthlessness. The same diagnosis as for God’s own people in chapters 1–12. The Lord’s moral standard is consistent — he does not judge Israel for sins he tolerates in the nations.

questions.header: 13:17–22: The fall of Babylon
questions.question: How is the Lord going to use the Medes? What does this tell us about the Lord’s sovereignty?
questions.answer: The Lord will stir up the Medes against Babylon (v17) and they will overcome it — this happened historically in 539 BC (cf. Daniel 5). But the Medes are merely the Lord’s instrument. Babylon’s pride and luxury will be overturned like Sodom and Gomorrah (v19).

questions.header: 14:1–27: The Day of Relief
questions.question: How is the Lord’s judgment going to be a good thing for his people?
questions.answer: The Lord will show compassion on his people (v1). He will restore them and overturn their fortunes. They will no longer be captives but rulers. Their suffering at the hands of Babylon will end. The judgment of the oppressor is inseparable from the liberation of the oppressed.

questions.header: 14:1–27: The Day of Relief
questions.question: Glance through chapter 14 and pick out everything we see about how Babylon has behaved. What has characterised them?
questions.answer: Oppression, fury, wickedness, relentless aggression, pomp, arrogance, cruelty (vv4–6, 17–20). The king aspired to ascend above God (vv13–14) — the ultimate expression of the pride the Lord judges.

questions.header: 14:1–27: The Day of Relief
questions.question: What is the future of Babylon’s mighty kings and their empire?
questions.answer: Their power will end. They will be laid low. They will die and become food for worms (vv11, 19) — without honourable burial. Their city will be laid waste. The one who made the earth tremble ends up in the pit, no different from the lowliest of the dead.

questions.header: 14:1–27: The Day of Relief
questions.question: How do vv26–27 help us apply Isaiah’s prophecy against Babylon more broadly?
questions.answer: ‘This is the plan determined for the whole world; this is the hand stretched out over all nations.’ The prophecy against Babylon is a pattern for all arrogant human power. No ruler in history has escaped the grave. The evil of the world does not go unnoticed and a day of reckoning will come.

questions.header: 14:28–32: The fall of the Philistines
questions.question: What warning does Isaiah give the Philistines, and where does this leave God’s people? (v32)
questions.answer: Although the Philistines may think they have been freed from oppression, a worse threat is coming and they will still be destroyed. Verse 32 closes the whole section with hope: ‘The afflicted of his people will find refuge in him.’ Amid all the oracles of judgment, this is the destination for God’s people — refuge in the Lord.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: Where do we see ‘Babylonian’ behaviour today — powerful human society united against the Lord? What difference does it make to know that the Day of the Lord will come for the whole world? What does it mean to live in the light of that now?
questions.answer: Draw the group toward concrete contemporary examples of arrogant human power that exalts itself above God. The key pastoral point: the people of God are called to find their refuge not in allying with the powers of the day but in the Lord who judges all of them. Revelation 14:8 and 18:2 pick up ‘Babylon has fallen’ language and apply it to the final judgment, showing how the NT reads these oracles as speaking far beyond their immediate historical context.
questions.leaderNote: Revelation 14:8 and 18:2 echo the ‘Babylon has fallen’ language of Isaiah 21:9. It is worth pointing the group to this — it shows how the NT writers read the Babylon oracles as speaking far beyond their immediate historical context.

chapters.title: Isaiah 15–18 — True Security Found Only in the Lord
chapters.bigIdea: True security can only be found in the Lord and his Messianic King.

background.body:
Moab (chs 15–16) was descended from Lot (Genesis 19:37) and situated to the east of Judah. The prophecy against Moab is striking for its tone of genuine lament.

Damascus and Ephraim (ch 17) are paired because they had sought security in a political alliance against Assyria — the Syro-Ephraimite War of 735 BC studied in session 4. Both now face the consequences of having trusted each other rather than the Lord.

Cush (ch 18) was the kingdom in what is now southern Egypt and northern Sudan. The Cushite king Piankhi took over Egypt in 715 BC and sent envoys around the ancient Near East trying to build anti-Assyrian alliances. Isaiah’s response to the Cushite envoys is conspicuous silence from the Lord — the nations must come to him on his terms, not theirs.

The common thread. All these nations are seeking security in political alliances, military strength, or their own religious resources — anything except the Lord. The prophecies expose these securities as empty and point toward the only true security: the Lord himself and the coming Davidic king (16:5).

keyPoints.body:
The Lord laments over the nations. The tone of 15:5–8 and 16:9–12 is remarkable: the Lord himself mourns over the destruction coming on Moab. This is not a God who takes pleasure in judgment but one who grieves over the consequences of pride and the refusal to turn to him.

The offer to Moab: come under the Davidic king. The remarkable offer of 16:4–5 invites Moab to come under the protection of the coming ruler on David’s throne — one who seeks justice and speeds the cause of righteousness. The offer is real; Moab’s pride prevents them accepting it.

Pride is the consistent reason for the nations’ destruction. Moab’s ‘excessive pride’ (16:6) is explicitly named. Damascus and Ephraim forgot the Lord their Saviour (17:10). Pride and forgetfulness of God lead to the same place.

Judgment can bring spiritual renewal. The promise of 17:7–8 — ‘in that day people will look to their Maker’ — suggests the Lord can bring repentance and renewal even out of judgment on the nations, hinting at the universal scope of the gospel.

The Lord’s sovereignty over the nations is total. The Lord simply rebukes the terrifying nations (17:13) and by morning they are gone. Against the backdrop of Assyrian military dominance, this is a staggering claim.

exegetical.body:
The offer to Moab (16:4–5). One of the most unexpected moments in the oracles against the nations. The Lord invites Moab to come under the protection of the faithful Davidic ruler — the same messianic figure of chapters 9 and 11. The offer is universal: even Moab can find refuge under the Lord’s king.

Moab’s pride (16:6). ‘We have heard of Moab’s pride — how great is her arrogance!’ This is the explicit reason for Moab’s refusal and destruction. Pride is the consistent enemy throughout Isaiah.

‘In that day people will look to their Maker’ (17:7–8). The word for ‘people’ (ha’adam) is more like ‘humankind’ than ‘the people of Israel.’ The promise of renewal may go beyond the Israelite remnant — a hint of the universal scope of salvation.

The Lord’s response to the Cushite envoys (18:1–7). The Lord is conspicuously silent in response to the Cushite diplomatic mission (v4). The point: the real alliance must be made with the Lord, not with Cush. The nations must come to him on his terms (v7).

pastorals.body:
These are relatively unfamiliar passages and the group may feel lost in the geography. Spend a moment placing Moab, Damascus/Ephraim, and Cush on a sketch map. The theological point is the same in all three cases: where are these peoples looking for their security, and what does the Lord say about it?

The lament tone of the Moab oracle (15:5–8; 16:9–12) is worth drawing out. Some in the group may be surprised that the Lord grieves over the destruction of a foreign nation. This is pastoral gold: the God of Isaiah grieves over the consequences of human pride and offers even Moab a way out.

watch.body: Watch for the group treating these chapters as simply ancient political history. The pastoral heart is the question Isaiah keeps pressing: where are you looking for your security? Moab’s pride stopped her accepting the Lord’s offer. Damascus and Ephraim forgot the Lord their Saviour. Cush sent envoys everywhere except to the Lord. Each is a mirror held up to the reader.

questions.header: 15:1–16:14: Moab — don’t let pride keep you from the Lord
questions.question: What is the tone of the prophecy against Moab? What will happen to them?
questions.answer: A lament at the disaster coming on them. Moab is going to be overwhelmed — most likely by Assyria (16:8). The tone is one of genuine grief, not triumph.

questions.header: 15:1–16:14: Moab — don’t let pride keep you from the Lord
questions.question: In 15:9 there is a clue that the lamenter is the Lord himself. What do these verses tell us about the Lord’s attitude to the nations and his character?
questions.answer: The Lord has love and compassion for the nations — for the ordinary people caught up in the turmoil, for their insistence on seeking help in the wrong place. He laments over their destruction even as he brings it. This is not a God who enjoys judgment.

questions.header: 15:1–16:14: Moab — don’t let pride keep you from the Lord
questions.question: What help is offered to Moab? (16:4–5)
questions.answer: To come under the protective rule of God’s Davidic king — the one who seeks justice and righteousness. Turning to the God of Israel in faith and seeking a spiritual alliance with the people of God would have been the answer. The offer is real and generous.

questions.header: 15:1–16:14: Moab — don’t let pride keep you from the Lord
questions.question: How do 16:6–7 hint at Moab’s response to this offer? What is the reason for their refusal?
questions.answer: They are too proud and arrogant to turn to the God of Judah/Israel. Their arrogance ultimately leads to their destruction. Pride is the wall between Moab and salvation.

questions.header: 17:1–11: Damascus and Ephraim — look to the Lord alone
questions.question: Damascus and Israel (Ephraim) had sought security in an alliance. How did the alliance work out for them? (17:1‑3) What is their destruction like? (17:4‑6)
questions.answer: Their alliance was utterly pointless — both are overcome and their cities left ruined. Their destruction is almost total: like a wasting disease, like the harvest — reaping, cutting down, gleaning what is left.

questions.header: 17:1–11: Damascus and Ephraim — look to the Lord alone
questions.question: What was the root cause of their destruction? (17:9–11)
questions.answer: They had forgotten the Lord. Note how he is described: Saviour, Rock, Fortress — the source of protection they had abandoned. They turned to political alliances instead. Their idolatrous religion proved as futile as their military alliances.

questions.header: 17:1–11: Damascus and Ephraim — look to the Lord alone
questions.question: The Lord judges sin, but what else does he bring? (17:7‑8)
questions.answer: Spiritual renewal out of judgment. They will ‘look to the Lord their Maker and turn their eyes to the Holy One of Israel.’ The word for ‘people’ here may go beyond Israel to ‘humankind’ generally — the Lord can bring repentance and renewal from judgment even for those outside his people.

questions.header: 17:12–18:7: Cush — let the nations come to the Lord
questions.question: How does the Lord respond to the turmoil of the nations? (17:13) What is the real alliance that must be made? (18:3, 7)
questions.answer: He simply rebukes them and by morning the enemy is gone. The Lord is utterly sovereign. The real alliance must be spiritual, not political: all nations must come to the Lord (v7 — ‘gifts will be brought to the Lord Almighty’). The envoys should be coming to the Lord, not to each other.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: Where were people in Isaiah’s time looking for their security? What was the solution for them? Where do we look for security today? What threats do people fear? How does this passage point us beyond Isaiah’s day to Jesus?
questions.answer: In Isaiah’s day: political alliances, military strength, their own religious resources. The solution: look to the Lord, their Saviour, Rock, Fortress — and to the Davidic king who judges justly (16:5). Today: political leadership, economic stability, national defence, personal wealth. The Davidic king of 16:5 is ultimately Jesus — the one who truly seeks justice, and under whom even Moab can find refuge.
questions.leaderNote: The offer to Moab in 16:4–5 is worth dwelling on: even Moab — not part of God’s people — can come under the protection of the Davidic king. This is a powerful pointer toward the universality of the gospel.

chapters.title: Isaiah 19–20 — The Lord Comes to Egypt to Judge, Heal, and Restore
chapters.bigIdea: The Lord comes to Egypt to judge, heal, and restore.

background.body:
Egypt in the ancient world. Egypt was one of the two great superpowers of the ancient Near East. Its agricultural productivity (dependent on the Nile), its linen, and its political influence made it the constant temptation for small nations like Judah seeking protection against Assyria. The Lord consistently warns against this throughout Isaiah (cf. chs 30–31).

Historical context (from 715 BC). From 715 BC, Egypt tried to stir up anti-Assyrian sentiment. Ashdod joined them in 713 BC and was overwhelmed by Assyria in 711 BC. The Egyptians (under Cushite rule at this point) were attacked by Assyria in 701 BC. Isaiah’s visual prophecy in chapter 20 speaks directly into this context.

Isaiah’s visual prophecy (ch 20). The Lord asks Isaiah to walk around stripped and barefoot for three years — a deliberately dramatic and uncomfortable prophetic act. The point: Egypt will be led away stripped and barefoot as Assyrian captives. Don’t put your trust in them.

The stunning promise of 19:19–25. One of the most remarkable passages in all of Isaiah. The Lord promises that Egypt and Assyria — Israel’s two greatest historical enemies — will one day worship the Lord alongside Israel. Egypt will have an altar to the Lord in its midst. Assyria will be called ‘the work of my hands.’ And Israel, Egypt, and Assyria will together be ‘a blessing on the earth’ (v24). A staggering anticipation of the gospel’s universal reach.

keyPoints.body:
The Lord comes to Egypt — even Egypt is not outside his reach. The repeated phrase ‘in that day’ (vv16, 18, 19, 23, 24) structures the whole passage, moving from judgment to healing to worship to unity to blessing. The arc is remarkable.

The Lord’s judgment of Egypt is total and structural. Society breaks down (v2), the economy collapses (vv5–10), the political leadership becomes foolish (vv11–13). The Lord is responsible for all of it (v14). Egypt’s strength is entirely under the Lord’s control.

The Lord’s ultimate plan for Egypt is healing, not destruction. The promise of 19:22 is stunning: ‘The Lord will strike Egypt with a plague; he will strike them and heal them. They will turn to the Lord, and he will respond to their pleas and heal them.’ Judgment is in the service of healing.

Egypt, Assyria, and Israel as one people of God. The vision of 19:23–25 is one of the most breathtaking anticipations of the gospel in the whole OT. Egypt called ‘my people’ — the covenant language extended to a Gentile nation.

Egypt is too weak and disgraced to be trusted now. Chapter 20 is the practical counterpoint. Whatever Egypt’s future destiny, right now she is helpless before the Assyrians. Trust the Lord.

exegetical.body:
‘The Lord rides on a swift cloud and is coming to Egypt’ (19:1). The cloud imagery is theophanic — the Lord comes in power and glory (cf. Psalm 104:3; Daniel 7:13). Nothing in Egypt can withstand him.

The Nile (19:5–10). Egypt’s entire agricultural economy depended on the annual flooding of the Nile. The Lord’s judgment strikes at the root: he will dry up the Nile. No crops, no fishing, no flax for linen.

‘An altar to the Lord in the heart of Egypt’ (19:19). An altar in the heart of a foreign nation signals that the Lord’s presence and worship will spread to the very heart of the nations.

‘My people Egypt’ (19:25). This is the most striking phrase in the passage. The title ‘my people’ was Israel’s covenant designation. Here it is extended to Egypt. The gospel in seed form.

Isaiah’s three years (20:3). Three years of walking stripped and barefoot was a sustained, costly prophetic act — impossible to ignore or forget. The Lord sometimes calls his people to costly proclamation.

pastorals.body:
The group should not leave this session without spending significant time on 19:18–25. It is one of the most universally hopeful passages in the OT. After the relentless judgment of the surrounding chapters, the vision of Egypt and Assyria worshipping alongside Israel is breathtaking. Help the group feel its weight before pressing to application.

Chapter 20 may feel anticlimactic after the glories of chapter 19 — but it serves an important practical purpose. The glorious future of Egypt does not mean that Egypt is trustworthy now. The people of God are called to live in the light of God’s future while being clear-eyed about present realities.

watch.body: Watch for the group rushing past 19:18–25 to get to the visual prophecy of chapter 20. The five ‘in that day’ promises are the theological heart of this session — the most explicit anticipation of the gospel’s universal reach in the first half of Isaiah. Give it the time it deserves.

questions.header: 19:1–15: The Lord comes to Egypt
questions.question: What is the effect of the Lord coming to Egypt? (v1) How do the Egyptians respond?
questions.answer: Utter terror. The coming of the Lord brings fear to idols and Egyptians alike. Even the gods of Egypt tremble before him.

questions.header: 19:1–15: The Lord comes to Egypt
questions.question: What is the social impact of the Lord coming to Egypt? (vv2–4)
questions.answer: Breakdown in society — civil war, division even between neighbours and family members, despair, failure, and ultimately oppression by a hard master.

questions.header: 19:1–15: The Lord comes to Egypt
questions.question: What is the economic impact of the Lord coming to Egypt? (vv5–10)
questions.answer: Everything they rely on economically falls apart. The Nile will fail: no crops, no fishing, no flax for linen. All of Egypt’s famous productivity is stripped away.

questions.header: 19:1–15: The Lord comes to Egypt
questions.question: What is the political impact? (vv11–13) What is the role of the Lord in all this? (vv14–15)
questions.answer: The wise leaders become fools and lead the people astray. Their famous wisdom is rendered worthless. The Lord is the one sending this judgment — he has poured into them a spirit of dizziness (v14). Despite being one of the great superpowers, Egypt is entirely under the Lord’s control.

questions.header: 19:16–25: The Lord heals Egypt
questions.question: What ultimate future does the Lord have in store for Egypt? Look at the five ‘in that day’ statements (vv16, 18, 19, 23, 24) and work out what the Lord will do.
questions.answer:
v16: Bring about the fear of the Lord in Egypt.
v18: Unite the cities of Egypt under the Lord.
v19: Bring about worship of the Lord — altar, prayer, revelation, discipline.
v23: Unite Egypt and Assyria in worship of the Lord.
v24: Give Egypt and Assyria the status of Israel and make them a blessing to the whole earth. Egypt will be ‘my people’; Assyria ‘the work of my hands.’

questions.header: 19:16–25: The Lord heals Egypt
questions.question: How does this point us forward to the gospel?
questions.answer: The most unexpected peoples — Egypt (Israel’s ancient oppressor) and Assyria (the current threat) — will worship alongside Israel. ‘My people Egypt’ is the covenant language extended to a Gentile nation. A direct anticipation of the gospel’s universal reach and the inclusion of all nations in the people of God.
questions.leaderNote: The phrase ‘my people Egypt’ in 19:25 is worth dwelling on — it is the most dramatic extension of covenant language to a Gentile nation in the OT, and a clear pointer to what Paul says in Romans 9:24–26 about the nations being called ‘my people.’

questions.header: 20:1–6: Assyria disgraces Egypt
questions.question: What visual prophecy does the Lord ask Isaiah to perform, and what is its point?
questions.answer: Isaiah is to go around stripped and barefoot for three years — sustained, costly, impossible to ignore. The point: Egypt will be led away stripped and barefoot as Assyrian captives. Egypt is too weak and disgraced to be trusted. Don’t put your trust in political alliances with great nations. Trust the Lord.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: What can this passage teach us about the Lord’s plans for the nations? How do we get from Isaiah’s day to Jesus? Where are we tempted to look for security, and what does it mean to find our security in the Lord?
questions.answer: The Lord’s plans for the nations include healing, restoration, and worship — not just judgment. The vision of ‘my people Egypt’ (19:25) is fulfilled as people from every nation are brought into the people of God through Christ. The practical point of chapter 20 remains: whatever the future holds for the nations, right now the temptation is to seek security in powerful alliances rather than in the Lord. Egypt was too weak to save then; the things we trust in today are equally unable to deliver ultimate security.

chapters.title: Isaiah 21–23 — No Earthly Power Can Stand in the Day of the Lord
chapters.bigIdea: No earthly power can stand in the day of the Lord.

background.body:
Babylon (ch 21). This oracle probably dates from the time of Merodach-Baladan, who rebelled against Assyria and was defeated in 710 BC, then tried again in 705 BC. The fall described anticipates the eventual fall of Babylon to the Medes and Persians in 539 BC.

Edom/Dumah and Arabia (21:11–17). Edom (also called Dumah, a wordplay on ‘silence’ or ‘doom’) receives nothing but silence from the Lord. Arabia/Kedar will also be reduced by the Assyrians.

Jerusalem (ch 22). This is the most surprising oracle in the section — because it is an oracle against the people of God’s own city. Jerusalem is celebrating while Isaiah weeps — because their joy is misplaced. Their self-sufficiency (vv8–14) and misplaced joy in the face of coming judgment are condemned.

Shebna and Eliakim (22:15–25). These two officials serve as case studies in the dangers of misplaced trust. Shebna, who puts his confidence in his own position, will be removed and disgraced. Eliakim, who is commended, is still warned: even apparently secure things will break.

Tyre (ch 23). Tyre was the financial and commercial capital of the Mediterranean. Its destruction would have been as shocking to Isaiah’s hearers as the collapse of a major global financial centre would be today.

keyPoints.body:
The common thread: all human power and confidence comes under the Lord’s judgment. Babylon’s political power, Jerusalem’s self-sufficiency, Tyre’s economic might — all of it is shown to be temporary and fragile before the Lord.

Jerusalem is not exempt. God’s people cannot assume their status as the Lord’s city protects them from his judgment. Their self-confidence (vv8–14) and celebration in the face of coming judgment are as offensive to the Lord as the pride of the surrounding nations.

The Lord humbles pride — whether political, military, or economic. Babylon represents political and military power. Tyre represents economic power. Both come under the Lord’s judgment. He will not share his glory with human achievement in any sphere.

Even apparently secure positions cannot be trusted. The Shebna/Eliakim episode (22:15–25) illustrates this individually. Even the commended Eliakim is warned: the peg driven into a firm place will give way (v25). Human position and authority are always provisional.

Even Tyre’s wealth will ultimately be used for the Lord’s glory. The brief promise of 23:17–18: after 70 years Tyre will be restored, but her wealth will be ‘set apart for the Lord.’ Even commercial wealth is ultimately redirected toward God’s purposes.

exegetical.body:
The fall of Babylon (21:9). ‘Babylon has fallen, has fallen!’ The repetition is emphatic. This phrase is picked up in Revelation 14:8 and 18:2, applied to the final fall of the power Babylon represents.

Casual overconfidence at Babylon’s feast (21:5). The image of Babylon’s leaders eating and drinking while the enemy approaches is historically plausible (cf. Daniel 5, Belshazzar’s feast) and symbolically powerful.

Jerusalem’s misplaced joy (22:1–2, 12–14). The Lord called them to weeping and mourning (v12) — appropriate responses to impending judgment. Instead they eat and drink, saying ‘Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die!’ (v13) — an attitude Paul confronts in 1 Corinthians 15:32.

The peg in a firm place (22:23–25). Even this peg will give way (v25). The warning is not against Eliakim personally but against over-reliance on any human position. What seems secure will break under excessive weight.

Tyre’s 70 years (23:15–18). The number may be symbolic (a full generation). After this period Tyre will be restored, but her wealth will be ‘set apart for the Lord.’ Even commercial power is ultimately pressed into God’s service.

pastorals.body:
The oracle against Jerusalem (ch 22) is pastorally the most important section of this session, and also the most easily skipped. Make sure the group spends time with it. The question of what Jerusalem’s misplaced confidence looked like — and what ours looks like — is where the pastoral weight lies.

The Shebna/Eliakim section can feel obscure but is very accessible as a study in individual trust. Shebna’s self-aggrandisement and Eliakim’s over-burdened position are both recognisable human situations. The warning — even what seems most secure will give way — is timeless.

watch.body: Watch for the group spending all the session on Babylon and Tyre and missing the oracle against Jerusalem in chapter 22. The sting of this session is the application to God’s own people — their misplaced confidence, their celebration in the face of judgment. That’s the question to press: where do we celebrate when we should be mourning? What are we trusting that will give way?

questions.header: 21:1–10: Babylon
questions.question: What is the tone of the prophecy against Babylon? What attitude do the Babylonians display? What is their destruction like?
questions.answer: Horror, shock, fear, ominous dread (vv1–2). The Babylonians display casual overconfidence — eating and drinking while the enemy approaches (v5). Their destruction is overwhelming, painful, and swift. ‘Babylon has fallen, has fallen!’ (v9).

questions.header: 21:1–10: Babylon
questions.question: ‘Babylon has fallen’ is picked up in Revelation 14:8 and 18:2. In Scripture, Babylon represents humans united against the Lord. With that big picture in mind, what can we learn?
questions.answer: Only God’s kingdom is secure and worth aligning ourselves with. Overconfidence and political power will not help us. Whatever human power stands in the place of Babylon in any age — claiming ultimate authority, promoting its own glory — is under the same sentence.

questions.header: 21:11–16: Edom and Arabia
questions.question: What hope is offered to Edom, and what will become of Arabia/Kedar?
questions.answer: None for Edom — Dumah is a wordplay on Edom and ‘silence.’ The Lord has nothing to say; relief will be only temporary. Arabia/Kedar will also be reduced by the Assyrians within a year.

questions.header: 22:1–25: Jerusalem
questions.question: What is happening in Jerusalem? (vv1–2) Why is Isaiah weeping? (v4) What does the Lord have in store for them? (vv5‑7)
questions.answer: They are all celebrating on the rooftops. Isaiah weeps because their joy is misplaced — the day of the Lord is coming where their joy will turn to tears. The Lord has in store a day of tumult, trampling, and terror: the city will be overcome by enemies and its leaders taken captive or will flee.

questions.header: 22:1–25: Jerusalem
