questions.question: What do vv8–14 tell us about the reasons for the Lord’s judgment on Jerusalem?
questions.answer: They have had confidence in their own resources — their weapons, defences, water supply — but not in the Lord who supplied them. The Lord called them to repentance and they did not listen but celebrated instead. ‘Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die!’ (v13) — unforgivable precisely because it came in response to a call to repentance.

questions.header: 22:1–25: Jerusalem
questions.question: What is the warning to Shebna and Eliakim and what does it signify for Israel?
questions.answer: Shebna — warning about exile and disgrace. He has used his position to glorify himself (his own tomb, v16) rather than the Lord. Eliakim — commended but still warned: even the peg driven into a firm place will give way (v25). The most apparently secure human positions are provisional. No human authority can bear the weight that only the Lord can bear.
questions.leaderNote: The Shebna/Eliakim contrast is worth exploring: one trusts in his own position and is removed; the other is commended but even he cannot bear unlimited weight. The application: don’t over-invest in any human figure or institution, however godly. Only the Lord can bear that weight.

questions.question: What has happened to Tyre and why is it so shocking? Why has Tyre been destroyed? What is the end of the story?
questions.answer: It has been destroyed — the economic capital of the Mediterranean, whose collapse would have seemed as unthinkable as the destruction of a major global financial centre today. The reason: the Lord has humbled their pride (v9) — ‘The Lord Almighty planned it, to bring down her pride in all her splendour.’ The end: after 70 years Tyre will be restored, but her wealth will be ‘set apart for the Lord’ (vv17–18). Even commercial wealth is ultimately redirected toward God’s purposes.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: Self-confidence, pride, political power, military power, self-sufficiency, good planning, earthly security, wealth — these all come under fire in today’s chapters. How do we fall prey to this kind of thinking in the church? Where do these chapters point us? What is to be the response of the people of God?
questions.answer: The church is not exempt from the patterns Isaiah diagnoses in Jerusalem (ch 22): celebrating when we should be mourning; trusting in our own resources; misplacing confidence in human leadership. The chapters point toward a posture of humility before the Lord, recognition that all human power is provisional, and trust in the Lord alone. The oracle against Jerusalem in chapter 22 is the most directly applicable section for a church group — the charge (confidence in their own resources, celebrating when they should be mourning) is worth pressing carefully.
questions.leaderNote: The oracle against Jerusalem in chapter 22 is the most directly applicable section for a church group. The specific charge — confidence in their own resources, celebrating when they should be mourning — is worth pressing into carefully.

chapters.title: Isaiah 24–27 — The Lord Destroys the Lofty City of Man
chapters.bigIdea: The Lord will destroy the lofty city of man but establish the eternal city of salvation for his atoned and gathered people.

background.body:
Chapters 24–27 are often called the ‘Isaiah Apocalypse’ because they shift from specific temporal judgments on named nations (chs 13–23) to a more cosmic and universal vision of judgment and salvation. This is deliberate: all temporal judgment points to eternal judgment.

The City of Man vs the City of God. There is strong ‘city’ imagery throughout these chapters — the ruined City of Man (representing humanity in rebellion against the Lord) contrasted with the City of God (representing the people of God in his presence). This contrast will recur in Revelation.

The feast on the mountain (25:6–8). One of the great eschatological passages of the OT. The Lord prepares a feast for all peoples — the best food and wine (v6). He will destroy death for ever (v8). He will wipe away every tear. Directly echoed in Revelation 7:17 and 21:4, and Paul quotes v8 in 1 Corinthians 15:54.

The resurrection (26:19). ‘Your dead will live, Lord; their bodies will rise — let those who dwell in the dust wake up and shout for joy.’ One of the clearest anticipations of bodily resurrection in the OT (alongside Daniel 12:2).

Leviathan (27:1). Leviathan is the chaos-monster representing the forces of evil and chaos opposed to God. In Isaiah 27:1, the Lord destroys Leviathan on the Day of the Lord. Picked up in Revelation 12 where the ‘dragon’ (Satan) is defeated by Christ.

keyPoints.body:
The judgment of God is universal and total. Chapter 24 describes the devastation of the whole earth — not a specific nation but the entire created order. The earth is ruined because of human disobedience and covenant-breaking (v5–6).

Judgment is ultimately a reason for praise. Even as the earth is judged, the people of God from the four corners of the earth sing praises. Judgment is God’s undoing of the evil of the world and the ushering in of his eternal righteous reign.

The Lord’s salvation is for all peoples. The feast of 25:6–8 is for ‘all peoples’ and ‘all nations.’ The destruction of death is universal. The wiping away of tears is universal.

The City of God is where the Lord’s people find their ultimate home. Chapter 26 is structured around the contrast between the lofty city of man (laid low, v5–6) and the strong city of God (v1–2). The Lord provides a city for his people where they are truly secure.

The Lord defeats evil at its root and gathers his people. Chapter 27 describes the destruction of Leviathan, the fruitfulness of the Lord’s renewed vineyard, the atonement of his people’s sins, and the gathering of the exiles — an echo of the great trumpet in Matthew 24:31.

exegetical.body:
The earth’s ruin (24:4–6). The connection between human sin and the suffering of the created order is a recurring biblical theme (cf. Genesis 3:17–18; Romans 8:20–22). The whole created order languishes under the weight of human rebellion.

‘He will swallow up death for ever’ (25:8). Paul quotes this in 1 Corinthians 15:54 as the ultimate result of Christ’s resurrection. Revelation 21:4 echoes it in the description of the new creation. The destruction of death is a literal promise about what the Lord will do.

The resurrection (26:19). This sits at the climax of a lament in which the people have confessed their inability to bring salvation (v18). The Lord’s answer: even the dead will rise. This goes beyond the mere survival of the soul to the resurrection of the body.

Leviathan (27:1). In Canaanite mythology, Leviathan was the chaos-monster of the sea, defeated by the god Baal. Isaiah deliberately uses this imagery but redirects it: it is the Lord who defeats Leviathan. Revelation 12–13 and 20:2–10 pick up this imagery in describing the defeat of Satan.

The gathering trumpet (27:12–13). The great trumpet that will be blown to gather the scattered people of the Lord echoes through the NT: Matthew 24:31; 1 Corinthians 15:52; 1 Thessalonians 4:16.

pastorals.body:
Chapters 24–27 can feel overwhelming. The key is to help the group trace the arc: universal judgment (ch 24) → the Lord’s feast and the defeat of death (ch 25) → the City of God and the discipline that leads to it (ch 26) → the final defeat of evil and gathering of God’s people (ch 27). Once the group can see the shape, the individual passages make more sense.

The feast of 25:6–8 and the resurrection promise of 26:19 deserve extended attention. If the group has been through the sustained darkness of chapters 1–23, the light of these passages should feel all the more brilliant. Give it time. Allow discussions about end times to be kept short — the main theological claims are clear regardless of eschatological timeline.

watch.body: Watch for the group getting stuck in chapter 24’s darkness and not making it to the feast of chapter 25. The session builds toward the feast, the resurrection, and the gathering trumpet — make sure you budget time for those. Chapters 24–27 are not primarily about judgment but about the Lord’s ultimate triumph over evil and death.

questions.header: 24:1–23: The Lord judges the earth and is glorified
questions.question: What is the Lord going to do? What will it be like? (Look at the imagery and language.)
questions.answer: Total devastation and scattering — it won’t matter how important or humble someone is. The whole earth will be like cities attacked and laid waste. God’s judgment will be like the undoing of creation. The imagery of the drunkard/hut (v20) is powerful: the earth sways and collapses. This is meant to be terrifying.

questions.header: 24:1–23: The Lord judges the earth and is glorified
questions.question: What imagery connects these two sections? Why will the Lord judge the earth? What awaits the people of the earth?
questions.answer: Withering/wasting away — the withered earth and a person who wastes away. The people have violated God’s laws and broken his everlasting covenant. The whole created order withers and languishes because of human sin. They will be destroyed, with few left, meeting terrifying judgment with no escape.

questions.header: 24:1–23: The Lord judges the earth and is glorified
questions.question: What kind of music has been heard in the City of Man? What will happen to their songs? What music will there be on that day? (vv14–16a)
questions.answer:
Drunken revelry — wine, music, celebration. But all their music will fall silent when the Lord judges the earth. Instead of filling the streets with music they will lock themselves away in fear.

The praises of God from the ends of the earth — proclaiming the Lord’s majesty, giving glory to the God of Israel, singing ‘Glory to the Righteous One.’ The world’s party ends and the Lord’s praise begins.

questions.header: 24:1–23: The Lord judges the earth and is glorified
questions.question: Why is judgment ultimately a reason for rejoicing? (vv21–23)
questions.answer: Judgment is God’s undoing of the evil of the world and the ushering in of his eternal righteous reign. The world has been ruled by wicked powers (spiritual and temporal, v21) but the Lord will reign on Mount Zion with great glory. All evil will be dealt with for ever. For those who belong to him, this is cause for joy.

questions.header: 25:1–12: The Lord gathers his people to his table
questions.question: Read 25:1‑8. Why is Isaiah praising the Lord? What are the results of God’s judgment/salvation? List as many as you can.
questions.answer: Isaiah praises the Lord for his faithfulness and wonderful things. Results: people and nations honour the Lord (v3); the Lord protects the poor and needy (v4); the Lord silences the enemies of his people (v5); the Lord prepares a feast for all peoples (v6); the Lord will destroy death for ever (v8); the Lord will wipe away all tears (v8); the Lord will remove his people’s disgrace (v8). These verses are echoed in 1 Corinthians 15:54, Revelation 7:17, and Revelation 21:4.
questions.leaderNote: The NT connections here are rich and worth pointing out: 1 Corinthians 15:54 directly quotes v8 (‘death has been swallowed up in victory’); Revelation 7:17 and 21:4 echo the wiping away of tears. These are fundamental promises that the NT builds on.

questions.header: 26:1–21: Salvation is found in the City of God
questions.question: Read 26:1–21. List the deeds of the Lord that his people will sing about. What responses to the Lord are encouraged?
questions.answer:
The Lord provides a city of salvation (v1); perfect peace to those who trust in him (v3); humbles the lofty (v5–6); makes the way of the righteous smooth (v7); establishes peace (v12); brings ruin to the oppressors (v13–14); raises his people from death (v19); saves his people while judging the earth (v20–21).

Responses encouraged: trust in the Lord for ever (v4); walk in his ways (v8); wait for the Lord (v8); desire his renown (v8); yearn for him (v9).

questions.header: 26:1–21: Salvation is found in the City of God
questions.question: What failure in God’s people meant he had to discipline them? (vv16–18) What hope are we left with? (v19)
questions.answer:
They failed in their mission to bring salvation to the earth. What they did was useless — they could not save themselves let alone the world.

Despite this, the Lord will raise his people from death: ‘Your dead will live, Lord; their bodies will rise.’ The Lord’s rescue goes beyond the restoration of the nation to the resurrection of the body. Death and judgment do not have the last word; the Lord’s life-giving power does.

questions.header: 27:1–13: The Lord defeats evil and gathers his people
questions.question: Read 27:1–13. What will the deliverance of Israel look like?
questions.answer: The destruction of Leviathan — the evil chaos-monster representing the powers opposed to God (v1); making his people fruitful — contrast with ch 5 (vv2–6); atoning for his people’s sins and removing their idols (vv7–9); destroying the City of Man (vv10–11); gathering his exiled people from the ends of the earth with the great trumpet (vv12–13 — echoed in Matthew 24:31 and 1 Corinthians 15:52).

questions.header: Application
questions.question: Why do you think the people of Isaiah’s day needed to be pointed beyond temporal judgment and restoration to something more cosmic and eternal? What difference would it have made to them? How do we apply the sobering message of coming judgment? What does it mean for the people of God to look forward to ‘that day’?
questions.answer: Isaiah’s people were facing temporal judgment and needed to know it was not the end — beyond the exile lay the feast, the resurrection, the gathering. The cosmic scope stops them treating the return from exile as the fulfilment of all God’s promises. For us: the coming judgment is sobering and shapes how we live now (humility, repentance, urgency). But it is also the basis of our hope: the feast is coming, death will be destroyed, tears will be wiped away, the scattered will be gathered. Living in the light of that changes everything.
questions.leaderNote: If the group has time, Revelation 21:1•4 alongside Isaiah 25:6–8 is a deeply rewarding comparison. The NT does not replace Isaiah’s vision but fulfils and expands it.

chapters.title: Isaiah 28–29 — The Lord is Over All: Delight in Him, Listen to Him, Trust in Him
chapters.bigIdea: The Lord alone is over all. Delight in him, listen to him, trust in him.

background.body:
Chapters 28–33 form a section of five ‘woes.’ This session covers the first two woes (28–29).

The Northern Kingdom’s pride (28:1–13). The opening woe is addressed to ‘Ephraim’ — the northern kingdom. Their pride in their own beauty and achievement is the ‘fading flower’ of vv1–4. The Lord offers an alternative: he himself will be their ‘glorious crown’ (v5).

The attitude to God’s word (28:7–13). The prophets and priests who should be teaching the word of the Lord are drunk (v7–8). They treat Isaiah’s message with contempt — mocking it as if he is babbling like a child. The Lord will respond by sending his word through the Assyrian invaders in a language they literally cannot understand.

The ‘cornerstone’ of 28:16. The Lord’s response to their false security is to announce that he himself is laying a cornerstone in Zion — a sure foundation. One of the great Messianic texts of Isaiah, quoted extensively in the NT (Romans 9:33; 10:11; 1 Peter 2:6).

Jerusalem’s superficial religion (29:1–14). The second woe targets Jerusalem specifically. The people are very religious — they honour the Lord with their lips — but their worship is merely human tradition (v13). Jesus quotes this verse directly in Mark 7:6–7/Matthew 15:8–9.

keyPoints.body:
Pride in human achievement is a ‘fading flower.’ The antidote (vv5–6) is not less achievement but different pride: the Lord himself will be their glorious crown.

Dismissing God’s word has consequences. The word of God that is rejected does not simply go away; it returns in a form that cannot be ignored, but by then it is too late to hear it graciously.

The Lord himself is the true foundation. The ‘cornerstone’ of 28:16 stands in deliberate contrast to the ‘lie’ that the people have made their refuge (v15). The NT applies this to Jesus explicitly.

Religion that stays on the lips never reaches the heart. The diagnosis of 29:13 — ‘these people come near to me with their mouth and honour me with their lips, but their hearts are far from me’ — is one of the most searching indictments of formal religion in the whole Bible. Jesus uses it against the Pharisees. It is equally applicable today.

The Lord will astound and restore. Despite all the darkness, 29:17–24 offers radical transformation: the deaf will hear, the blind will see, the humble will rejoice, the arrogant will be no more.

exegetical.body:
The agricultural metaphors (28:23–29). A farmer knows when to plough, when to sow, when to harvest, and what implement to use for each crop. The Lord similarly knows the right approach for each situation. His judgments are not arbitrary but wise and precisely calibrated. ‘All this also comes from the Lord Almighty, whose plan is wonderful, whose wisdom is magnificent’ (v29).

‘A tested stone, a precious cornerstone’ (28:16). Paul quotes this twice (Romans 9:33; 10:11) as referring to Jesus. Peter quotes it in 1 Peter 2:6. The stone that is the foundation for believers is the stone of stumbling for unbelievers (cf. 8:14).

Ariel (29:1–2). A name for Jerusalem meaning either ‘altar hearth’ or ‘lion of God.’ Both meanings are ironic: the city that should be the place of true sacrifice to the Lord will become a place of destruction.

‘These people come near to me with their mouth’ (29:13). Jesus quotes this verbatim (from the LXX) in Mark 7:6–7 and Matthew 15:8–9, identifying the Pharisees’ tradition-keeping as the exact same sin. The diagnosis has not changed.

‘Turning things upside down’ (29:15–16). The clay claiming the position of the Creator. Paul uses the same potter/clay image in Romans 9:19–21 to address human questioning of the Lord’s sovereignty.

pastorals.body:
The diagnosis of 29:13 — lips near, hearts far — is one of the most personally confronting verses in this section. It deserves extended time. The question ‘Is this us?’ is the pastoral heart of the session.

The cornerstone promise of 28:16 is one of the great gospel texts of Isaiah and should not be rushed past. The contrast — you have made a lie your refuge, but the Lord is laying a true foundation — is pastorally rich.

The agricultural metaphors of 28:23–29 serve an important function: they give the group a framework for understanding why the Lord’s judgments take the forms they do. The wisdom of God is not arbitrary; it is precisely calibrated.

watch.body: Watch for the group treating the ‘lips/heart’ diagnosis of 29:13 as applying primarily to other people (Pharisees, nominal churchgoers) rather than to themselves. Jesus quoted it against the most religiously serious people of his day. Help the group sit with that honestly.

questions.header: 28:1–29: Where is your focus?
questions.question: What repetition do you see in vv1‑4? What does it tell us about the Northern Kingdom? Why is their pride so misplaced? What is the antidote? (vv5‑6)
questions.answer:
Wreath, pride, fading flower, glorious beauty — repeated twice (vv1, 4). Their pride is in something beautiful but transient. The Assyrians will swallow it up as easily as eating a ripe fig (v4).

Their pride is misplaced because it is fading and the Lord is coming to judge. The antidote: the Lord himself is their glorious crown and beautiful wreath. His character — glorious, beautiful, a spirit of justice, strength — is the true source of beauty and pride.

questions.header: 28:1–29: Where is your focus?
questions.question: What is their attitude to God’s word? (vv7‑10) How will the Lord respond? (vv11–13)
questions.answer:
Completely dismissive. Those who should be teaching are drunk and incapable. They mock Isaiah’s message as babbling — mocking its repetitive style (v10 is probably a contemptuous imitation).

As they will not listen to the Lord’s clear word, he will speak through a people whose language they do not understand — the Assyrian invaders. The word they rejected in grace they will hear in judgment.

questions.header: 28:1–29: Where is your focus?
questions.question: How are they boasting? (vv14‑15) How will the Lord respond? (vv16‑22)
questions.answer:
Over-confident. They have made a ‘covenant with death’ and an ‘agreement with the realm of the dead’ — probably a treaty with Egypt or Assyria they imagine will protect them. Their refuge is a lie (v15).

The Lord will sweep away their false refuge. He announces the laying of a cornerstone in Zion — tested, precious, a sure foundation (v16). Those who rely on it will never be put to shame. But the false refuge will be swept away.

questions.header: 28:1–29: Where is your focus?
questions.question: What do these agricultural metaphors teach about the Lord and how he deals with his people? (vv23‑29)
questions.answer: Just as the Lord teaches the farmer the right time and method for each agricultural task, he knows the best and most appropriate ways to deal with his people. His judgments are not arbitrary or excessive but precisely calibrated to the situation. ‘All this also comes from the Lord Almighty, whose plan is wonderful, whose wisdom is magnificent’ (v29).

questions.header: 29:1–24: The Lord is above all
questions.question: How will the Lord deal with Jerusalem? (vv1‑8) How will the Lord deal with their understanding? (vv9‑12) Why? (vv13‑14)
questions.answer:
Warfare metaphors — he will besiege and bring distress. But he will also have victory over their enemies (v8).

He will bring a stupor over them — like drunk people, like those asleep. His word will be like a sealed scroll neither the educated nor uneducated can read (vv11–12).

The reason (v13): their religion is merely superficial. They say all the right words but their hearts are far from the Lord. Their worship is merely human tradition. The Lord will therefore astound them by destroying the wisdom of their wise men.

questions.header: 29:1–24: The Lord is above all
questions.question: What attitude is at the heart of this? (vv15‑16) What will the day of the Lord bring? (vv17‑24)
questions.answer:
They turn things upside down — the clay questioning the potter, the creature claiming the position of the Creator. This is the fundamental inversion.

What the day of the Lord will bring (vv17–24): true hearing, light and sight; rejoicing in the Lord; eradication of evil; justice; redemption; restoration; understanding. The deaf will hear, the blind will see, the humble will rejoice, the arrogant will be no more.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: How do we see the characteristics of the gospel in today’s passage? What difference does it make to us? In particular, how does the cornerstone of 28:16 and the diagnosis of 29:13 speak to us today?
questions.answer: The gospel appears in the cornerstone promise (28:16) — the Lord provides a true foundation when all human refuges fail. The NT identifies this foundation as Jesus (Romans 9:33; 10:11; 1 Peter 2:6). The diagnosis of 29:13 is equally gospel-shaped: Jesus quotes it to expose the gap between formal religion and genuine heart-engagement. The good news is that the Lord himself provides what we cannot — a foundation that holds, and a transformation of understanding (vv17–24) that makes us truly see and hear.
questions.leaderNote: Mark 7:6–7 / Matthew 15:8–9 (Jesus quoting 29:13 against the Pharisees) is worth reading alongside the Isaiah passage. The fact that Jesus applies an Isaiah text about 8th-century Judah to 1st-century Pharisees shows that the diagnosis is not historically limited.

chapters.title: Isaiah 30–32 — Trust in the Lord Alone, Whose Righteous King Will Renew His People
chapters.bigIdea: Trust in the Lord alone, whose righteous King will renew his people.

background.body:
These chapters probably reflect the reign of Hezekiah and the specific political situation of 705–701 BC, when Sennacherib came to the Assyrian throne and the nations of the ancient Near East faced renewed Assyrian aggression. Judah’s temptation — to which they apparently succumbed — was to seek Egyptian support rather than trust the Lord.

The Egyptian alliance (30:1–7; 31:1–5). The ‘obstinate children’ of 30:1 have gone down to Egypt without consulting the Lord. Egypt’s help is described as utterly useless — ‘Rahab the Do-Nothing’ (30:7, a sarcastic nickname for Egypt). This is a recurring theme in Isaiah: seeking military and political solutions rather than trusting the Lord is consistently diagnosed as faithlessness.

The response to the Lord’s word (30:8–17). The people want only to hear pleasant things. Their salvation would have been found in ‘repentance and rest, in quietness and trust’ (30:15) — but they refused.

The Messianic King (32:1–8). Chapter 32 opens with a vision of a king who reigns in righteousness. This is the coming Davidic king of chapters 9 and 11, now seen in his function of creating a righteous society.

The outpoured Spirit (32:15–20). The renewal the Lord brings is through the outpouring of his Spirit — a theme that recurs in chapters 40–66 and is fulfilled at Pentecost (cf. Acts 2). The result is justice, righteousness, peace, quietness, and confidence for ever.

keyPoints.body:
Seeking help from Egypt is a form of faithlessness. The Lord’s indictment is not primarily strategic but theological: they have gone ‘without consulting me’ (30:2). Every time we seek security by our own means without reference to the Lord, we are doing the same thing.

The word they are refusing is the word that would save them. ‘In repentance and rest is your salvation, in quietness and trust is your strength, but you would have none of it’ (30:15). Help the group feel the cost of that refusal.

The Lord’s grace is patient and will come. Despite all their refusal, 30:18–26 offers astonishing grace: the Lord ‘longs to be gracious’ (v18). The offer has not expired.

Assyria is not the real threat — the Lord is. The Lord’s own coming judgment in 30:27–33 is terrifying. It is far more important to be on the right side of the Lord than to be protected by the strongest military alliance.

The Messianic King and the outpoured Spirit bring lasting renewal. Chapter 32 holds together the coming King (vv1–8) and the outpoured Spirit (vv15–20) as the two elements of the Lord’s renewal of his people. Both point forward to Jesus and Pentecost.

exegetical.body:
‘Rahab the Do-Nothing’ (30:7). Rahab is a chaos-monster name (cf. Psalm 89:10; Job 26:12), used here as a sarcastic nickname for Egypt. For all her reputation, Egypt will do nothing for Judah.

‘In repentance and rest is your salvation’ (30:15). One of the great summary statements of Isaiah’s theology. Salvation comes through repentance and rest — trusting the Lord to act. The tragedy is compressed in the final clause: ‘but you would have none of it.’

‘Your Teacher will be hidden no more’ (30:20). A promise of the Lord’s personal guidance after the period of judgment. The promise that ‘your ears will hear a voice behind you, saying, “This is the way; walk in it”’ (v21) is one of the most comforting in Isaiah.

The complacent women (32:9–14). As in chapter 3, the complacent women represent a broader social attitude — contentment with worldly security and prosperity, unwillingness to reckon with the coming judgment.

The outpoured Spirit (32:15). ‘Till the Spirit is poured on us from on high.’ The ‘till’ is important: the current desolation is not permanent. Joel 2:28–29 picks up this language, and Peter quotes Joel on the Day of Pentecost (Acts 2:17–21).

pastorals.body:
The diagnosis of 30:8–11 — only wanting to hear pleasant things, telling prophets to say what is pleasant — is one of the most culturally relevant passages in this section. It speaks directly to the temptation to shape preaching and teaching around what people want to hear rather than what they need to hear. Handle it carefully and honestly.

The application questions for this session are particularly rich and pastorally important. The question of where we are relying on ‘Egypt’ — our own solutions and resources rather than the Lord — is deeply personal and deserves more than a superficial answer.

watch.body: Watch for the group treating the Egyptian alliance as a purely historical example and not pressing the theological principle: trusting in our own resources and alliances rather than the Lord is a form of faithlessness that operates in every generation. The specific form changes; the underlying pattern does not. Help the group identify their own ‘Egypt.’

questions.header: 30:1–33: Whom will we trust in and whom will we fear?
questions.question: Where is Judah turning to for help? What is the problem with that? What would have been the solution?
questions.answer: Alliance with Egypt. The problem: not the Lord’s plan (v1–2); it will bring disgrace and shame but no advantage (vv3–5); Egypt’s help is useless — ‘Rahab the Do-Nothing’ (v6–7). The solution: turn to the Lord. Every element of the Egyptian alliance is both theologically wrong (not consulting the Lord) and practically futile.

questions.header: 30:1–33: Whom will we trust in and whom will we fear?
questions.question: How are they responding to the Lord’s word? (vv8‑11)
questions.answer: Completely dismissive. They want only to hear pleasant things — prophesying illusions, leaving the right path, stopping the Holy One from confronting them. They want a manageable God who affirms their existing decisions.

questions.header: 30:1–33: Whom will we trust in and whom will we fear?
questions.question: What will be the result of their sin? What would have been the solution? (vv12‑17)
questions.answer: Their sin will destroy them — one threat will be enough to put a thousand to flight. The solution is stated starkly in v15: ‘In repentance and rest is your salvation, in quietness and trust is your strength — but you would have none of it.’ Salvation was available; they refused it.

questions.header: 30:1–33: Whom will we trust in and whom will we fear?
questions.question: What hope does the Lord offer? (vv18‑26)
questions.answer: Grace, restoration, compassion, personal guidance, prosperity, freedom from idolatry, healing. The Lord ‘longs to be gracious’ (v18). He will hear the moment they cry out (v19). He will guide them personally — ‘your ears will hear a voice behind you’ (v21). Despite their refusal, the Lord’s offer of grace has not expired.

questions.header: 30:1–33: Whom will we trust in and whom will we fear?
questions.question: Why is Assyria not the real threat? (vv27–33)
questions.answer: The Lord himself, when he comes in judgment, is utterly terrifying — fire, storm, overwhelming stream. Assyria is nothing in comparison. The Lord will judge Assyria as easily as he judges anyone who exalts themselves. It is far more important to be rightly related to the Lord than to be protected by the strongest earthly alliance.

questions.header: 31:1‑9: Only the Lord is truly powerful
questions.question: Why is it such folly to rely on Egypt? (vv1‑5) What should God’s people do instead, and why? (vv6‑9)
questions.answer: The Lord is wise and able to save his people. The Egyptians are mere mortals — their horses are flesh, not spirit (v3). The Lord is like a lion or an eagle in saving his people. God’s people should return to the Lord (v6), who is the one who will destroy the Assyrians — not Egypt, not their own military resources (v8‑9).

questions.header: 32:1–20: Listen to the Lord who will renew his people
questions.question: What are the complacent women trusting in and why is it folly? (vv9‑14) How will the Lord renew his people and what will that time be like? (vv1‑8; 15–20)
questions.answer:
They trust in worldly security: wealth, finery, a good harvest. And it will all be stripped away — the harvest will fail, the city will be abandoned. Their complacency is exposed as fragile.

Through his righteous Messianic King (vv1–8) and the outpoured Spirit (vv15–20). The time will be characterised by: true spiritual understanding (vv3–4); genuine security (v2); righteousness and justice (v16–17); lasting peace, quietness, and confidence (v17–18); abundance and fruitfulness (v20). The renewal is comprehensive.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: Where are we tempted to rely on other things than the Lord? How do we justify that to ourselves? What does it look like for us to rely on the Lord? How do we see Jesus, God’s Messianic King, fulfilling what we read of in chapter 32? What are we still waiting for?
questions.answer: Open application question. Draw the group toward honest identification of their own ‘Egypt’ — the resources, relationships, or plans they trust when they should be trusting the Lord. The theological principle of 30:15 is universal: salvation comes through repentance and rest, not through our own strategies. Jesus fulfils the Messianic King of 32:1–8 — he reigns in righteousness and provides shelter for the weary. The Spirit has been poured out (Acts 2) but the full renewal of 32:15–20 awaits the new creation. We live in the tension.
questions.leaderNote: It is worth spending time on the specific sins of the complacent women in 32:9–14 and asking the group what the modern equivalent looks like. The ‘complacent women’ represent a broader social attitude of contentment with worldly security. Where do we share that attitude?

chapters.title: Isaiah 33–35 — The Lord Will Come Bringing Judgment and Joy
chapters.bigIdea: The Lord will come bringing judgment and joy when he saves his people; long for him and be strong.

background.body:
Read chapter 33 first in the session, discuss it, then read chapters 34–35 together. The contrast between the two is central: judgment (ch 34) and joy (ch 35) held side by side.

The background to chapter 33 (2 Kings 18:13–16). Hezekiah attempted to buy off the Assyrians with temple treasure — stripping the gold from the temple doors and giving Sennacherib everything he demanded. But the Assyrians took the money and betrayed him anyway. This is the context of the despair and appeal to the Lord in chapter 33.

Chapter 33 as a liturgical drama. Chapter 33 moves from lament (vv1–6), through a vision of the Lord’s sovereignty (vv7–12), to a description of those who can dwell with the holy God (vv14–16), to a vision of the glorious King and the restored city (vv17–24). It is one of the most carefully structured chapters in Isaiah.

Chapters 34–35 as bookends. These chapters form a dramatic contrast: chapter 34 describes the Lord’s judgment on all the nations (with Edom as the representative example) and chapter 35 describes the joy of the redeemed as they walk the ‘Way of Holiness’ back to Zion. Together they form a hinge between the first main section of Isaiah (chs 1–35) and the historical bridge of chapters 36–39.

‘The Way of Holiness’ (35:8). The road through the wilderness on which the redeemed of the Lord will walk home is picked up explicitly in 40:3 (‘prepare the way for the Lord’) and underlies the whole narrative of the return from exile — and ultimately the proclamation of the gospel as ‘the Way’ (Acts 9:2; John 14:6).

keyPoints.body:
Knowing the Lord’s nature and character changes how we see our situation. The turning point of chapter 33 is not a change in circumstances but a change in perspective. Seeing the Lord as he truly is transforms the experience of despair and betrayal.

The Lord will arise and be exalted. Verse 10 — ‘Now will I arise, says the Lord. Now will I be exalted’ — is the hinge of chapter 33. After the despair of vv7–9, the Lord’s declaration that he will act is the basis for all the hope that follows.

Seeing the King is the goal of the redeemed. The promise of 33:17 — ‘your eyes will see the King in his beauty’ — is one of the most personal and intimate promises in Isaiah. The ultimate hope of God’s people is the vision of the Lord himself.

Judgment and joy belong together. Chapters 34 and 35 must be read together. The joy of chapter 35 only makes sense in the context of the Lord’s judgment of chapter 34. Joy is not the denial of judgment but its consequence.

The Way of Holiness is the road home. Chapter 35’s vision of the redeemed walking the Way of Holiness back to Zion picks up the Exodus imagery and looks forward to the new Exodus — and ultimately to Christ as the Way (John 14:6).

exegetical.body:
‘Destroyer… traitor’ (33:1). The opening woe is addressed to Assyria — the destroyer who has not yet been destroyed, the traitor who has not yet been betrayed. The historical context is Sennacherib’s betrayal of Hezekiah after taking the ransom money. The principle is universal: those who destroy and betray will themselves face the same.

Who can dwell with the devouring fire? (33:14–16). The sinners in Zion tremble. The answer (vv15–16) is a description of moral integrity — walking righteously, speaking uprightly, rejecting unjust gain and violence. This is not a works-righteousness list but a description of the person who has genuinely trusted the Lord and been transformed by him.

‘Your eyes will see the King in his beauty’ (33:17). The King the redeemed will see is both the earthly Davidic king and ultimately the Lord himself. The vision of God is the highest aspiration of the OT people of God (cf. Psalm 27:4; 17:15). It finds its fulfilment in the beatific vision of Revelation 22:4.

Edom (34:5–17). Edom is chosen as the representative of all nations hostile to the Lord and his people. The desolation of Edom is described in graphic detail, with the land returning to wilderness, inhabited by wild animals.

The Way of Holiness (35:8). The road through the wilderness is holy — but the redeemed of the Lord will walk on it safely, even the most vulnerable (v9). The obstacles — burning sand, thirsty ground — will be transformed into water, streams, and green growth (vv6–7). The Way is the path of transformation as well as the path home.

pastorals.body:
Chapter 33 is a deeply pastoral chapter. The situation it addresses — Hezekiah’s betrayal by the Assyrians after paying an enormous ransom — is the situation of someone who did what seemed right and was let down anyway. The pastoral question: what resource do you have when human plans fail? The answer is not a programme but a person: the Lord, high and lifted up (v10), the King in his beauty (v17).

Chapter 35 is one of the most joyful passages in Isaiah and should be allowed to breathe. If the group has been through the sustained darkness of the preceding chapters, the vision of the redeemed of the Lord returning with singing and everlasting joy (v10) should feel like emerging from a long tunnel. Read it aloud. Give it time.

watch.body: Watch for the group treating chapter 34’s judgment as an embarrassing interruption between the promise of chapter 33 and the joy of chapter 35. The judgment is not an interruption but integral: the joy of the redeemed only exists on the other side of the Lord’s righteous judgment of everything that opposes him. Help the group hold judgment and joy together.

questions.header: 33:1–24: Returning to the exalted Lord who saves his people
questions.question: The background to chapter 33 is probably 2 Kings 18:13–16. What happens there and what is the result? How does an understanding of the Lord’s nature and character change how they see their situation? (vv1‑6)
questions.answer:
Hezekiah tries to buy off the Assyrians with temple treasure. But the Assyrians take the money and betray him anyway, advancing further toward Jerusalem. They tried the political solution and it failed.

Turning to God for grace, strength, and salvation. Seeing his sovereignty over the nations. Trusting for all the salvation and wisdom they need. The Lord is their stability, their wisdom, their knowledge, their salvation, their treasure (vv5–6). When human plans have failed, the Lord’s character is the remaining foundation.

questions.header: 33:1–24: Returning to the exalted Lord who saves his people
questions.question: How is their situation described, and what will the Lord do about it? (vv7‑12)
questions.answer: Despair, shame, sorrow, broken faith, weakness, withering — the roads are deserted, the treaties are broken, the land mourns (vv8–9). Then the Lord speaks: ‘Now will I arise’ (v10). He will be exalted. His breath will consume the nations like fire. The Lord’s arising is the answer to human despair.

questions.header: 33:1–24: Returning to the exalted Lord who saves his people
questions.question: What will it be like when the King saves his people? (vv13‑24) How does this apply to Judah and how does it apply to us, this side of the cross?
questions.answer: Those who walk righteously will dwell securely — seeing the King in his beauty (v17), in a land that stretches far (v17), in a peaceful dwelling (v20), with the Lord as judge, lawgiver, king, and saviour (v22). For Judah this was the survival of Jerusalem against the Assyrians. For us it is the vision of Christ, the King in his beauty, in whom all these promises find their fulfilment.

questions.header: 34:1–35:10: Judgment and Joy, Fear and Hope
questions.question: What is the message of the Lord to the nations? (34:1) What will the judgment be like, and why does he have a day of vengeance? (34:8)
questions.answer: All nations and peoples must listen — the Lord is angry with them all (v2). His judgment will be total and graphic — the skies rolled up, the mountains melting (vv4, 9‑10). The reason: ‘The Lord has a day of vengeance, a year of retribution, to uphold Zion’s cause’ (v8). The Lord defends his people by judging their enemies.

questions.header: 34:1–35:10: Judgment and Joy, Fear and Hope
questions.question: How does the salvation of the Lord in chapter 35 contrast with his judgment in chapter 34? Look at the promises in chapter 35. How do they give hope to the redeemed of God?
questions.answer:
Chapter 34: desolation, burning, smoke, wilderness, uninhabited. Chapter 35: desert blooming, abundance, streams in the wilderness, the Way of Holiness, the redeemed returning with singing and everlasting joy (v10). The contrast is total.

The promises of chapter 35: the blind see, the deaf hear, the lame leap, the mute sing (vv5–6); water in the wilderness (vv6–7); the Way of Holiness, safe from predators (vv8–9); everlasting joy and gladness, sorrow and sighing fleeing away (v10). Jesus quotes and embodies these promises in his ministry (cf. Luke 7:22).

questions.header: Application
questions.question: How does the contrast between judgment and salvation help us in our daily lives? What does it mean in practical terms to ‘long for the Lord’ and to ‘be strong and not fear’ (35:4)?
questions.answer: The contrast keeps our hope calibrated: we are not waiting for things to improve but for the Lord to arise and be exalted. Longing for the Lord means keeping our vision fixed on the King in his beauty (33:17) rather than on circumstances around us. Being strong and not fearing (35:4) is not self-confidence but confidence in the Lord — ‘your God will come, he will come with vengeance; with divine retribution he will come to save you’ (v4). The ground of courage is the certainty of his coming.
questions.leaderNote: The parallel between 35:5–6 (blind see, deaf hear, lame leap, mute sing) and Jesus’s answer to John the Baptist’s disciples in Luke 7:22 is worth pointing out. Jesus’s healing ministry was the beginning of the fulfilment of Isaiah 35 — the first fruits of the transformation that will be complete when the redeemed travel the Way of Holiness.

chapters.title: Isaiah 36–39 — Trust the Lord and Seek His Glory in Danger, Death, and Prosperity
chapters.bigIdea: Trust the Lord and seek his glory in the face of danger, death, and prosperity.

background.body:
Chapters 36–39 form the historical bridge between the two halves of Isaiah. They are largely parallel to 2 Kings 18–20 and describe two crises in Hezekiah’s reign: the Assyrian threat (chs 36–37) and his illness (chs 38–39), followed by the ominous Babylonian embassy that closes the section and points toward the exile that will be the context of chapters 40–66.

Read chapters 36–37 first, then chapters 38–39. The session notes recommend reading the chapters in two chunks and discussing each before moving on.

Hezekiah versus Ahaz. The contrast with the earlier study on Ahaz (session 4) is deliberate. Both kings face overwhelming military threats. Ahaz refused to trust the Lord and turned to Assyria. Hezekiah, despite his imperfections, models genuine trust — he goes to the temple, spreads the threatening letter before the Lord, and prays. His prayer is focused on the Lord’s glory, not just his own survival.

The field commander’s strategy (36:4–22). The Assyrian field commander (Rabshakeh) is a master of psychological warfare. His speech raises doubt about Egypt’s reliability, claims the Lord sent him, argues that Hezekiah’s removal of high places was sacrilege, and points to the track record of other gods failing to protect their cities. The speech is brilliant and devastating — and its logic is exposed as hollow by what follows.

The Babylonian embassy (ch 39). Merodach-Baladan of Babylon sends envoys ostensibly to congratulate Hezekiah on his recovery. Hezekiah shows them everything. Isaiah’s prophecy that all of this will be carried to Babylon is the first explicit prediction of the Babylonian exile and the transition that makes chapters 40–66 necessary.

keyPoints.body:
Hezekiah models genuine prayer in a crisis. When the field commander’s letter arrives, Hezekiah goes to the temple and spreads the letter before the Lord (37:14). His prayer (37:15–20) is focused not primarily on his own safety but on the Lord’s honour: ‘so that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that you, Lord, are the only God.’

The field commander’s blasphemy is the real issue. His speech raises the Lord to the same level as the gods of other nations. This is the blasphemy that provokes the Lord’s response. The Lord does not tolerate being compared to idols.

Hezekiah does better in crisis than in prosperity. In the Assyrian crisis and his illness, Hezekiah trusts the Lord and seeks his glory. But in chapter 39, when the Babylonian embassy arrives, he shows off everything he has — no prayer, no reference to the Lord. The pastoral question: why is it sometimes easier to trust the Lord in crisis than in prosperity?

The Lord acts in history in specific and decisive ways. The destruction of the Assyrian army (37:36 — 185,000 in one night) and the healing of Hezekiah are specific, datable, historical acts of the Lord.

The Babylonian embassy closes one era and opens another. The ominous ending of chapter 39 — ‘Everything in your palace will be carried off to Babylon’ (39:6) — is the transition point. From here, chapters 40–66 address exiles who need comfort, not the inhabitants of Jerusalem who need warning.

exegetical.body:
The geographical detail of 36:2. The field commander meets Hezekiah’s officials at the same location where Isaiah met Ahaz in 7:3 — ‘the upper pool, on the road to the Launderer’s Field.’ The repetition is almost certainly deliberate: it reminds the reader of the earlier offer of trust and Ahaz’s refusal. Hezekiah will respond differently.

The field commander’s theological argument (36:10). ‘Furthermore, have I come to attack and destroy this land without the Lord? The Lord himself told me to march against this country and destroy it.’ This echoes the truth that Assyria is the Lord’s instrument of judgment (cf. 10:5–6). But he goes too far by claiming to speak for the Lord directly.

Hezekiah’s prayer (37:15–20). Structure: (1) address to the Lord as the enthroned Creator (v16); (2) acknowledgment of what the Assyrians have done (vv17–18); (3) acknowledgment that the Assyrians are right about the impotence of other gods (v19); (4) petition for deliverance — not for Israel’s sake but ‘so that all the kingdoms of the earth may know that you, Lord, are the only God’ (v20). Prayer whose primary concern is the Lord’s glory.

Hezekiah’s poem (38:9–20). One of the most moving personal testimonies in the OT. It describes the terror of facing death (vv10–14), the Lord’s rescue (vv15–17), and the resolution to praise for ever (vv18–20). The key verse is 38:17: ‘you have put all my sins behind your back.’

The sign of the stairway (38:7–8). The shadow going back ten steps stands in direct contrast to Ahaz’s refusal of a sign in chapter 7. Hezekiah asks; the Lord provides. The contrast underscores the difference between faith that asks and unbelief that refuses.

pastorals.body:
The contrast between Hezekiah in crisis (trusting, prayerful, God-focused) and Hezekiah in prosperity (self-displaying, careless) is one of the most pastorally honest portraits in the OT. It should not be handled in a way that simply condemns Hezekiah — help the group see themselves in his pattern. The question ‘why is it sometimes harder to trust the Lord when things are going well?’ is genuinely difficult and deserves honest engagement.

Hezekiah’s reaction to Isaiah’s prophecy in 39:8 is deeply ambiguous: ‘The word of the Lord you have spoken is good… for there will be peace and security in my lifetime.’ Is this genuine acceptance or selfish relief? The group may have different views. Either way: he seems primarily concerned with his own generation rather than those who will follow.

watch.body: Watch for the group spending all the session on the Assyrian crisis (chs 36–37) and running out of time for the illness and Babylonian embassy (chs 38–39). The Babylonian embassy is theologically crucial as the transition to the second half of Isaiah — and the contrast between Hezekiah in crisis and Hezekiah in prosperity is the pastoral heart of the session.

questions.header: 36:1–22: Sennacherib’s Threat
questions.question: What happens in the fourteenth year of Hezekiah’s reign? Why do you think Isaiah includes the geographical detail in v2? (Cf. 7:3.) What is the field commander trying to achieve? What techniques does he use? (vv4–22)
questions.answer:
Sennacherib attacks all the fortified cities of Judah and sends his field commander to Jerusalem. The geographical detail — the upper pool on the road to the Launderer’s Field — is the same location as Isaiah’s meeting with Ahaz in 7:3. The repetition is deliberate: another king faces another overwhelming military threat at the same spot. Hezekiah will respond differently.

The field commander’s techniques: he points out the weakness of Egypt (v6); suggests the Lord is angry with Hezekiah because of his removal of high places (v7); claims the Lord sent him (v10); argues that Hezekiah and the Lord cannot save them (vv14–15, 18–20); points that no god has saved any nation from the Assyrians. He speaks publicly to terrify the people as well as the officials.

questions.header: 37:1–28: Hezekiah’s response
questions.question: How does Hezekiah respond to the threat? What is his main concern? What message does Isaiah give from the Lord? What happens?
questions.answer:
He tears his clothes and goes to the temple. He sends for Isaiah to hear from the Lord. His main concern is for the Lord’s honour (37:4; 37:17; 37:20). His primary anxiety is not his own survival but the blasphemy against the Lord.

Isaiah tells Hezekiah not to be afraid (v6) — the Lord will cause Sennacherib to withdraw and fall by the sword in his own land (v7). The blasphemy: the field commander has compared the Lord to the gods of other nations that were powerless (36:18–20; 37:10–13).

What happens: the Assyrian army is destroyed by the Lord’s angel (185,000 in one night, v36), Sennacherib withdraws, and he is killed by his own sons in the temple of his god (v37–38). Irony: the god supposedly so much more powerful than the Lord cannot protect his worshipper.

questions.header: 37:1–28: Hezekiah’s response
questions.question: What is the main focus of Hezekiah’s prayer? What does this say about his priorities?
questions.answer: Focused on the Lord’s glory, not primarily his own protection. He addresses the Lord as the enthroned Creator (v16), acknowledges the Assyrian claims honestly (vv17–19), and asks for deliverance so that ‘all the kingdoms of the earth may know that you, Lord, are the only God’ (v20). His own survival is not the ultimate goal — the Lord’s renown is.

questions.header: 38:1–39:8: Hezekiah’s illness and recovery
questions.question: What is Hezekiah’s response to his illness? How is Hezekiah a contrast to Ahaz in 7:11–12?
questions.answer: He turns his face to the wall and prays and weeps (vv2–3). He asks for and receives a sign from the Lord (the shadow going back on the stairway of Ahaz, vv7–8). He contrasts directly with Ahaz: Ahaz refused a sign, hiding behind pious language. Hezekiah asks for one and accepts what the Lord says. Both face mortal crises; one trusts and asks, the other refuses and turns away.

questions.header: 38:1–39:8: Hezekiah’s illness and recovery
questions.question: How does Hezekiah describe his experience in vv9–20? What lessons has he learned?
questions.answer: He describes the terror of facing death (vv10–14) — being cut off from the Lord’s presence. The Lord’s rescue (vv15–17) — ‘you have put all my sins behind your back’ (v17). The resolution to praise for ever (vv18–20). He has learned: the living, only the living, can praise the Lord. And the Lord’s rescue involves the forgiveness of sin, not just the extension of life.

questions.header: 38:1–39:8: Hezekiah’s illness and recovery
questions.question: Where does Hezekiah go wrong in chapter 39? How does this show a different attitude from the previous chapters?
questions.answer: When the Babylonian envoys arrive, Hezekiah shows them everything — all his treasures, his armoury, his storehouses. There is no prayer, no reference to the Lord, no seeking of guidance. He is focused on himself and his own glory, not the Lord’s. When Isaiah announces that everything will be carried to Babylon, Hezekiah is relieved that it will not happen in his lifetime (v8). Prosperity has undermined the trust he showed in crisis.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: What priorities do we see in Hezekiah’s prayers? What would similar priorities look like for us? What does it look like to trust the Lord in a crisis? Why do you think Hezekiah did better in crises than in prosperity? What is the remedy?
questions.answer:
Hezekiah’s prayer priorities: the Lord’s honour first, the Lord’s word sought, honest acknowledgment of the situation, petition grounded in the Lord’s character. For us: prayer that starts with the Lord’s glory rather than our own needs; willingness to spread our ‘letters’ before the Lord honestly.

Crises strip away the illusion of self-sufficiency; prosperity can restore it. The remedy: cultivating in prosperity the habits of dependence that crisis forces upon us — regular prayer, ongoing reference to the Lord, conscious gratitude that redirects glory to him.
questions.leaderNote: The contrast between Hezekiah in chapter 37 (spreading the letter before the Lord) and Hezekiah in chapter 39 (showing off his treasures) is the pastoral heart of the session. What is Hezekiah doing with his ‘assets’ in each case? In crisis he brings them to the Lord; in prosperity he displays them to others.

chapters.title: Isaiah 40 — The Great God Is Coming to Comfort, Gather, and Renew His People
chapters.bigIdea: The great God is coming to comfort, gather, and renew his people. Nothing compares to him, so keep trusting in him as we wait.

background.body:
Chapter 40 is one of the great hinges of Scripture. After the darkness of chapters 1–39, the sustained indictment and judgment, the ominous ending of chapter 39, the first word is: ‘Comfort, comfort my people.’ The change of tone is as dramatic as anything in the Bible.

The context: the Babylonian exile (prophetically anticipated). Chapters 40–66 address the situation of the Babylonian exiles — a situation Isaiah is seeing prophetically. The exiles need comfort, not warning. The first word of this new section addresses that need directly.

The structure of chapter 40. Three clear sections: vv1–11 (the great God comes to comfort his people), vv12–26 (the great God has no rivals), vv27–31 (the great God renews his people). The movement is from the announcement of comfort to the theological grounding of that comfort to its practical application.

The New Testament use of this chapter. All four Gospels quote 40:3‑5 in their accounts of John the Baptist (Matthew 3:3; Mark 1:3; Luke 3:4‑6; John 1:23). Paul quotes 40:13 in Romans 11:34 and 1 Corinthians 2:16. The NT sees chapter 40 as describing the coming of the Lord in the person of Jesus.

The theological argument of vv12–26. A sustained argument for the incomparable greatness of the Lord over against the nations, idols, and rulers of the world. The Lord who created everything is immeasurably greater than any nation, any idol, or any prince. Therefore trusting him is the only rational response to reality.

keyPoints.body:
‘Comfort’ is the first word of a new era. The double ‘comfort, comfort’ (v1) is emphatic and deliberate. After the sustained darkness of chapters 1–39, the Lord’s first word in this new section is not rebuke but consolation. Help the group feel the weight of this transition.

The Lord is coming in a way that cannot be stopped. The vision of vv3‑5 — mountains levelled, valleys filled, the glory of the Lord revealed before all flesh — describes a coming that is unstoppable. Nothing in the landscape of human history can prevent the Lord from coming to his people.

The word of our God endures for ever. Verses 6–8 are a comfort in themselves: all flesh is frail and passing, but the word of the Lord stands for ever. His promises cannot fail because he is eternal.

The Lord’s incomparability is the theological ground of hope. The sustained argument of vv12–26 — the Lord is greater than the nations, greater than idols, greater than princes — is not mere theology but pastoral comfort. The Babylonians may seem to rule the world, but the Lord is the one enthroned above the circle of the earth (v22), who reduces princes to nothing (v23).

Those who wait on the Lord will renew their strength. The final promise (v31) is one of the most comforting in all of Isaiah. Waiting is active trust in the Lord’s timing and purposes. And the promise is specific: even those who are exhausted will find new energy; even those who have stumbled will soar.

exegetical.body:
‘Her hard service has been completed’ (40:2). The language of ‘hard service’ is military or labour-service language — the exile is compared to a period of enforced service that has a fixed end point. The Lord is announcing that the end has come. The double blessing is not that she has been punished twice over but that her restoration will be doubly generous.

‘Prepare the way for the Lord’ (40:3). All four Gospels quote this verse in introducing John the Baptist. In the Gospels, the Lord’s coming is in the person of Jesus. The identification of Jesus’s coming as the fulfilment of Isaiah 40:3‑5 is one of the most significant hermeneutical moves in the NT.

‘Sovereign Lord comes with power, and he tends his flock like a shepherd’ (40:10–11). The juxtaposition of sovereign power and tender shepherding is one of Isaiah’s favourite paradoxes. Help the group hold both together.

Comparing idols with the Lord (40:18–20). The idol polemic is the first of several in chapters 40–48. The argument is simple and devastating: the Lord asks ‘to whom will you compare me?’ and points to the idols — made by craftsmen, carried by beasts, unable to move.

‘Those who hope in the Lord’ (40:31). The Hebrew qavah carries the sense of active, expectant waiting — not passive resignation but confident anticipation. The promise is graduated: they will soar, run, and walk — even the humblest form of faithful endurance is sustained by the Lord.

pastorals.body:
Chapter 40 is one of the great pastoral chapters of the Bible. If the group has been through the darkness of chapters 1–39, the opening ‘Comfort, comfort my people’ should land with considerable force. Give it time.

The question of vv27–28 — ‘Why do you say, Israel, “My way is hidden from the Lord; my cause is disregarded by my God”?’ — is worth pressing into personally with the group. The Lord’s response is not rebuke but reassurance: do you not know? have you not heard?

The promise of v31 is familiar to many and therefore easily heard without being felt. Help the group slow down and receive it. Ask them to think about specific situations of exhaustion, discouragement, and failure where this promise speaks.

watch.body: Watch for the group treating the theological argument of vv12–26 as abstract theology and missing its pastoral function. The argument is directly aimed at the exiles’ specific experience: the Babylonians seem to rule everything, their gods seem powerful, their king seems supreme. The theological argument is the answer to the pastoral despair. Keep connecting the theology to the experience.

questions.header: 40:1–11: The Great God comforts his people
questions.question: Look at 40:1‑2 and think back to chapter 39. What clues are there as to the situation this prophecy is speaking into? Why is it a message of comfort?
questions.answer: Chapter 39 ended with the prophecy that everything would be carried to Babylon. Now the Lord speaks comfort: their hard service has been completed, their sin has been paid for, a double portion of blessing is coming. The exile will not be permanent — the Lord will comfort his people and the judgment will end.

questions.header: 40:1‑11: The Great God comforts his people
questions.question: Go through vv1‑11 and pick out the messages of comfort the Lord offers his people.
questions.answer: Her hard service has been completed (v2); the Lord is coming (v3); the glory of the Lord will be revealed and all people will see it together (v5); the word of our God endures for ever (v8); the Sovereign Lord comes with power (v10); the Lord tends his flock with gentleness — gathering lambs in his arms, carrying them close, gently leading the nursing ewes (v11).

questions.header: 40:1‑11: The Great God comforts his people
questions.question: What will the coming of the Lord be like in vv3‑5? How would this comfort the exiles? Read John 1:23 and Luke 3:4‑6. How does the NT apply these verses?
questions.answer:
Heralded in advance and without obstacle. Even the natural world cannot stop his coming — mountains levelled, valleys raised, rough ground made level. His coming will be glorious and seen by everyone. Nothing can stop the Lord from coming to save and restore his people.

The NT applies these verses to John the Baptist and the coming of Jesus. John’s ministry in the wilderness is the voice crying to prepare the way. The Lord who comes is Jesus himself. The exile of the human race from God’s presence can only be ended in the person and ministry of Jesus.
questions.leaderNote: All four Gospels quote Isaiah 40:3 in their accounts of John the Baptist. The identification of Jesus’s coming as the fulfilment of this verse is a fundamental NT claim: the Lord who comes to comfort his exiled people comes in the person of Jesus.

questions.header: 40:1‑11: The Great God comforts his people
questions.question: How are vv6‑8 a comfort to the people of God?
questions.answer: Humanity is frail and passing at its very best. But the Lord and his word are eternal. His promises can be utterly relied upon. So whatever the exiles experience of human frailty and imperial power, the Lord’s word that he will comfort and restore his people cannot fail — because he endures for ever.

questions.header: 40:1‑11: The Great God comforts his people
questions.question: What images are used to describe God in vv9‑11? Why is this good news for the exiles? How do we see vv10‑11 fulfilled in Jesus?
questions.answer: Sovereign Lord and tender Shepherd. To the exiles, the Babylonians seemed to rule — but God is the true Sovereign. And in their suffering he is the Shepherd who gathers and gently cares. In Jesus: throughout his ministry he showed both authority (calming storms, raising the dead) and tenderness (the Good Shepherd who lays down his life, who gathers the children, who heals and restores).

questions.header: 40:12‑26: The Great God has no rivals
questions.question: What is emphasised about the Lord in vv12–26? Isaiah contrasts the Lord with the nations (vv15–17), with idols (vv18–20), and with rulers of the world (vv22–24). What is emphasised in each contrast? How would this comfort the people of God in exile?
questions.answer:
His immensity as Creator, his wisdom and knowledge, his sovereignty over all creation. He holds the waters in his hand, measures the heavens with a span, brings out the stars by name.

Nations: to him all nations are just a drop of water or a little dust. Not intimidating.
Idols: manmade and pathetic. Idolatry is absurd in the context of the Lord’s greatness.
Rulers: they soon wither and are blown away like chaff. But the Lord is enthroned above the earth.

To the exiles: Babylon may seem impressive, but the kings are chaff, the idols are pathetic, the nations are a drop of water. The Lord is the one enthroned. It is an easy thing for him to gather his people from exile.

questions.header: 40:27‑31: The Great God renews his people
questions.question: Why might the people of God think he has forgotten them? How does the Lord address this fear? How are the people of God to respond? (v31)
questions.answer:
In exile, surrounded by Babylonian power and its gods, it would have seemed that the Lord is very distant and has cast off his people for ever. His response: if he can watch over the stars so that not one is missing, he can watch over his people. He is untiring and all-wise. He can strengthen his people in their weakness.

The response (v31): wait on the Lord — active, expectant, confident trust in his timing. Those who wait will soar, run, and walk without fainting.
questions.leaderNote: Allow time for this final question. Ask the group: in what specific situations of exhaustion, discouragement, or failure does this promise speak? Let the familiar words land freshly.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: Are we tempted to feel as Israel did and think that our ‘way is hidden from the Lord’? How does chapter 40 address this? What is the impact of v11 (the shepherd) in the context of the whole chapter? What difference does chapter 40 make to us as we live as ‘foreigners and exiles’ on earth? (1 Peter 2:11)
questions.answer:
The temptation to feel forgotten is universal. Chapter 40 addresses it not by denying the reality of hardship but by reorienting the sufferer toward the Lord’s incomparable greatness and faithful care. The shepherd of v11 is surprising after the cosmic sovereignty of vv10–26: the Lord who holds the nations as a drop in a bucket also carries lambs in his arms. The contrast is the comfort.

As exiles and foreigners we are in exactly the situation the Babylonian exiles faced: living far from our true home, under the apparent sovereignty of other powers. Chapter 40 gives us resources to endure: the word that endures for ever, the Shepherd who carries us, the Creator who renews our strength.
questions.leaderNote: 1 Peter 2:11 (‘foreigners and exiles’) is a rich cross-reference. Peter’s letter is written to scattered exiles and draws extensively on Exodus and exile imagery of Isaiah. The situation of the NT church is, in Peter’s reading, directly analogous to that of the Babylonian exiles. Isaiah 40 speaks to us too.

chapters.title: Isaiah 41:1–42:17 — Sing Praise to the One True God Who Through His Servant Brings Justice to the Earth
chapters.bigIdea: Sing praise to the one true God who through his Servant brings light to the Gentiles, sight to the blind, freedom to the captives, and justice for the whole earth.

background.body:
This session covers two related sections: the courtroom scene of chapter 41 and the first Servant Song (42:1–9), followed by a call to praise (42:10–17). It introduces one of the central figures of Isaiah 40–66: the Servant of the Lord.

The courtroom metaphor (41:1–29). Isaiah uses a legal/courtroom framework extensively in chapters 40–48. The nations are summoned before the Lord as judge; the idols are called to present their evidence; the Lord presents his own case. The metaphor serves a specific theological purpose: the Lord’s claim to be the one true God is not merely asserted but argued, with evidence and logical demonstration.

The Servant Songs. Isaiah 40–66 contains four passages known as the Servant Songs (42:1–4; 49:1–6; 50:4‑9; 52:13–53:12). In the immediate context, Israel is called the Lord’s servant (41:8; 42:19); but the Servant of the songs is distinguished from Israel by his perfect obedience and his mission to Israel. The NT consistently identifies the Servant as Jesus (Matthew 12:15–21; Acts 8:32–33; 1 Peter 2:22–25).

Cyrus (41:2, 25). The ‘one from the east’ and ‘one from the north’ are generally understood to refer to Cyrus the Great, the Persian king who will overthrow Babylon and allow the exiles to return (cf. 44:28; 45:1). He is significant in Isaiah’s argument: the Lord controls world history, including the rise and fall of empires, and his prediction of Cyrus’ actions is evidence of his divinity that the idols cannot match.

The handout for 41:8–20. The PDF includes a handout exercise for 41:8–20 in which three transformations offered by the Lord to his people are worked through. The completed version is reproduced in the handout section below.

keyPoints.body:
The Lord is the sovereign over all of history. Who raised up Cyrus? Who has ruled the world since the beginning? The answer is the Lord. All world leaders are ultimately in his service, whether they know it or not.

The nations’ response to this truth is futile idolatry. Instead of renewing their strength in the Lord (cf. 40:31), the nations respond with fear, empty alliances, and useless idol-making (41:5–7). Their idols cannot even stand up without being nailed down.

The Lord transforms the lives of his people through their weakness. The three transformations of 41:8–20 — from enslaved to upheld (vv8–13), from weak to powerful (vv14–16), from thirsty to refreshed (vv17–20) — all begin with an uncomplimentary description of God’s people. The Lord meets them in their weakness and transforms their situation.

The Servant of the Lord is distinct from Israel. The first Servant Song (42:1–4) describes someone chosen, filled with the Spirit, bringing justice to the whole world, gentle and unfaltering. The NT’s identification of this figure as Jesus is not an imposition on the text but its most natural fulfilment.

The Servant’s mission is universal in scope. Justice for ‘the nations’ (42:1), for ‘the earth’ (42:4), for ‘a light for the Gentiles’ (42:6). This universal scope points directly toward the mission of Jesus and the church.

exegetical.body:
The idols’ futility (41:5–7). While the Lord upholds his people, the nations have to carry their gods and nail them down so they won’t fall over. The Lord’s people are upheld by him; the nations’ gods have to be upheld by them. Who is the stronger?

‘You worm Jacob, little Israel’ (41:14). Not an insult but an honest description of Israel’s condition in exile — weak, small, apparently insignificant. It is precisely from this point of acknowledged weakness that the Lord’s transformation begins.

The Servant Song (42:1–4). Four characteristics: (1) chosen and delighted in by the Lord; (2) endowed with the Spirit; (3) bringing justice to the nations; (4) gentle and unfaltering — he will not break a bruised reed or snuff out a smouldering wick. This fourth characteristic is particularly striking: the Servant’s power is expressed in gentleness, not domination.

‘I will keep you and make you to be a covenant for the people and a light for the Gentiles’ (42:6). The Servant does not merely bring a covenant or proclaim a light — he is the covenant and is the light. Jesus is identified as the fulfilment: he is the light of the world (John 8:12) and the mediator of the new covenant (Hebrews 8:6; 9:15).

‘A new song’ (42:10). Almost all references to ‘a new song’ in Scripture are in response to God’s salvation (Psalm 33:3; 40:3; 96:1; 98:1; Revelation 5:9; 14:3). The new thing the Lord is doing through his Servant calls for a new song from the whole earth.

pastorals.body:
The handout exercise for 41:8–20 is designed to help the group engage actively rather than passively. Give it adequate time — the three transformations are each worth working through, and the exercise helps the group see the consistent pattern: the Lord meets his people in their actual weakness and transforms their situation through his strength, not theirs.

The Servant Song (42:1–4) is theologically rich. Help the group read it slowly and carefully. Matthew’s quotation (12:15–21) in the context of Jesus’s healing ministry is worth reading alongside it — it shows how the gentle, restoring character of the Servant’s mission is embodied in specific acts of healing.

watch.body: Watch for the group rushing through the courtroom scene (41:1–29) to get to the Servant Song. The courtroom establishes the theological framework that makes the Servant Song intelligible: the Lord is the sovereign of history, the idols are nothing, and it is in this context that the Lord announces his Servant.

handout.body:
The handout asks the group to work through three transformations offered by the Lord to his people in 41:8–20. The completed version (for leader reference) is below.

v8–13v14–16v17–20
Uncomplimentary descriptionMy servant (literally ‘slave’)You worm Jacob, little IsraelPoor and needy
Their situationThey have enemies (vv11–12). They are afraid.They are weak and afraid in the face of their enemies.They are parched and thirsty — in a spiritual and physical desert.
How the Lord promises to transform itHe will strengthen and uphold them. He will overturn their enemies. He reminds them of their ongoing relationship with him (v10, 13).The Lord will make them phenomenally strong. They will overcome their enemies in a way that brings glory to the Lord (vv14–16).The Lord will provide water and shade where it is least expected — streams in the desert, trees in the wasteland.
What it demonstrates about the LordTenderness, strength, covenant faithfulness, ability to help.Weakness is not a problem to the Lord. He is able to help and overturn bad situations.He is the life-giving Creator who cares for his people even in the most barren circumstances.

questions.header: 41:1‑7: The Lord rules the world
questions.question: What scene is set in v1? What questions are presented in the courtroom? (vv2‑4) What point is being made? Instead of renewing their strength in the Lord, how do the nations respond? Why is their response folly? (vv5‑7)
questions.answer:
A courtroom scene: the nations are gathered before the Lord as judge. The questions: who calls a world leader (Cyrus) into his service? (v2) Who has ruled the world since the beginning? (v4) The point: all world leaders are ultimately in the service of the Lord. He is the sovereign of history.

The nations respond with fear (v5); empty alliances (v6); useless idolatry — hurriedly making new idols and propping them up so they won’t fall (v7). Their idolatry is worthless because they make these gods themselves — the nations have to uphold their gods; the Lord upholds his people.

questions.header: 41:8–20: The Lord transforms his people’s lives
questions.question: In vv8–20 we see God’s people referred to in uncomplimentary ways, and then offers of transformation. Work through the handout and discuss what the Lord offers each group of people and what it demonstrates about him. How does the Lord’s dealings with his people contrast with the nations’ relationship with their gods?
questions.answer:
See the Handout section for full details. The consistent pattern: the Lord meets his people in their actual weakness — as slaves, as worms, as the poor and needy — and transforms their situation through his strength, not theirs.

No comparison: the Lord acts for his people and upholds and provides for them. The nations have to create and steady their idols so they won’t topple over. The Lord’s people are carried by him; the nations’ gods must be carried by them.

questions.header: 41:21‑29: Idols are nothing but the Lord controls history
questions.question: The courtroom metaphor continues. Who is in the dock and what evidence does the Lord ask from them? (vv21–23) What conclusion does the Lord come to? (v24) How does the Lord compare? (vv25‑29)
questions.answer:
The idols of the world are in the dock. The Lord asks them to prove their divinity by recounting history and predicting the future — or to do anything at all (v23). Conclusion: idols are worthless, less than nothing; it is detestable to worship them (v24). They can say and do nothing.

The Lord by contrast acts in history and controls it. He foretells the future (vv25‑26, referring to Cyrus) and knows the end from the beginning. The idols can do nothing; the Lord does everything.

questions.header: 42:1‑9: The Servant of the Lord
questions.question: The atmosphere suddenly changes as the Servant of the Lord is introduced. What do we learn about the Servant in vv1‑4? What is his mission? What is he like? How is he different from leaders like Cyrus? Read Matthew 12:15–21. How does Matthew show that Jesus is the Servant of the Lord?
questions.answer:
Chosen by God who delights in him. Has the Spirit of the Lord. Brings justice to the whole world. Not aggressive or brash — gentle and unfaltering. Will not break a bruised reed or snuff out a smouldering wick. A hope for all the nations. Cyrus is the Lord’s instrument for political change; the Servant brings something far deeper — justice, light, and release from darkness.

Matthew’s context: Jesus is healing but not drawing attention to himself. Not using the sick for self-promotion. The specific context illuminates what ‘not breaking a bruised reed’ looks like in practice.
questions.leaderNote: Reading Matthew 12:15–21 alongside this question is valuable. Matthew quotes Isaiah 42:1‑4 in the context of Jesus’s healing ministry and withdrawal from public confrontation, showing how the Servant’s gentleness is embodied in specific acts.

questions.header: 42:1‑9: The Servant of the Lord
questions.question: How does the Lord reassure the Servant? How do vv6‑8 help us understand who Jesus is and what he came to do?
questions.answer:
The Lord is the Almighty Creator who brings life to everything (v5). He is the one calling and sustaining his Servant. The Servant cannot fail to come and cannot fail to complete his task.

Jesus doesn’t just bring about the new covenant — he is the covenant for the people of God. He is the one who releases the world from the darkness and imprisonment of idolatry (v7). The ministry of Jesus, the Covenant of God, is part of God’s glory (v8).

questions.header: 42:10‑17: Praise the Lord
questions.question: What response to the Lord’s work through his Servant is called for? Who is the response for? What does the Lord promise to do?
