questions.answer: A new song — not the old song of familiar religious routine but a new song in response to the new thing the Lord is doing. The response is not just for Israel: the ends of the earth, the sea, the islands, the desert, Kedar, the mountaintops are all called to sing. The Lord promises to come like a warrior and defeat his enemies (v13), to do something new among the Gentiles (v14), and to lead the blind by ways they did not know, turning darkness into light before them (v16).
questions.leaderNote: The universality of the new song is worth emphasising: the scope of this particular new song — from the sea to the desert, from Kedar to the mountaintops — reflects the scope of the Servant’s mission.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: How does this passage help us understand Jesus, the Servant of the Lord, and his ministry better? What has been completely fulfilled and what are we still waiting for? As those who have been brought into the people of God from the ends of the earth, what cause do we have to sing a new song?
questions.answer: Jesus is the Servant who has come: chosen by the Father, filled with the Spirit at his baptism, bringing justice and light, gentle with the bruised and smouldering. His mission to be a light for the Gentiles is underway — people from every nation have been gathered to him. The new song is already being sung from every tribe, language, people, and nation (Revelation 5:9). What remains: the final and complete establishment of justice in all the earth (v4), the full transformation of darkness into light (v16). We live between the Servant’s first coming and his return, singing the new song and waiting for the day when the whole creation joins in.
questions.leaderNote: This is a good session to close with genuine praise. Consider reading Revelation 5:9–10 alongside Isaiah 42:10 — the new song of the redeemed fulfilling the prophecy of Isaiah.

chapters.title: Isaiah 42:18–44:23 — The Lord Redeems His Failing Servant Israel
chapters.bigIdea: Despite Israel’s failure and blindness, the Lord redeems them by his grace and calls them to remember, return, and rejoice.

background.body:
This session is designed around the two-part handout exercise in the PDF. The passage falls into two sections (42:18–43:21 and 43:22–44:23) which are worked through in sequence.

The contrast between the Servant (42:1–4) and Israel (42:18–25). The session opens by revisiting the Servant Song and comparing it with Israel’s description as the Lord’s servant in 42:18–25. The contrast is stark: the Servant of the Song is chosen, filled with the Spirit, bringing justice. Israel the servant is blind, deaf, and plundered — because of its own sin. This contrast is theologically essential: it shows why a different Servant is needed.

The ‘three Rs’ of 43:22–44:23. The second section is structured around three movements: Remember (44:21), Return (44:22), and Rejoice (44:23). These three responses to the Lord’s salvation are the practical application of what the Lord announces about himself.

The idol polemic (44:9–20). This extended and satirical description of idolatry — a man cuts down a tree, uses half for firewood, and carves the other half into a god — is one of the most sustained pieces of prophetic irony in the OT. Its function is not primarily mockery but theological diagnosis: this is what happens when the creature forgets the Creator.

keyPoints.body:
Israel the servant is blind, deaf, and failing. Israel was called to be the Lord’s servant — a light to the nations — but has failed completely. This failure is why a different Servant is needed.

The Lord’s discipline is purposeful, but Israel has not understood it. 42:25 is key: the Lord has poured out his wrath on Israel — but they did not understand and paid no attention. The discipline that should have produced repentance has been ignored.

The Lord redeems his people despite their failure — because he is their God. The astonishing grace of 43:1–7 — ‘Fear not, for I have redeemed you; I have summoned you by name; you are mine’ — comes immediately after the description of Israel’s blindness and failure.

The Lord alone is God — the testimony of Israel is the proof. 43:10–13: ‘Before me no god was formed, nor will there be one after me. I, even I, am the Lord, and apart from me there is no saviour.’

Idolatry is self-deception. The idol polemic of 44:9–20 exposes idolatry not just as futile but as self-deception: ‘a deluded heart has led him astray’ (v20). The application extends to any functional idol — any source of identity or security made by human hands or human imagination.

exegetical.body:
‘Who is blind but my servant?’ (42:19). Rhetorical and devastating. The one who should be the Lord’s messenger is blind to his message; the one who should be the Lord’s servant is deaf to his word.

‘When you pass through the waters, I will be with you’ (43:2). The promise of the Lord’s presence in the midst of water and fire echoes the Exodus. The Lord has been with his people in their deepest trials before; he will be with them again.

The new Exodus (43:14–21). The Lord announces a new act that will surpass the Exodus from Egypt: ‘Forget the former things’ (v18). Not a dismissal of the Exodus but a claim that what the Lord is about to do will be so remarkable it will make the first Exodus seem small by comparison.

The idol polemic structure (44:9–20). Carefully structured: (1) the craftsman makes an idol and worships it (vv9–12); (2) a man cuts down a tree — half for firewood, half becomes a god (vv13–17); (3) the theological diagnosis: a deluded heart has led him astray; he cannot see the lie in his own hand (vv18–20).

pastorals.body:
The contrast between Israel’s failure (42:18–25) and the Lord’s grace (43:1–7) is pastorally powerful. Many people will identify with Israel’s failure — awareness of how far short they have fallen of what they were called to be. The move from that honest acknowledgment to ‘fear not, for I have redeemed you; I have summoned you by name; you are mine’ is the movement of the gospel. Don’t rush past it.

The idol polemic of 44:9–20 can feel remote (nobody makes wooden idols). Help the group engage with the underlying diagnosis: the deluded heart that is unable to see the lie in its own hand. What are the functional idols — things made by human hands and human imagination — that we feed on as ashes?

watch.body: Watch for the group skipping lightly over the description of Israel’s failure in 42:18–25 to get to the grace of 43:1–7. The grace only lands with full force when the failure has been honestly reckoned with. The sequence — failure, discipline, grace — is the shape of the gospel, and it requires all three movements.

questions.header: 42:18–43:21: Israel’s failure and the Lord’s grace
questions.question: What is the problem with Israel in these verses? Contrast Israel as the Lord’s servant (42:18–25) with the Servant of 42:1–4. How does that add to our understanding of Jesus’s ministry?
questions.answer:
Israel the servant is blind, deaf, robbed, plundered, and trapped — because of its own sin. They have not obeyed the Lord’s law (v24). The Lord has poured out his wrath on them and they have not understood (v25).

The contrast with the Servant of 42:1–4 is total: that Servant is chosen and delighted in, filled with the Spirit, bringing justice, gentle and unfaltering. Israel was called to be this but has failed completely. This is why a different Servant is needed — Jesus does perfectly what Israel was called to do.

questions.header: 42:18–43:21: Israel’s failure and the Lord’s grace
questions.question: What solution does the Lord offer Israel? How does the poetic imagery help us understand what the Lord will do?
questions.answer: ‘Fear not, for I have redeemed you; I have summoned you by name; you are mine’ (v1). The solution is grace — the Lord’s unilateral act of redemption, not earned or deserved. The imagery: through water and fire, the Lord will be with his people; nations will be given as their ransom (vv3–4); he will gather them from the ends of the earth (vv5‑6). The language deliberately echoes the Exodus.

questions.header: 42:18–43:21: Israel’s failure and the Lord’s grace
questions.question: What reason is given for the Lord’s solution? (vv8–13) What relationship does Israel’s testimony have to the Lord’s solution? (vv10–11)
questions.answer: The Lord is the one true God — ‘Before me no god was formed, nor will there be one after me’ (v10). Apart from him there is no saviour (v11). Israel is called to be his witnesses — the evidence of his reality and power in the world. The reason the Lord redeems and restores Israel is in part so that they can testify to his uniqueness among the nations.

questions.header: 42:18–43:21: Israel’s failure and the Lord’s grace
questions.question: What will be the outcome of the Lord’s solution? Is there a pattern in his dealings with his people?
questions.answer: The Lord will deliver them from Babylon (v14), overthrow the Babylonians, and bring them home in a new Exodus — so remarkable that the original Exodus will seem old news (vv18–19). He will make a way in the desert and rivers in the wasteland (vv19–20). The pattern: the Lord has done this before (Egypt) and will do it again (Babylon). Each successive act of salvation surpasses the previous one.

questions.header: 43:22–44:23: Israel’s sin and the Lord’s pardon
questions.question: What is the problem with Israel’s relationship to the Lord in 43:22–28?
questions.answer: They have not called on the Lord or wearied themselves for him (v22) — they have brought him no offerings. Instead they have burdened him with their sins (v24). Their religious leaders have sinned (vv27–28). Israel’s relationship to the Lord has been characterised by neglect, sin, and unfaithfulness.

questions.header: 43:22–44:23: Israel’s sin and the Lord’s pardon
questions.question: What solution does the Lord offer in 44:1‑5? How does the poetic imagery help us understand what the Lord will do?
questions.answer: Despite Israel’s sin, the Lord will pour out his Spirit on their offspring and his blessing on their descendants (vv3‑4). The imagery: as water poured on thirsty ground makes it spring up with growth, the Spirit poured on Israel’s descendants will produce spiritual flourishing. And people will claim the Lord as their God — some even writing ‘The Lord’s’ on their hand (v5).
questions.leaderNote: The Spirit imagery anticipates the great Spirit passages of Isaiah 32:15 and 61:1–3, and ultimately Pentecost (Acts 2:17–21 quoting Joel 2:28–29, itself echoing Isaiah). The pattern — the Spirit poured out on the dry and thirsty — is the shape of renewal throughout Scripture.

questions.header: 43:22–44:23: Israel’s sin and the Lord’s pardon
questions.question: What reason is given for the Lord’s solution? (vv6‑8) What is the effect of the long section on idols? (vv9–20) What point is made about the Lord?
questions.answer:
The Lord is ‘the first and the last; apart from me there is no God’ (v6). There is no other Rock (v8). He alone declared the future from the beginning (v7). This uniqueness is the reason for his solution: there is no one else to save.

The idol section (vv9–20) makes turning to anything other than the Lord seem not just sinful but profoundly foolish. A man uses half a log for firewood and carves the other half into a god (vv16–17). He cannot see the lie in his own hand (v20). The Lord’s uniqueness is not merely a theological claim but the most obvious reality in the world — if you have not been deceived by a deluded heart.

questions.header: 43:22–44:23: Israel’s sin and the Lord’s pardon
questions.question: What will be the outcome of the Lord’s salvation? (vv21–23) How does the poetic imagery help us understand the outcome?
questions.answer: The three Rs: Remember (v21 — ‘return to me, for I have redeemed you’), Return (v22 — ‘I have swept away your offences like a cloud, your sins like the morning mist’), Rejoice (v23 — all creation bursting into song because the Lord has redeemed Jacob). The cloud imagery is striking: sins swept away like the morning mist — gone completely, leaving no trace.
questions.leaderNote: The three Rs (Remember, Return, Rejoice) make a simple and memorable structure for the second half of this session. They are also the shape of repentance and renewal: remembering who we are and who God is, returning to him, and finding that his pardon produces genuine joy.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: How did Israel fail in this passage and how might we fail in the same way? How does the way the Lord responded to Israel’s sin give us a pattern for the Lord’s dealings throughout history? How does this passage point us to Jesus? What responses to God’s salvation are encouraged?
questions.answer: Israel failed through blindness to the Lord’s word, neglect of his worship, and self-deception leading into idolatry — all perennial human failures. The Lord’s response follows a consistent pattern: diagnosis of failure → discipline → grace and redemption → call to remember, return, and rejoice. Jesus fulfils the pattern: he is the Servant who does perfectly what Israel failed to do, and he is the one in whom Israel’s experience of redemption finds its ultimate meaning (‘I have swept away your offences’ — grounded in Christ’s atoning work). The responses encouraged: remember who you are and who God is (v21); return to the Lord, knowing that sins are swept away like mist (v22); rejoice with all creation (v23).
questions.leaderNote: The question of functional idolatry (what is the ‘log’ that we use for both firewood and worship?) is worth pressing into specifically. The idol polemic of 44:9–20 is not ultimately about wooden carvings but about the human tendency to find security, identity, and meaning in things made by our own hands or imaginations.

chapters.title: Isaiah 44:24–47:15 — I Am the Lord, and There Is No Other
chapters.bigIdea: I am the Lord, and there is no other.

background.body:
Read the passage in two chunks: 44:24–45:25 first, then 46:1–47:15. While reading the first passage, ask the group to look out for any striking repetition. The big idea — ‘I am the Lord, and there is no other’ — should emerge from the group’s own observation, so don’t announce it at the start.

‘I am the Lord, and there is no other.’ This declaration appears six times in 45:5–22 (vv5, 6, 14, 18, 21, 22). It is the theological heartbeat of the whole passage and one of the clearest monotheistic statements in the OT.

Cyrus (44:28; 45:1). The naming of Cyrus over 150 years before he was born is one of the most remarkable predictions in Scripture — the central evidence the Lord presents for his uniqueness. Cyrus the Great overthrew Babylon in 539 BC and decreed the return of the exiles (Ezra 1:1‑4).

The potter and the clay (45:9–11). The charge of 45:9 — the clay questioning the potter — recurs in Romans 9:20–21, where Paul applies it to human questioning of the Lord’s sovereign purposes in election.

Babylon’s downfall (ch 47). The shocking element of chapter 47 is that Babylon, after all the declarations of ‘I am the Lord, and there is no other,’ uses exactly the same language of itself: ‘I am, and there is none besides me’ (vv8, 10). This is blasphemy — a human city claiming the unique divine prerogative. Its downfall is the inevitable consequence.

keyPoints.body:
‘I am the Lord, and there is no other.’ Help the group notice this repeated declaration (45:5, 6, 14, 18, 21, 22) and feel its cumulative weight. This is not a theological proposition to be filed away but the most fundamental claim about reality that can be made.

The Lord controls history — including the rise of Cyrus. The naming of Cyrus is evidence for the Lord’s uniqueness. His purposes are not limited by the willingness of his instruments to recognise him.

The clay cannot question the potter. The pride that leads people to question the Lord’s sovereign choices is the pride of the clay questioning the potter. The Lord does not owe an explanation for his sovereign purposes.

The Lord carries his people; they have to carry their idols. The contrast of 46:1–7: Babylon’s gods (Bel and Nebo) must be loaded onto weary animals and carried — they are a burden. The Lord carries his people from birth to old age. The direction of carrying is everything.

Babylon’s pride is its downfall. Babylon claims for itself the very language that belongs to the Lord alone. This is not merely arrogance but ontological confusion — claiming to be what only the Lord can be. Its downfall is swift and total.

exegetical.body:
‘His anointed’ (45:1). The word translated ‘anointed’ is mashiach — Messiah. Cyrus is called the Lord’s Messiah. This does not make Cyrus a messianic figure in the full theological sense, but it is striking: the Lord anoints pagan kings for his purposes.

‘I summon you by name and bestow on you a title of honour, though you do not acknowledge me’ (45:4). The Lord uses Cyrus for his purposes even though Cyrus does not know the Lord. One of the clearest statements of the Lord’s sovereignty over those who do not acknowledge him.

Every knee will bow (45:23). Paul quotes this in Romans 14:11 and Philippians 2:10–11, applying it to Jesus. The universal acknowledgment of the Lord — which Isaiah sees as a future eschatological reality — is for Paul fulfilled in the universal Lordship of Jesus.

Bel and Nebo (46:1). Bel (Marduk) was the chief god of Babylon; Nebo (Nabu) was the god of wisdom, associated with Nebuchadnezzar’s name. Both will be loaded onto animals and carried away — their processional pride turned into the humiliation of captivity.

‘I am, and there is none besides me’ (47:8, 10). The exact words the Lord uses of himself (‘I am the Lord, and there is no other’) are used by Babylon of itself. This is the literary climax: Babylon’s pride is specifically blasphemous — claiming the divine name.

pastorals.body:
The declaration ‘I am the Lord, and there is no other’ can feel like an abstract theological statement. Help the group feel its pastoral weight: it means that the powers they feared (Babylon, the Assyrians, the idols) are nothing; it means that the Lord’s purposes cannot be frustrated; it means that trust in him is the only rational response to reality.

The ‘carrying’ contrast of 46:1–7 is worth dwelling on practically. Ask the group: what do you carry? What carries you? The answer diagnoses the direction of your trust. Things that are functionally idolatrous are things we carry — they are a burden. The Lord is the one who carries his people.

watch.body: Watch for the group treating the Cyrus prophecy as primarily an apologetics argument (proof that the Bible predicts the future) rather than a pastoral comfort (the Lord who knows the end from the beginning has not abandoned his people). The prophecy’s primary function is to assure the exiles that the Lord’s purposes for them are on track.

questions.header: 44:24–45:25: No other god
questions.question: What repetition did you notice while reading 44:24–45:25? What big point is being made? What do we learn about the only God in 44:24–28 and 45:1‑8?
questions.answer:
‘I am the Lord, and there is no other’ — declared in 45:5, 6, 14, 18, 21, 22. The Lord declares his absolute uniqueness. Everything else in the passage flows from this single, foundational claim.

44:24–28: He is the Creator of the heavens and earth; only his word is meaningful; false prophets are nothing; the Lord rules the world and his word will come to pass — naming Cyrus and commissioning the rebuilding of Jerusalem centuries before it happens.

45:1‑8: He works in history to break down obstacles for Cyrus, acting for the sake of Israel (v4) so that people from east to west will know that he is the Lord (v6). Both the sovereign of history and the one whose purposes are ultimately redemptive.

questions.header: 44:24–45:25: No other god
questions.question: What attitude is described in 45:9‑11? How does the imagery highlight the Lord’s response? What promises does the Lord make in vv14–17? What is the correct response to the Lord and why? (vv22–25)
questions.answer:
Arrogance and pride — the clay questioning the potter about what it is being made is absurd. Only the Lord has the right to be God. Humans cannot question him on his use of instruments.

He is Israel’s Lord and Maker, who knows what is best. He will act through Cyrus to restore them (v13). He will honour his people (v14). He is the Saviour who will save them eternally (v17).

The correct response: ‘Turn to me and be saved, all you ends of the earth; for I am God, and there is no other’ (v22). He is the judge; every knee will bow (v23). Only in the Lord will all the offspring of Israel be found righteous.

questions.header: 46:1‑13: The idols are helpless but the Lord carries and sustains his people
questions.question: What repetition do you notice in 46:1–7? What point is being made? How does the Lord summarise his character and purpose in vv8–13?
questions.answer:
Idolaters have to carry their gods on beasts of burden, tiring the animals. The idol can do nothing for itself or for those who worship it. But the Lord carries his people from birth to old age — he created them, carries and sustains them, and will rescue them. The direction of carrying is the key difference.

There is none like the Lord. He has laid out his purposes from the beginning and will do what he has said. He is bringing his righteousness near and will grant salvation to his people (v13). His track record of fulfilling his word — demonstrated by the naming of Cyrus — is the ground of their confidence.

questions.header: 47:1‑15: The fall of Babylon
questions.question: Why is chapter 47 shocking in the light of chapters 45–46? What is going to happen to Babylon? Why is the Lord going to judge Babylon? How does the Lord show Babylon to be helpless?
questions.answer:
Shocking because the declaration ‘I am the Lord, and there is no other’ is echoed by Babylon: ‘I am, and there is none besides me’ (vv8, 10). This is blasphemy — a human city claiming the unique divine prerogative. Babylon will go from queen to widow, from power to desolation.

Reasons for judgment: showed no mercy to God’s people (v6); proud and overconfident (v8); trusted in herself and claimed the position of God (vv8–10); trusted in wickedness and wealth and thought she would not be held accountable (v10).

Helpless: her spells, wisdom, and astrologers are worthless (vv12–14). They cannot save her.
questions.leaderNote: The parallel between ‘I am the Lord, and there is no other’ and Babylon’s ‘I am, and there is none besides me’ is the literary climax of the whole section. Help the group see this echo — it is the most precise diagnosis of Babylon’s sin: not merely pride but specifically claiming the divine name.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: Look again at 45:23–25 and compare it to Romans 14:11 and Philippians 2:10. What does this tell us about Jesus? How does the emphasis of these chapters help us understand the character of Jesus better? How did the Lord want Israel to respond to ‘I am the Lord, and there is no other’? How should we respond?
questions.answer:
Romans 14:11 and Philippians 2:10 apply the universal acknowledgment of the Lord (Isaiah 45:23) to Jesus: every knee will bow to Jesus, every tongue confess that he is Lord. This means that when Isaiah says ‘I am the Lord, and there is no other,’ the NT’s reading is that this uniqueness is expressed in the person of Jesus. The character of Jesus becomes even more astonishing — the one who humbled himself to the cross is the one to whom every knee will bow.

Israel’s response: to stop making idols, to stop trusting in Babylon, to turn to the Lord and be saved (45:22). Our response: the same — turn to Jesus, who embodies the ‘I am the Lord’ of Isaiah, and find in him the only true salvation.
questions.leaderNote: Revelation 18 picks up on and applies Isaiah 47 extensively — the fall of Babylon in Revelation is the eschatological fulfilment of Isaiah’s oracle. It is worth pointing the group to this: the NT does not treat Isaiah 47 as merely historical but as the pattern of all human pride that sets itself against the Lord.

chapters.title: Isaiah 48–50:3 — The Lord Acts for His Name’s Sake and Sends His Servant
chapters.bigIdea: Despite Israel’s stubborn unfaithfulness, the Lord acts for his name’s sake and sends his Servant to bring salvation to the ends of the earth.

background.body:
This session is designed around the handout exercise in the PDF, which asks a series of reflective questions on the two sections (48:1–11; 48:12–22; 49:1‑7; 49:8–50:3) in sequence.

Chapter 48: Israel’s stubbornness and the Lord’s patience. Chapter 48 is one of the most challenging in Isaiah. The Lord addresses a people who call themselves by the name of Israel, who take oaths by the Lord’s name, but whose hearts are not right before him (vv1–2). They are obstinate and their necks are iron (v4). Despite this, the Lord has been acting on their behalf and will continue to do so — not because of their faithfulness but for his own name’s sake (vv9–11).

The delay of judgment (48:9). ‘For my own name’s sake I delay my wrath; for the sake of my praise I hold it back from you, so as not to destroy you.’ The Lord does not delay judgment because Israel deserves it but because his own name and glory are bound up with Israel’s survival and restoration. Echoed in the NT (cf. Romans 2:4; 2 Peter 3:9).

The second Servant Song (49:1‑6). Here the Servant speaks in the first person, addressing the nations directly. His mission is expanding beyond Israel to the whole world: ‘I will also make you a light for the Gentiles, that my salvation may reach to the ends of the earth’ (v6). Luke 2:29–32 (Simeon’s song) and Acts 13:47 (Paul’s mission to the Gentiles) both quote this verse.

The restoration of 49:8–50:3. Despite the Servant’s apparent failure (49:4: ‘I have laboured in vain’) and Israel’s sense of abandonment (49:14: ‘The Lord has forsaken me’), the Lord promises extraordinary restoration — the scattered will be gathered, even if a mother could forget her nursing child, the Lord will not forget his people (49:15).

keyPoints.body:
Israel’s stubbornness does not thwart the Lord’s purposes. Chapter 48 is brutally honest about Israel’s condition: obstinate, neck an iron sinew, forehead bronze (v4). And yet the Lord acts — for his own name’s sake, not theirs.

The ground of God’s patience is his own name and glory. 48:9–11 is theologically important: the Lord delays his wrath to protect his own reputation. This is not selfishness on God’s part but the deepest possible assurance: his commitment to them is as secure as his commitment to his own glory.

The Servant’s mission goes beyond Israel to the nations. The expansion in 49:6 — from restoring Israel to being a light for the Gentiles — is one of the most significant moments in Isaiah. Luke and Acts both quote this verse in the context of Jesus’s birth and Paul’s mission.

‘Can a mother forget the baby at her breast?’ (49:15). The Lord’s response to Israel’s complaint is one of the most tender in Scripture. Even if a mother could forget her nursing child, ‘I will not forget you!’ The grounding in v16 — ‘I have engraved you on the palms of my hands’ — is permanent and indelible.

The restoration goes far beyond the return from exile. The promises of 49:8–50:3 exceed what the return from Babylon accomplished. They point to something more eternal: the gathering of all God’s people from every nation into his presence.

exegetical.body:
The stubborn people (48:1‑4). They invoke the Lord’s name and take oaths by the God of Israel — but not in truth or righteousness (v1). The Lord announced things in advance so they couldn’t give credit to their idols (vv5‑6) — yet still they did not listen (v8).

‘For my own name’s sake’ (48:9–11). The phrase occurs twice within three verses. The Lord’s patience is entirely grounded in his own character and reputation, not in Israel’s merit. Simultaneously humbling (Israel contributes nothing) and reassuring (the Lord’s commitment is as stable as his own character).

‘Oh, that you had paid attention to my commands!’ (48:18). The subjunctive of grief — what might have been. If Israel had listened, their peace would have been like a river (v18). The contrast between the actual and the possible is painful.

The Servant’s complaint (49:4). ‘I have laboured in vain; I have spent my strength for nothing at all.’ The Servant’s most vulnerable moment. But the response is immediate: his cause is with the Lord, his reward is with God (v4b). This anticipates Gethsemane and the cross.

‘I have engraved you on the palms of my hands’ (49:16). The image of engraving — cutting permanently into a hard surface — is the strongest possible image of permanence. Israel’s name is carved into the Lord’s hands; he cannot look at his hands without seeing Israel.

pastorals.body:
Chapter 48’s description of Israel’s stubbornness is uncomfortable and should be. The group may recognise the pattern of formally correct religion that lacks genuine heart engagement. The pastoral move from the honesty of the diagnosis to the grace of the Lord’s patience (48:9–11) requires both to be held without rushing from one to the other.

The complaint of 49:14 — ‘The Lord has forsaken me, the Lord has forgotten me’ — is one of the most honest expressions of spiritual desolation in Scripture. Some in the group may be living in this experience. The Lord’s answer (vv15–16) is not a rebuke but a reassurance of extraordinary tenderness. Give it the attention it deserves.

watch.body: Watch for the group treating the Servant Song of 49:1‑6 as a repeat of 42:1–4 without noticing what is new. The key new element is the expansion of the Servant’s mission to the Gentiles: ‘It is too small a thing for you to be my servant to restore the tribes of Jacob’ (v6). This is one of the most important statements in Isaiah — the scope of the Servant’s mission is nothing less than the ends of the earth.

questions.header: 48:1‑11: Israel’s stubbornness and the Lord’s patience
questions.question: What are God’s people like in these verses? How has the Lord been interacting with them? Why has the Lord been delaying judgment? What are the implications of this for God’s character? Do we see this reiterated in the NT?
questions.answer:
They call themselves by the name of Israel and take oaths by the Lord’s name — but not in truth or righteousness (vv1–2). They are obstinate with an iron sinew in their neck (v4). The Lord has been telling them things in advance so they couldn’t give credit to idols (vv5‑6). Despite all his revelation, they have not heard or understood — they are treacherous rebels from birth (v8).

For his own name’s sake (vv9, 11). Not because Israel deserves patience but because the Lord’s reputation is bound up with Israel’s survival. The implications: God’s patience is as stable as his own character and glory — it does not depend on human merit. In the NT: Romans 2:4 (the kindness of God is meant to lead to repentance); 2 Peter 3:9 (the Lord is patient, not wanting anyone to perish).

questions.header: 48:12‑22: What the Lord teaches Israel about himself
questions.question: What does the Lord teach Israel about himself in 48:12‑22? How does he promise to act? What should they learn? How does it link to the previous section?
questions.answer: He is the first and the last (v12). He is the Creator who laid the foundations of the earth (v13). He has summoned Cyrus to do his will against Babylon (vv14‑15). He is the Lord their God who teaches what is best and directs their paths (v17). If they had listened, their peace would have been like a river (vv18‑19). They should learn that the Lord who has done all this is the one to be trusted. The link: despite their stubbornness (vv1‑11), the Lord continues to act and reveal himself. His patience (vv9‑11) is matched by his continued engagement (vv12‑22).

questions.header: 49:1‑7: The second Servant Song
questions.question: Isaiah 49:1‑6 is the second Servant Song. How do we see these verses fulfilled in Jesus? How do they respond to the issues of chapter 48? (Cf. Luke 2:29–32; Acts 13:47.)
questions.answer:
The Servant is called before birth (vv1–2). He has apparently laboured in vain (v4a) — but his reward is with the Lord (v4b). And his mission expands: not merely to restore the tribes of Jacob but to be a light for the Gentiles, so that salvation reaches to the ends of the earth (v6).

Fulfilment in Jesus: Simeon quotes v6 in Luke 2:32 at Jesus’s birth as the promise being fulfilled. Paul and Barnabas quote v6 in Acts 13:47 as the basis for the Gentile mission. The Servant’s mission is Jesus’s mission, extended through the church to the ends of the earth.
questions.leaderNote: The expansion from ‘restoring Israel’ to ‘light for the Gentiles’ in v6 is the key new element in this Servant Song. Ask the group: what does it mean that it is ‘too small a thing’ for the Servant just to restore Israel? The scope of the gospel is not national but universal — and the church’s mission is the continuation of this universal Servant mission.

questions.header: 49:8–50:3: The restoration of the Lord’s people
questions.question: How do 49:8–50:3 relate to Israel’s restoration after exile? What clues point to something more eternal? How are the themes picked up in the NT? How does the restoration of the people of God relate to the work of the Servant?
questions.answer:
The restoration described goes beyond the return from Babylon: the Servant is a covenant for the people (v8); prisoners will be released from darkness (v9); people will come from every direction and from Sinim (possibly China) (vv12–13). This is more than the partial return under Cyrus.

Clues pointing beyond: the scope (ends of the earth), the tenderness of 49:15–16 (the Lord will not forget his people — ‘I have engraved you on the palms of my hands’), the promise that the Lord’s salvation will not be frustrated (50:2–3).

NT themes: the Servant as covenant for the people (the new covenant in Jesus’s blood, Luke 22:20; Hebrews 8:6); the release of captives (Luke 4:18 quoting Isaiah 61:1); the gathering of all nations (Revelation 7:9). The restoration is entirely the work of the Servant.
questions.leaderNote: The image of v16 — ‘I have engraved you on the palms of my hands’ — has been applied by many readers to the wounds of Christ. Whether or not that connection is intended in Isaiah, the image of God’s permanent, indelible commitment to his people written in his hands is theologically profound.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: What does today’s passage teach us about the character of God and how he works to save his people? What difference did that make to Israel then, and to us now? How does today’s passage help us understand the ministry of Christ better? What response is called for?
questions.answer:
God’s character: patient beyond deserving (for his name’s sake), faithful to his purposes even when his people are stubborn, tender in his commitment (‘I have engraved you on the palms of my hands’), universal in his saving purposes (light for the Gentiles). He saves not because we deserve it but because his name and glory are bound up with our salvation.

For Israel then: assurance that the exile was not the end, that the Lord had not forgotten them, that his purposes were on track. For us: assurance that our failure and stubbornness do not put us outside the Lord’s purposes; that Christ the Servant has fulfilled what we could not; that the Lord’s commitment to us is as permanent as an engraving in stone.

Response: genuine heart engagement rather than merely formal religion (ch 48’s challenge); trust in the Servant’s universal mission and participation in it (49:6’s horizon).

chapters.title: Isaiah 50:4–52:11 — Wake Up! The Lord Is Acting to Save His People
chapters.bigIdea: Wake up! The Lord is acting to save his people. Listen to him and follow his obedient Servant.

background.body:
Read the passage in two chunks: 50:4–51:8 first, then 51:9–52:11. Ask the group to look for striking repetition while reading the first passage.

The third Servant Song (50:4–9). The third of the four Servant Songs. The Servant speaks in the first person about his experience of obedient suffering. He receives the Lord’s word morning by morning (v4), offers his back to those who beat him (v6), and sets his face like flint (v7). The Song ends with the Servant expressing complete confidence in the Lord’s vindication, despite his suffering.

The structure of 51:9–52:11 around three ‘wake up’ calls. (1) The arm of the Lord is called to wake up and act (51:9); (2) Jerusalem is called to wake up from the cup of wrath she has drunk (51:17); (3) Zion, the people of God, is called to wake up and put on strength (52:1). After three calls to others, there is a final call to depart — leave Babylon, go out from exile (52:11). The progression is from the Lord’s activity to Jerusalem’s suffering to Zion’s liberation.

The cup of wrath (51:17, 22). The image of the cup of the Lord’s wrath is one of Isaiah’s most evocative (cf. Jeremiah 25:15–29). Jerusalem has drunk it to its dregs. But the Lord promises to take it from her hand and give it to her tormentors. In the NT, Jesus speaks of drinking the cup in Gethsemane (Matthew 26:39) — he drains the cup of God’s judgment as the obedient Servant who sets his face like flint.

‘How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of those who bring good news’ (52:7). This climactic verse is quoted by Paul in Romans 10:15 as the basis for the Gentile mission. The proclamation of the gospel is the continuation of this declaration: ‘Your God reigns!’

keyPoints.body:
The Servant’s obedience is active, not passive. In 50:4‑9, the Servant does not merely endure suffering — he actively offers himself. ‘I offered my back to those who beat me, my cheeks to those who pulled out my beard’ (v6). His suffering is an act of obedience and trust in the Lord’s vindication. This anticipates the cross as an act of willing self-offering.

Fear the Lord; do not fear mere mortals. The call of 50:10–11 and 51:7 is to choose who to listen to — the Lord and his Servant, or men who will soon be gone. The Servant’s confidence in the Lord despite his suffering is the model for the people of God.

The Lord will wake up and act. The first ‘wake up’ call (51:9) is an appeal to the Lord himself to act with the strength he has shown before — in defeating the chaos-monster Rahab, in the Exodus crossing of the sea. He is being called to do again what he has done before. And the promise (51:11 — a word-for-word repeat of 35:10) is that the redeemed will return with singing.

The news of 52:7 is the announcement that God reigns. The gospel is not primarily a message about human benefit but an announcement of who rules: ‘Your God reigns!’ Paul reads the Gentile mission through this lens (Romans 10:15). The beauty is in the feet of those who go.

The Servant’s obedience is the basis for Zion’s liberation. The connection between 50:4‑9 (the Servant’s willing suffering) and 52:7–11 (the liberation of Zion) is not stated but is theologically essential. The liberation of God’s people is accomplished through the Servant’s obedient suffering. This prepares the ground for the full statement of 52:13–53:12 in the next session.

exegetical.body:
‘He wakens me morning by morning, wakens my ear to listen like one being instructed’ (50:4). The Servant’s obedience is rooted in daily attention to the Lord’s word. He does not act from his own initiative but from faithful, sustained listening. This is the foundation of his mission and his endurance.

‘I set my face like flint’ (50:7). An expression of absolute, unshakeable determination. The Servant knows the outcome (the Lord will vindicate him) and this knowledge enables him to face anything without flinching. Luke 9:51 uses the same image of Jesus: ‘he resolutely set out for Jerusalem.’

The Abraham appeal (51:1–3). The call to ‘look to the rock from which you were cut’ is a call to remember Abraham. He was just one man; Sarah was just one woman; yet the Lord blessed them and made a great nation from them. He can easily bless his seemingly barren and ruined people in the same way. He can bring joy from sorrow and life from death.

51:11 as a verbatim repeat of 35:10. The promise that ‘the ransomed of the Lord will return. They will enter Zion with singing; everlasting joy will crown their heads’ appears word for word in both 35:10 and 51:11. The repetition is deliberate and significant: the great promise of chapter 35 is being recalled and reaffirmed at the climax of this section. The Lord’s word stands.

‘How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of those who bring good news’ (52:7). The Hebrew word translated ‘good news’ is bissar, the root of the Greek euangelion (gospel). This is the proclamation of the gospel in seed form: peace, good news, salvation, the reign of God. Paul quotes it in Romans 10:15 as the basis for the sending of preachers to the Gentiles.

pastorals.body:
The third Servant Song (50:4‑9) is one of the most personally revealing passages in Isaiah. The Servant’s experience — daily dependence on the Lord’s word, willing submission to suffering, unshakeable confidence in the Lord’s vindication — is a direct challenge to the group’s own spiritual life. How does my daily pattern of listening to the Lord’s word compare to the Servant’s morning-by-morning wakefulness? Give the group time to sit with this before pressing to the ‘wake up’ calls of the second half.

The image of Jerusalem having drunk the cup of wrath to its dregs (51:17–23) may resonate powerfully with those who have experienced what feels like the Lord’s absence or discipline. The promise that the Lord will take the cup from her hand is not a denial of her suffering but a declaration that it will end. This is pastoral gold for those in seasons of sustained difficulty.

watch.body: Watch for the group treating the three ‘wake up’ calls of 51:9–52:11 as three separate unrelated sections. The progression is deliberate: the Lord wakes up and acts (51:9) → Jerusalem wakes up and is liberated from the cup (51:17) → Zion wakes up and puts on strength (52:1) → the call to depart (52:11). The movement is from divine initiative to human response. Help the group trace this arc.

questions.header: 50:4–11: Who will you follow, the Lord’s obedient Servant or yourself?
questions.question: What relationship does the Servant have to the Sovereign Lord in v4? What does listening to the Lord result in for the Servant? (vv5‑6) How does the Servant’s knowledge of the Sovereign Lord help him? (vv7‑9) How do we see this applied to Jesus in the NT?
questions.answer:
The Servant listens to and is taught by the Sovereign Lord morning by morning, and this enables him to teach and sustain others. He is utterly dependent on the Lord’s daily word — and that dependence is the source of his strength, not a weakness.

Listening results in obedient suffering. Notice how active the Servant is: he offers himself, does not hide from it. He is not merely a victim but a willing participant in his own suffering.

He knows the ultimate outcome — the Lord will vindicate him — so he is able to endure whatever happens. He trusts the Lord’s judgment, not men’s. His adversaries will eventually be gone.

Fulfilled in Jesus: Luke 9:51 uses the same ‘set his face’ image (cf. Isaiah 50:7) as Jesus resolutely set out for Jerusalem. The whole pattern of voluntary, obedient suffering followed by vindication is the pattern of the cross and resurrection.

questions.header: 50:4‑11: Who will you follow, the Lord’s obedient Servant or yourself?
questions.question: What does fearing the Lord look like? (v10) What two options and two outcomes are offered? (vv10‑11)
questions.answer:
Fearing the Lord looks like obeying the Servant. There is a close connection made here between obeying the Lord and obeying his Servant — they are not two different things. Do you want to fear the Lord? Then listen to Jesus, his Servant who is characterised by obedient suffering.

Two options: (1) recognise your darkness and turn to the Lord and live in trust and reliance on him; (2) trust in and rely on your own light and receive ultimate torment from him. Darkness with trust in the Lord is better than all the manmade fires that give an illusion of light but only lead to deeper darkness.

questions.header: 51:1‑8: Who will you listen to, the Lord or mere mortals?
questions.question: This section is structured around three calls to listen (vv1, 4, 7) and two calls to look (vv1–2, 6). Who is being addressed in each call? What instruction does the Lord give and why? (vv1‑2, 4‑6, 7‑8)
questions.answer:
v1‑2: Those who pursue righteousness and seek the Lord. They are told to remember Abraham — one man, one barren woman, yet the Lord blessed them and made a great nation. He can easily bless his seemingly barren and ruined people in the same way. He can bring joy from sorrow and life from death.

v4‑6: The people of God. The Lord’s instruction, justice, and salvation is coming not only for Israel but for nations. The created order — which seems so solid and permanent — will vanish like smoke. But the Lord’s salvation, which they can’t yet see, is what is really permanent and lasting.

v7‑8: Those who know what is right and have taken the Lord’s instruction to heart. Don’t be afraid of mere mortals — they will soon be gone like moth-eaten clothes. The Lord’s salvation is what will truly last.

questions.header: 51:9–52:11: Wake up! God’s salvation is coming and his judgment is finished
questions.question: Who is being woken up in v9? Why? What is he being woken up to do? What does the Lord teach them about himself and why is this the antidote to fear of men? (vv12–16)
questions.answer:
The arm of the Lord. This is a call from the people to the Lord himself to act with the strength he has shown throughout history — defeating the chaos-monster Rahab, bringing his people safely through the sea (the Exodus). The Lord is being called to do again what he has done before.

The Lord is the Creator of the whole universe. He controls everything in the created order. Oppressors may terrify them but they are nothing compared to the Lord. He will redeem and release his suffering and imprisoned people. The antidote to fear of men is knowing who truly rules.

questions.header: 51:9–52:11: Wake up! God’s salvation is coming and his judgment is finished
questions.question: Who is being woken up in v17? What has happened to them? What is the Lord going to do? (vv21–23)
questions.answer:
Jerusalem. They have experienced the Lord’s judgment to the full — they have drunk the cup of his wrath to its dregs (v17). They have lost children, they have experienced calamity, they have had no one to comfort them. They have known ruin, destruction, famine, and the sword. They have been trapped under the judgment of the Lord.

The Lord is going to remove his judgment from them and judge their oppressors instead (vv21–23). The cup of wrath will be taken from Jerusalem’s hand and given to those who tormented her.

questions.header: 51:9–52:11: Wake up! God’s salvation is coming and his judgment is finished
questions.question: Who is being woken up in 52:1? What connection do you notice with 51:9? What imagery is used and what does it tell us about what the Lord will do? How do vv7‑10 climax the section?
questions.answer:
Zion, the people of God. First the arm of the Lord was clothed with strength (51:9); now the people of the Lord are called to clothe themselves with strength (52:1). A woman who has been a captive, covered in dust, is throwing off her chains and being clothed in splendour. All enemies will be sent away for ever.

Verses 7‑10 are the climax: ‘How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of those who bring good news, who proclaim peace, who bring good tidings, who proclaim salvation, who say to Zion, “Your God reigns!”’ The watchmen see the return of the Lord to Zion. The ruins of Jerusalem burst into song. The Lord has comforted his people and redeemed Jerusalem. The whole world will see his salvation.
questions.leaderNote: Romans 10:15 quotes 52:7 as the basis for the sending of preachers. The ‘good news’ (bissar) is the gospel in seed form: peace, good news, salvation, the reign of God. The beauty is in the feet of those who go. This is a good moment to make the connection between Isaiah’s vision and the church’s mission explicit.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: What difference does it make to us to read about the Servant? How does it encourage us to listen to Jesus? Why is 52:10 both a challenge and a comfort? Much of Isaiah is addressed to the faithful remnant as they faced coming judgment and exile. What truths about the Lord in today’s passage would help them not be afraid of ‘mere mortals’? How does this apply to us, this side of the Cross?
questions.answer:
The Servant models daily dependence on the Lord’s word, willing obedience even to suffering, and unshakeable confidence in the Lord’s vindication. This is a direct challenge to our own patterns of spiritual life and response to difficulty. Listening to Jesus means learning the Servant’s pattern.

52:10 — ‘the Lord will lay bare his holy arm in the sight of all the nations, and all the ends of the earth will see the salvation of our God’ — is both a comfort (the Lord’s salvation is certain and will be seen by all) and a challenge (are we participating in the proclamation that makes it visible?).

Truths that help: the Lord is the Creator who controls the created order (51:12–16); he has acted before in Exodus-power and will act again; the cup of wrath will be removed; the Lord’s salvation is permanent while human power is as temporary as moth-eaten clothes. For us this side of the cross: Christ has drunk the cup; the Lord’s arm has been laid bare in the resurrection; the good news of ‘your God reigns’ is the gospel we have been given to proclaim.
questions.leaderNote: The question about what it looks like to proclaim ‘your God reigns!’ (52:7) in a twenty-first century context is worth pressing into concretely. What does this announcement look like for the group in their specific setting?

chapters.title: Isaiah 52:13–53:12 — The Servant Suffers to Justify Many and Is Exalted by the Lord
chapters.bigIdea: The Servant suffers to justify many and is exalted by the Lord.

background.body:
This is one of the most important and theologically rich passages in the entire Bible. Spend more time on this session than on others. Read the whole passage slowly, perhaps twice, before beginning the questions.

The chiastic structure. The passage is structured as a chiasm — a journey there and back again: (A) the Servant’s glory (52:13–15) → (B) his rejection (53:1–3) → (C) his suffering on our behalf (53:4–6, the central section) → (B’) his innocent suffering (53:7–9) → (A’) his exaltation (53:10–12). The central section (vv4–6) is therefore the main point: the Servant suffers on our behalf.

The New Testament’s use of this passage. Isaiah 52:13–53:12 is the most extensively quoted OT passage in the NT. It underlies almost every major atonement text. The handout lists key NT quotations and applications: Matthew 8:16–17; 27:57–60; Mark 14:60–61; Luke 22:37; 23:13–15; John 1:10–11; 12:38; Acts 8:32–33; Romans 10:16; 15:21; Hebrews 7:25; 1 Peter 2:22–25.

The question the original hearers faced. For Isaiah’s original hearers, this passage would have been deeply puzzling. The Servant who brings justice to the nations (42:1–4), who is a light for the Gentiles (49:6), who sets his face like flint (50:7) — how could this Servant be crushed and killed? The answer the passage gives: this is precisely how he accomplishes his mission. Suffering is not the defeat of the Servant’s mission but its fulfilment.

The handout. The PDF includes a handout for this session listing NT references and asking three questions: what the passage might have meant to original hearers; how the NT applies it; and whether anything is still awaiting fulfilment. These are reproduced in question 7 below.

keyPoints.body:
The pattern is glory → suffering → glory. The chiastic structure makes this clear. The passage begins and ends with the Servant’s exaltation; the suffering in the middle is framed by glory. This is the pattern of the cross and resurrection.

The Servant’s suffering is vicarious — on behalf of others. The central section (53:4–6) is explicit: ‘he took up our pain and bore our suffering’ (v4); ‘he was pierced for our transgressions, he was crushed for our iniquities’ (v5); ‘the Lord has laid on him the iniquity of us all’ (v6). The substitutionary nature of the Servant’s suffering is unmistakable.

The Servant suffers in silence and willingly. ‘He was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth; he was led like a lamb to the slaughter’ (v7). This is not passive resignation but active cooperation with the Lord’s purposes, consistent with the willing self-offering of 50:6. His silence is the silence of obedience, not helplessness.

The Servant is innocent. The surprise of v9 — ‘he had done no violence, nor was any deceit in his mouth’ — is that after describing a punishment that would normally indicate guilt, the text insists on his complete innocence. He suffers for others’ sin, not his own.

This is the Lord’s will, and it achieves the Lord’s purposes. Verse 10: ‘Yet it was the Lord’s will to crush him and cause him to suffer’. The suffering is not an accident or a failure but the Lord’s own plan for the accomplishment of salvation. The Servant’s death is a ‘guilt offering’ (v10) — the technical term for the sacrifice that deals with specific sins (cf. Leviticus 5:1–6:7).

exegetical.body:
‘He will be raised and lifted up and highly exalted’ (52:13). The same words used of the Lord in Isaiah 6:1 (‘high and exalted’) are used of the Servant here. The implication is that the Servant’s exaltation is the Lord’s own exaltation. John 12:38–41 quotes 52:13–15 and 53:1 and then says ‘Isaiah said this because he saw Jesus’ glory’ (v41).

‘He will sprinkle many nations’ (52:15). The word ‘sprinkle’ is the same word used for the priestly sprinkling of blood in the temple (cf. Leviticus 14:7; 16:14; Numbers 8:7). The sprinkling that enables people to enter the presence of God is achieved through the Servant’s suffering. This is one of the clearest priestly images in the Servant Songs.

‘Who has believed our message?’ (53:1). The ‘we’ of this verse is Israel, speaking retrospectively. The question — who has believed what we are reporting? — implies that the Servant’s suffering was not what people expected from the Lord’s anointed. John 12:38 and Romans 10:16 both quote this verse in the context of unbelief in response to the proclamation of Jesus.

‘A tender shoot’ and ‘a root out of dry ground’ (53:2). These images of humble, unpromising origins pick up the imagery of 11:1 (the shoot from the stump of Jesse). The Servant does not come with an impressive appearance that would attract attention. There is nothing in his external presentation that would cause people to desire him.

‘The punishment that brought us peace was on him’ (53:5). The Hebrew word translated ‘peace’ is shalom — wholeness, completeness, flourishing. The Servant’s punishment achieves not merely the cessation of hostility but the full restoration of human flourishing. ‘By his wounds we are healed’ — 1 Peter 2:24 quotes this directly.

The guilt offering (53:10). The word translated ‘guilt offering’ (asham) refers specifically to the sacrifice prescribed in Leviticus 5:1–6:7 for sins committed in ignorance or through unfaithfulness. Its purpose is to make amends for the offence and restore the relationship. The Servant’s death is the ultimate ‘asham’ — the definitive sacrifice that deals with all human sin.

pastorals.body:
This passage deserves more time than any other in the study. If possible, begin the session by reading the whole passage aloud slowly — perhaps the leader reading it to the group, or taking turns with single verses. Let the weight of the language land before pressing into the questions.

The central claim of the passage — that the Servant’s suffering is on behalf of others, for their sins, at the Lord’s own initiative — is simultaneously the most comforting and most demanding truth in the Bible. It is comforting because it means sin is fully dealt with; it is demanding because it exposes what sin actually cost. Help the group sit with both dimensions.

Some in the group may be very familiar with the passage and the risk is that familiarity dulls the sense of wonder. Read specific phrases slowly and ask the group to say in their own words what they mean. ‘He was pierced for our transgressions’ — what does ‘pierced’ mean here? What does ‘for’ mean? ‘The punishment that brought us peace was on him’ — what kind of peace? What punishment? Slow the group down.

watch.body: Watch for the group treating this passage as primarily a proof-text for substitutionary atonement rather than as a sustained piece of poetry to be read, felt, and marvelled at. The doctrine of substitution is in the text — but so is the astonishment of vv13–15 (kings shut their mouths), the tenderness of vv4‑6 (he took up our pain), and the triumph of vv10–12 (he will see the light of life and be satisfied). Give the whole passage room to breathe.

handout.body:
Use the following questions to explore the passage together:

1. What might this passage have meant to the original hearers? For Isaiah’s first hearers, the suffering Servant would have been deeply puzzling — they would have expected the Lord’s anointed to come in power. The passage reframes the expectation: the Servant’s suffering is the means of achieving what no powerful leader could achieve.

2. How does the NT apply this passage? The following references are worth working through:

NT ReferenceWhat aspect of the passage it applies
Matthew 8:16–17Jesus’s healing ministry as the fulfilment of ‘he took up our infirmities’ (53:4)
Matthew 27:57–60Jesus buried in a rich man’s tomb — fulfilment of 53:9
Mark 14:60–61Jesus’s silence before the Sanhedrin — fulfilment of 53:7
Luke 22:37Jesus quotes 53:12 (‘numbered with the transgressors’) and says it is being fulfilled in him
Luke 23:13–15Pilate’s declaration that Jesus is innocent — fulfilment of 53:9
John 1:10–11The world did not recognise him; his own did not receive him — 53:1–3
John 12:38Unbelief in response to Jesus quoted from 53:1
Acts 8:32–33Philip explains to the Ethiopian eunuch that 53:7–8 refers to Jesus
Romans 10:16The proclamation of the gospel meets with unbelief — quoted from 53:1
Romans 15:21Paul’s Gentile mission — those who were not told about him will see — quoted from 52:15
Hebrews 7:25Jesus intercedes for us — fulfilling 53:12 (‘he bore the sin of many, and made intercession for the transgressors’)
1 Peter 2:22–25The Servant’s innocent suffering as a model and as the means of our healing

3. Is there anything from today’s passage that we are still waiting for the fulfilment of? The full extent of ‘many’ (vv11–12) being justified and knowing the Servant is still unfolding. The Servant’s complete satisfaction (v11 — ‘he will see the light of life and be satisfied’) awaits the final gathering of all his people. We are still in the process of the Servant’s work being proclaimed to the nations (52:15).

questions.header: 52:13–15: The Servant is disfigured but exalted
questions.question: What is the first thing we learn about the Servant in v13? What might we expect ‘act wisely’ to look like? Is what follows a surprise? How does the Servant relate to the ‘many’? (vv14–15) What do you think ‘sprinkle’ refers to?
questions.answer:
The Servant will act wisely and be raised and lifted up and highly exalted (v13) — perhaps we might expect teaching or some kind of wise behaviour resulting in honour from God. But what follows is an account of extreme suffering that is described as shocking and disfiguring.

Many are appalled at him (v14); yet he will sprinkle many nations and kings will shut their mouths because of him (v15). The suffering that results in people being appalled is the very suffering that results in many nations being ‘sprinkled.’ Sprinkling is the priestly act of cleansing that enables people to enter the presence of God (Leviticus 14:7; 16:14; Numbers 8:7). The extreme suffering has an extreme and unexpected result: cleansing for many nations.

questions.header: 53:1‑3: The Servant is rejected
questions.question: Where have we seen the phrase ‘arm of the Lord’ already in Isaiah? What did it refer to there? Who is the ‘arm of the Lord’ here? What clues are there as to why no one recognises that the Servant is the arm of the Lord? How do people respond to him and why?
questions.answer:
51:9 — destroying evil/chaos, redeeming Israel as at the Exodus, rescuing his people. The Servant is the arm of the Lord — the Lord’s own saving power, come in person.

He comes as a tender shoot, from dry ground, without impressive appearance (v2). He is despised, rejected, familiar with suffering, and people hide their faces from him (v3). He doesn’t look like what people expected the arm of the Lord to look like. His humility and suffering make him unrecognisable as the Lord’s power.

questions.header: 53:4‑6: His suffering is on our behalf
questions.question: As briefly as possible, summarise why the Servant suffers. What do we learn about humanity here? What does the Servant experience and what does it result in?
questions.answer:
The Servant suffers for the sins of others. Not for his own sins (v9 will make this clear) but for ours.

Humanity is characterised by: pain, suffering, transgression, iniquity, deserving of punishment, need of healing, straying from the Lord to go our own way (v6). This is a comprehensive diagnosis of the human condition.

The Servant experiences our pain and suffering. He is punished, stricken, and afflicted — and people thought that God was punishing him for his own sin. He is pierced, crushed, and punished for the sins of others. This resulted in ‘our peace’ (with God) and healing by his wounds. The Lord punishes him for ‘the iniquity of us all’ (v6).

questions.header: 53:7‑9: The Servant suffers silently and innocently
questions.question: What is the main point of v7? What does it mean? What clues are there in v8 as to the nature of the Servant’s suffering? What is the surprise of v9?
questions.answer:
The Servant suffers in silence — not that he made no sound, but that he made no protest. Just as we saw in 50:4‑9, his suffering is an act of obedience that he actively co-operated with. He is like a lamb to the slaughter — not struggling, not resisting.

Verse 8: it was a judgment and an execution, and no one tried to stop it from happening. He was cut off from the land of the living and struck for the transgression of his people.

The surprise of v9: we have heard again and again of how the Servant was punished and suffered. Now we learn that he was entirely innocent — he had done no violence, nor was any deceit in his mouth. He suffers for others’ sin, not his own.

questions.header: 53:10–12: The Servant justifies many and is glorified by God
questions.question: How does v10 explain everything we have learned about the Servant’s suffering? What is the pattern of the Servant’s ministry in vv11–12? How does it connect to what we saw in 50:4‑9? How do vv11–12 mark a ‘homecoming’ in the chiastic structure?
questions.answer:
It was the Lord’s will. The Lord sacrificed the Servant as a guilt offering (asham, Leviticus 5:1–6:7). This results in life and prosperity — the will of the Lord will prosper in his hand, bringing about salvation and ongoing life. The suffering is not a defeat but the means of achieving the Lord’s purposes.

The pattern (vv11–12): suffering first, glory later. Life after death (v11a). He is exalted (v12). It echoes the active, willing suffering of 50:4‑9. He pours out his own life as an act of obedient righteousness and is vindicated.

The ‘homecoming’: we started with the Servant acting wisely — here he justifies many by his knowledge. We started with him being exalted — here he has a place among the great because he is exalted by God. We started with him being disfigured — here he poured out his life to death. We started with him sprinkling many nations — here he justifies many by bearing their sin.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: Use the handout to work through: (a) what this passage might have meant to the original hearers; (b) how the NT applies it to Jesus; (c) whether there is anything we are still waiting for. Spend time reflecting on what Jesus has done, what Isaiah 52–53 teaches about the Cross, and what the implications are for us.
questions.answer:
See the Handout section for the full NT reference list. Key points for discussion:

(a) For original hearers: deeply puzzling and reframing. The expected ‘arm of the Lord’ comes in humility and suffering. The means of cleansing for the nations is not conquest but sacrifice.

(b) NT application: the NT consistently applies this passage to Jesus — in his silence, his burial, his innocent suffering, his bearing of sin. Luke 22:37 records Jesus himself quoting 53:12 and saying it is being fulfilled in him. There is no ambiguity about the NT’s reading.

(c) Still waiting for: the full number of the ‘many’ to be justified and to know the Servant; the complete satisfaction of the Servant (v11); the proclamation of his surprising work to all nations (52:15).
questions.leaderNote: This session benefits from an extended closing time of reflection, prayer, and perhaps worship. The passage is so rich that intellectual discussion alone will not do it justice. Help the group move from understanding to adoration.

chapters.title: Isaiah 54–55 — Rejoice! The Lord Will Pardon and Restore His People and Love Them for Ever
chapters.bigIdea: Rejoice! The Lord will pardon and restore his people and love them for ever.

background.body:
Chapters 54–55 are the response to the Servant Song of 52:13–53:12. If chapter 53 is the act of redemption, chapters 54–55 describe the new reality that redemption creates. Chapter 54 uses three images for the people of God to describe their restored relationship with the Lord; chapter 55 issues a threefold invitation to receive the Lord’s grace.

The three images of chapter 54. The barren woman (54:1–3), the rejected wife (54:4–10), and the attacked city (54:11–17) all describe different aspects of Israel’s experience — the shame of childlessness, the grief of rejection, the devastation of exile. And in each case the Lord promises to overturn the situation completely. The images are layered and cumulative: each one adds a new dimension to the picture of God’s restorative love.

The unfailing covenant love (54:10). The climax of chapter 54 is one of the most powerful promises in the whole of Isaiah: ‘Though the mountains be shaken and the hills be removed, yet my unfailing love (hesed) for you will not be shaken nor my covenant of peace be removed, says the Lord, who has compassion on you.’ This is the ultimate ground of the people’s security: not their own faithfulness but the Lord’s unfailing covenant love.

The threefold invitation of chapter 55. Come, eat, and drink (55:1–3) — come to the Lord for true satisfaction. Come, seek, and return (55:6–7) — come in repentance and receive mercy and pardon. The invitation is to everyone who thirsts, who has no money, who is hungry. The salvation being offered is entirely free.

The word of God achieves what it is sent to do (55:10–11). The closing promise of chapter 55 grounds all the preceding invitations in the reliability of the Lord’s word. His word is like rain and snow that water the earth and make it fruitful — it will accomplish what he desires and achieve the purpose for which he sent it. This is why the invitations of the chapter can be trusted: the Lord’s word of promise cannot fail.

keyPoints.body:
The restoration described in chapter 54 exceeds what Israel actually experienced historically. Even after the return from exile, Israel never became so numerous as to overflow the nations (54:3), never possessed the fully restored and bejewelled city (54:11–14), and was attacked again (most notably by Rome in AD 70). These promises point beyond the historical return to what the Lord would do in Christ — bringing many nations into the people of God — and ultimately to the new creation of Revelation 21–22.

The Lord’s unfailing love (hesed) is the ultimate security of his people. The covenant of peace that will not be removed (54:10) is the ground on which everything else stands. Not the people’s faithfulness, not their moral achievement, but the Lord’s own unchanging character. This is what makes the invitations of chapter 55 credible.

The invitation of chapter 55 is radically free and radically inclusive. ‘Come, all you who are thirsty, come to the waters; and you who have no money, come, buy and eat!’ (55:1). The repeated emphasis on ‘without money and without cost’ (v2) is the ground of the gospel’s universality: anyone who is thirsty can come. No qualification required except need.

The Lord’s thoughts and ways are as far above ours as the heavens are above the earth. The promise of 55:8‑9 is both humbling and reassuring. Humbling: we cannot fully comprehend the Lord’s purposes. Reassuring: his purposes are better, higher, and more certain than anything we could plan. His word will achieve what he intends.

The invitation of chapter 55 is the outworking of the Servant’s work. The free forgiveness of 55:7 (‘he will freely pardon’) is possible because the Servant bore the sin of many (53:12). The covenant of peace of 54:10 is grounded in the punishment that brought peace (53:5). Chapters 54–55 cannot be read apart from chapter 53.

exegetical.body:
The barren woman (54:1‑3). In the ancient world, barrenness was a source of great shame. The Lord is about to reverse this completely: the barren woman will have more children than the married woman. The imagery echoes Sarah (Genesis 17:15–19; 21:1‑7) and points forward to the expansion of the people of God beyond the boundaries of natural Israel. Paul applies this verse in Galatians 4:27 to the inclusion of the Gentiles in the covenant community.

‘Your Maker is your husband’ (54:5). The Lord is described as both Maker and husband of his people. This is one of the most intimate descriptions of the covenant relationship in the whole OT. The people of God are not merely servants or subjects but beloved spouse. The rejection of v6 and the restoration of vv7–10 are therefore not just changes in circumstance but the restoration of the most intimate personal relationship.

The covenant of peace (54:10). The Hebrew word shalom (peace) here carries its full OT weight: not just the absence of conflict but wholeness, flourishing, completeness. The Lord’s covenant of shalom will not be removed — even if the mountains (the most stable things in creation) are shaken. The covenant is more stable than creation itself.

‘All your children will be taught by the Lord’ (54:13). Jesus quotes this verse in John 6:45 in the context of explaining who can come to him: ‘Everyone who has heard the Father and learned from him comes to me.’ The promise that all God’s children will be taught by him is fulfilled in the teaching ministry of Jesus and the Spirit.

‘Why spend money on what is not bread?’ (55:2). The rhetorical question exposes the futility of seeking satisfaction in what cannot satisfy. Food that is not bread, labour that yields no satisfaction — the contrast is with what the Lord offers: ‘listen to me, and eat what is good, and you will delight in the richest of fare.’ Jesus picks up this imagery in John 6:35 (‘I am the bread of life’).

‘As the rain and the snow come down from heaven’ (55:10–11). The agricultural imagery is precise and apt: rain and snow come down, water the earth, make it bud and flourish, and then return to their source. They do not return without accomplishing their purpose. The Lord’s word is like this — it will not return to him empty. This is one of the strongest assertions of the effectiveness of the Lord’s word in the whole OT.

pastorals.body:
Chapters 54–55 come immediately after the weight of chapter 53, and they are designed to be relief and joy. Give the group permission to receive them as such. After the sustained exposure to sin, judgment, and suffering in the preceding sessions, the invitation of chapter 55 — ‘Come, everyone who thirsts’ — should land with something like the relief of cold water on a hot day.

The three images of chapter 54 — barren woman, rejected wife, attacked city — may resonate with people in the group who have experienced shame, rejection, or devastation. The Lord’s specific reversals (shame turned to honour, rejection turned to compassionate restoration, ruins turned to a jewelled city) are personal and specific promises. Give space for the group to receive them personally, not just intellectually.

The imperatives of chapter 55 — come, listen, seek, return — are the application of the whole study so far. They are the practical response to everything Isaiah has been saying. Help the group take them seriously as real invitations rather than rhetorical flourishes.

watch.body: Watch for the group moving too quickly through the three images of chapter 54 to get to the invitation of chapter 55. The images are not merely illustrative — they are cumulative, each adding a new dimension to the picture of God’s restorative love. Give each image time to land, and ask the group which one resonates most with them and why.

questions.header: 54:1–17: The Lord overturns the fortunes of his people
questions.question: Use the session handout to work through the three images used in chapter 54 for the people of God (barren woman, 54:1–3; rejected wife, 54:4–10; attacked city, 54:11–17). For each image: how does it apply to Israel? What does the Lord promise? How does each image relate to the work of the Servant?
questions.answer:
Barren woman (54:1‑3): Israel in exile was like a barren woman — few in number, apparently without offspring, shameful by ancient standards. The Lord promises many offspring spreading across the world. The connection to the Servant: the Servant ‘will see his offspring and prolong his days’ (53:10). He is the one through whom the Lord produces offspring for God.

Rejected wife (54:4–10): Israel was like a wife rejected and sent away by her husband, living like a widow. The Lord punished Israel by sending her into exile because of her sin. The Lord promises comfort, restoration, and that his unfailing love will never again be removed — ‘my covenant of peace will not be removed’ (v10). The connection to the Servant: ‘the punishment that brought us peace was upon him’ (53:5). The covenant of peace is grounded in the Servant’s atoning work.

Attacked city (54:11–17): Jerusalem was attacked and destroyed when God’s people went into exile. The Lord promises to restore the city and make it better than before — with foundations of sapphires, battlements of rubies, gates of sparkling jewels. No enemy will be able to overcome it. The connection to the Servant: ‘the punishment that brought us peace was upon him’ (53:5) — the restoration of the city is the fruit of the Servant’s work.

questions.header: 54:1–17: The Lord overturns the fortunes of his people
questions.question: How do we see this played out in the history of Israel? How does Israel’s experience fall short of what is promised here? How then do we see the fulfilment of these things?
questions.answer:
Barren woman: there is still a remnant of the people of God but even in several hundred years they don’t fill the earth as this promises. Rejected wife: the Lord restores Israel after exile and brings her back, but Israel continues to sin. Attacked city: Jerusalem is rebuilt but isn’t better than before, and is attacked again — most notably in AD 70 when destroyed by the Romans.

Fulfilment: ultimately these come about through Jesus, the true Israel, who brings many nations into the people of God. The Lord comforts his people through Jesus and in Jesus makes a covenant of love which cannot fail. The description of the jewelled city is very like the new Jerusalem in Revelation 21–22. The earthly fulfilments of these prophecies point far beyond themselves to what the Lord does in Jesus, his Servant.

questions.header: 55:1–13: The Lord calls his people back
questions.question: What does the Lord call his people to do in vv1–3 and what does he offer them? How are vv1‑4 a picture of the grace of God to his people? What will be the result of that grace?
questions.answer: Come, eat, and drink and be satisfied. Listen to the Lord and live. He offers them true satisfaction — ‘eat what is good, and you will delight in the richest of fare.’ There is an emphasis on the fact that it is all free: ‘without money and without cost’ (v1). The Lord is offering them his everlasting love (v3). The result: the everlasting covenant established with David will flow out to the whole earth — nations that did not know Israel will come running to them because of the Lord their God (vv4‑5).

questions.header: 55:1–13: The Lord calls his people back
questions.question: What does the Lord call his people to do in vv6‑7 and what does he offer them?
questions.answer: He calls them to repentance: seek the Lord while he may be found, call on him while he is near. Let the wicked forsake their ways and the unrighteous their thoughts. Let them turn to the Lord — and he will have mercy on them. He will freely pardon. The pardon is the outworking of the Servant’s work in chapter 53. The invitation is urgent (‘while he may be found’, ‘while he is near’) but the response is not required to be earned — only to turn and come.

questions.header: 55:1–13: The Lord calls his people back
questions.question: In between these offers of grace, mercy, pardon, joy, and restoration are vv8–11, which are about the word of God. What is the Lord saying about his word, and why is that important here?
questions.answer: The Lord’s word is absolutely effective. He uses it to bring about exactly what he wants to happen — like rain and snow that water the earth and make it fruitful, and then return to their source having accomplished their purpose. His word will not return to him empty. This gives complete confidence that the Lord will achieve all he promises in this chapter. The promises of 54–55 are not wishful thinking but the word of the Lord — and his word always accomplishes its purpose.

questions.header: 55:1–13: The Lord calls his people back
questions.question: What does the Lord promise his people in vv12–13?
questions.answer: Joy, peace, rejoicing, overflowing blessing — the Lord’s name being glorified for ever. It is a total and joyful restoration. Even the created order participates: the mountains and hills burst into song, the trees of the field clap their hands. Instead of thorns and briers, trees will grow. This is a picture of new creation — the reversal of the curse of Genesis 3 (cf. 65:17–25; Revelation 21–22).

questions.header: Application
questions.question: We have seen how chapters 54–55 work out in the history of Israel and in our own lives as the NT people of God. Look at the imperatives in today’s passage.
