What would it mean to us to follow them? What prevents us from rejoicing? What makes us afraid? Where do we look for satisfaction? How does this passage help us understand what Jesus has achieved for the people of God by coming as the suffering Servant?
questions.answer:
The imperatives: sing, rejoice, don’t be afraid, don’t fear disgrace, come to the Lord for true satisfaction, listen to the Lord, seek the Lord in repentance. Each one is the fruit of the Servant’s work in chapter 53 — the shame is removed because he bore it (53:3); the fear is gone because the punishment that brought peace was on him (53:5); the pardon is free because he bore the sin of many (53:12).

What prevents rejoicing: lingering shame, unbelief that God’s love is truly free and unconditional, seeking satisfaction in other things (the ‘why spend money on what is not bread?’ question of 55:2 is perennial). The passage helps us understand what Jesus has achieved: not just a transaction that settles accounts but the restoration of the full covenant relationship — the barren woman now has many children, the rejected wife is restored to the most intimate love, the attacked city is rebuilt more beautifully than before. Joy, confidence, and satisfaction as we wait for the ultimate fulfilment come from receiving what has already been achieved, not from earning what we still need to achieve.
questions.leaderNote: This is the last session before the final block (sessions 24–28 covering chs 56–66). It is a good moment to pause and reflect on the arc of the whole study so far — from the devastating indictment of chapter 1 to the joyful invitation of chapter 55. The movement is from ‘your hands are full of blood’ (1:15) to ‘come, all you who are thirsty, come to the waters’ (55:1). That is the shape of the gospel.

chapters.title: Isaiah 56–57 — The Lord Will Gather the Exile and the Excluded
chapters.bigIdea: The Lord will gather the exile and the excluded and bless them with his presence, but the wicked should expect no peace.

background.body:
Chapters 56–66 form the final section of Isaiah, sometimes called ‘Third Isaiah’ by those who see a change of authorship here. Whether or not one accepts that division, there is clearly a shift of tone and emphasis: we move from the great proclamations of chapters 40–55 to their practical implications for the community of God’s people. The question becomes: in the light of the Servant’s work and the Lord’s universal saving purposes, who belongs to the people of God and how should they live?

The foreigner and the eunuch (56:3‑8). These two groups are highlighted as those who might fear exclusion. The eunuch was explicitly excluded from the temple assembly by Deuteronomy 23:1. The foreigner was not part of God’s covenant people. But the Lord promises both that they will be welcomed — the eunuch will have an everlasting name within the temple walls (v5), the foreigner will be brought to the Lord’s holy mountain with joy (v7). This is a direct fulfilment of the universal scope of the Servant’s mission (42:6; 49:6).

The Sabbath emphasis (56:2, 4, 6). Sabbath keeping is mentioned three times in this short section. The Sabbath celebrates God’s rest after Creation and looks forward to his eternal rest. It is an act of trust in the Lord — choosing not to work because the Lord is the ultimate provider and sustainer. Meaningful Sabbath keeping is the mark of genuine, heart-felt belonging to the Lord, as opposed to the empty religious observance condemned in chapter 1.

The wickedness of the Lord’s people (56:9–57:13). Despite the glorious promises of chapters 40–55, the reality of the community’s life falls far short. The leaders are characterised as blind watchmen, greedy dogs, self-seeking shepherds (56:9–12). The people themselves are given to idolatry (57:3–10). The root problem (57:11): they fear the wrong person. They should fear the Lord.

The Lord will revive the repentant (57:14–21). Despite the wickedness, the Lord offers hope for the contrite. He does not only dwell on high but also with the one who is contrite and lowly in spirit (v15). His anger will not be for ever (v16). But the wicked should expect no peace (v21) — the closing line echoes 48:22 and forms a refrain that will appear again at the end of chapter 66.

keyPoints.body:
The Lord’s coming salvation motivates present righteousness. The opening verse of chapter 56 — ‘Maintain justice and do what is right, for my salvation is close at hand’ — sets the agenda for chapters 56–66. The certain hope of the Lord’s salvation is the grounds for ethical obedience, not its replacement.

The Lord’s coming salvation is radically inclusive. The foreigner and the eunuch — both excluded from full participation in Israel’s worship — are not merely tolerated but welcomed and given a better standing than they had before. The Lord’s house will be called ‘a house of prayer for all nations’ (56:7 — quoted by Jesus in Mark 11:17). This is the practical outworking of the Servant’s universal mission.

True Sabbath keeping is about the orientation of the heart. The threefold emphasis on Sabbath keeping is not about ritual compliance but about genuine trust in the Lord — holding fast to his covenant, binding oneself to him, keeping one’s hands from evil. The Sabbath is the sign of real belonging to the Lord, in contrast to the empty religion condemned throughout Isaiah.

Poor leadership is a form of judgment and a cause of further sin. The blind watchmen and greedy dogs of 56:9–12 are both a punishment for the people’s sin (poor leaders are part of the Lord’s judgment) and themselves sinful. Wicked leaders compound the problem they are supposed to address.

The Lord dwells with the contrite as well as on the high and holy place. The remarkable promise of 57:15 — ‘I live in a high and holy place, but also with the one who is contrite and lowly in spirit’ — is the answer to the wickedness described in the preceding verses. The Lord does not abandon the repentant; he comes to them. But there is no peace for the wicked (57:21).

exegetical.body:
The eunuch’s ‘everlasting name’ (56:5). In a culture where one’s name was perpetuated through descendants, a eunuch had no hope of a lasting name. The Lord promises something better than children: a name within the temple walls, a monument within his house. This is the ultimate reversal — permanent inclusion in the Lord’s presence, more lasting than any human legacy.

‘A house of prayer for all nations’ (56:7). This phrase is quoted by Jesus in all three Synoptic Gospels (Matthew 21:13; Mark 11:17; Luke 19:46) when he drives out the money-changers from the temple. In its original context it refers to the inclusion of foreigners in Israel’s worship. Jesus applies it to the corruption of the temple which has prevented that inclusion. The universal scope of genuine worship is both Isaiah’s point and Jesus’s.

The blind watchmen (56:10). A watchman’s job is to see danger coming and warn the people. A blind watchman is an oxymoron — they are exactly the opposite of what they should be. The leaders of God’s people are failing at the most basic level of their calling. This echoes the diagnosis of the leaders in chapters 1–5.

Idolatry as adultery (57:3–10). The imagery of adultery and prostitution for idolatry is used throughout the OT prophets (Hosea, Jeremiah, Ezekiel). Here the detail is graphic: they sacrifice children under spreading trees, they pour out drink offerings to idols, they set up their bed wherever they want (v7–8). The point is that idolatry is not a neutral religious choice but a fundamental betrayal of the covenant relationship with the Lord.

‘The highway’ (57:14). The Lord commands: ‘Build up, build up, prepare the road! Remove the obstacles out of the way of my people.’ This picks up the highway imagery of 40:3 and 35:8 (‘the Way of Holiness’). The preparation for the Lord’s coming continues. The obstacle to the Lord’s blessing is not geography but the contrite and humble spirit that must be cultivated.

pastorals.body:
The inclusion of the foreigner and the eunuch (56:3‑8) is one of the most practically applicable passages in this section of Isaiah. Help the group think concretely about who in their own community or context might feel like the ‘foreigner’ or ‘eunuch’ — those who might fear that they are too excluded, too broken, or too far outside to belong to the Lord’s people. The Lord’s welcome to them is not a concession but a deliberate, joyful promise.

The description of the wicked leaders in 56:9–12 and the idolatry of the people in 57:3–13 can feel very distant from the glory of chapters 40–55. But this is precisely the pastoral point: the Lord’s magnificent promises do not automatically transform the community. The people of God can hear the greatest proclamations of grace and still live in ways that contradict them. The question the group should sit with: how does this happen, and how do we guard against it?

watch.body: Watch for the group treating the inclusion of the foreigner and eunuch as simply a pleasant historical note about ancient Israel’s expanding boundaries. The pastoral application is immediate: who are the people in our own community who feel too excluded, too broken, or too far outside to belong? And does our community’s practice reflect the Lord’s explicit promise that his house will be a house of prayer for all nations?

questions.header: 56:1‑8: The Lord gathers the exiles and the excluded
questions.question: What does the Lord ask his people to do in v1 and why?
questions.answer: Maintain justice and do what is right — because the Lord’s salvation is close at hand. This is a summons to righteousness based on what the future holds. Certain hope of salvation should be making a difference in how the people of God live as they wait for it. The coming salvation motivates present obedience — it does not replace it.

questions.header: 56:1‑8: The Lord gathers the exiles and the excluded
questions.question: Go through the passage and look out for what it looks like to ‘do what is right’ in the eyes of God. What ideas are repeated? Why do you think Sabbath keeping is given such emphasis? What does it mean to keep the Sabbath without desecrating it?
questions.answer:
Repeated ideas: hold fast to the covenant (vv2, 4, 6); keep the Sabbath without desecrating it (vv2, 4, 6); bind themselves to the Lord (vv3, 6).

Sabbath keeping celebrates God’s rest after Creation and looks forward to his eternal rest. It is an act of trust in the Lord — choosing not to work because the Lord is the ultimate provider. True Sabbath keeping means genuine obedience and keeping one’s hands from evil, truly holding fast to the Lord and his covenant — not just going through the motions. True obedience that looks to God’s coming salvation is the real mark of the people of God.

questions.header: 56:1‑8: The Lord gathers the exiles and the excluded
questions.question: Who does the Lord particularly welcome in this section and why is it significant? What does the Lord promise to the foreigner and the eunuch and what does this tell us about his coming salvation?
questions.answer:
The foreigner — not part of God’s covenant people, but the Lord says they can be. The eunuch — not even allowed to enter the temple (Deuteronomy 23:1), but the Lord will make them an integral part of his temple.

The eunuch: an everlasting name better than children, within the walls of the temple — permanent inclusion in the Lord’s presence, more lasting than any human legacy. The foreigner: brought to the Lord’s holy mountain with joy and acceptance. The Lord’s coming salvation is radically inclusive — not just gathering the exiles but welcoming the formerly excluded. His house will be called a house of prayer for all nations (v7).
questions.leaderNote: Jesus quotes Isaiah 56:7 in Mark 11:17 when driving out the money-changers from the temple. His point is that the temple’s corruption has prevented the inclusion of the Gentiles that Isaiah promised. The universal scope of genuine worship is both Isaiah’s point and Jesus’s.

questions.header: 56:9–57:13: The wickedness of the Lord’s people
questions.question: What is the problem with the people of God in vv9–12? What is the Lord doing for the righteous? (57:1‑2) What is the problem with the people in 57:3–10? What is at the root of their idolatry? (57:11–13)
questions.answer:
Terrible leadership (vv9–12): ineffective, seeking their own gain, greedy, blind, drunk. They are the opposite of good shepherds — they are consuming the flock rather than feeding it.

For the righteous (57:1‑2): the Lord is showing them mercy. They die to be spared from evil. The righteous perish but no one understands that they are being taken away from future calamity.

The people (57:3–10): rampant idolatry — child sacrifice, sexual immorality in pagan worship, seeking guidance from idols rather than the Lord. The detail is disturbing and is meant to be.

The root of their idolatry (v11): they fear the wrong person. They have not feared the Lord but have feared other powers — and that fear has driven them to idolatry. The Lord will expose their idols and show how worthless they are. He will still offer blessing to the one who takes refuge in him.

questions.header: 57:14–21: The Lord will revive his repentant people
questions.question: What hope does the Lord offer his people here? What warning is there?
questions.answer: The Lord does not only dwell on high but also with the contrite and lowly in spirit (v15). He will revive the spirit of the lowly and revive the heart of the contrite. He promises that his anger will not be for ever (v16) — he will heal them, guide them, and restore comfort to them and those who mourn for them. Healing, restoration, praise, and peace. But the warning is explicit: ‘There is no peace for the wicked’ (v21). The Lord’s offer is genuine but not unconditional.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: How are these chapters fulfilled in Israel’s history and how are they fulfilled in Jesus? Who in our context are the ‘foreigners’ and ‘eunuchs’ who might fear exclusion? How does the promise of 56:3‑8 speak to them — and to us?
questions.answer:
Historically: the return from exile did include some Gentiles but nothing like the full scope of 56:7–8. Fulfilment in Jesus: the inclusion of the Gentiles in the people of God through Christ (Acts 10; Ephesians 2:11–22; Galatians 3:28) is the direct fulfilment of these promises. The house of prayer for all nations is the church. The Philip and Ethiopian eunuch episode in Acts 8 is a remarkable specific fulfilment of 56:3‑5 — an eunuch is brought into the people of God through the proclamation of Isaiah 53.

In our context: help the group think concretely about who might feel too excluded or broken to belong — and whether the community’s practice reflects the Lord’s explicit promise. The challenge is not merely to be inclusive in theory but to create communities where those who feel like foreigners and eunuchs actually experience the welcome of the Lord.
questions.leaderNote: The Acts 8 episode (Philip and the Ethiopian eunuch) is a beautiful specific fulfilment worth pointing to: an actual eunuch, a foreigner from Ethiopia, is brought into the people of God through the proclamation of Isaiah 53. Isaiah 56:3‑5 and 53 are linked in this narrative in a remarkably direct way.

chapters.title: Isaiah 58–59 — The Lord Blesses Faithful Obedience and Personally Brings Salvation
chapters.bigIdea: The Lord blesses faithful obedience, and will personally bring salvation and judgment and make an everlasting covenant with his people.

background.body:
Chapter 58: Fasting from sin, not just food. The people’s religious observance looks exemplary: they fast, they seek the Lord, they appear to be eager for his presence. But the Lord’s response is blunt: their fasting is a performance. The real fast he is calling them to is ethical — justice for the oppressed, food for the hungry, shelter for the homeless, care for the vulnerable. True fasting is not primarily about what you abstain from but about how you treat others.

Chapter 59: Sin separates. The opening verses of chapter 59 make a foundational point: the problem is not the Lord’s inability to save but the people’s sin which has separated them from him (v1–2). What follows is one of the most comprehensive catalogues of sin in Isaiah — iniquities, sins, bloodshed, lies, injustice, darkness, stumbling. The community has completely lost its moral bearings.

The Lord himself acts (59:15b–21). The theological climax: the Lord looks and sees no one acting with justice, no one interceding. So he himself takes on the role of warrior and redeemer. He dresses himself in the armour of righteousness, salvation, vengeance, and zeal. This passage is picked up in Ephesians 6 (the armour of God) and in Revelation 19:11–16 (the rider on the white horse). The warrior who comes to save is the Lord himself — and ultimately, in the NT’s reading, Jesus.

The everlasting covenant (59:21). The session closes with the promise of an everlasting covenant: the Lord’s Spirit and his word will remain with his people and their descendants for ever. This echoes the new covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:31–34 and points forward to its fulfilment in Christ.

keyPoints.body:
True religious observance and ethical living cannot be separated. The diagnosis of chapter 58 — correct religious practice that leaves the heart unchanged and the poor unhelped — is one of Isaiah’s most consistent themes (cf. 1:11–17; 29:13). The fast the Lord wants is not an addition to justice but justice itself. Help the group feel the specific and concrete nature of what the Lord calls for (vv6‑7).

Sin separates from God. The foundational claim of 59:1–2 is not that the Lord is absent or unable but that sin creates a barrier. ‘Your iniquities have separated you from your God; your sins have hidden his face from you.’ The problem is not on the Lord’s side but on ours. This is the precondition for understanding everything that follows.

The Lord takes matters into his own hands. The image of 59:15b–17 is startling: the Lord looks around and sees no human agent of justice, no one to intercede. So he himself comes as a warrior. This is the shape of the gospel: the Lord’s own arm brings salvation (59:16), just as 53:1 asked ‘to whom has the arm of the Lord been revealed?’ The Servant is the Lord’s own arm, coming in person to save.

The warrior’s armour is the armour of the Lord. Paul’s instruction to put on the full armour of God in Ephesians 6:10–17 draws on this passage (and Isaiah 11:5). The point is that the armour belongs to the Lord — it is his righteousness, his salvation, his zeal. The Christian’s battle is fought not with their own weapons but by sharing in the Lord’s own armour.

The everlasting covenant includes the Spirit and the word. The promise of 59:21 — the Lord’s Spirit and word remaining with his people and their descendants for ever — is the new covenant promise in compressed form. It is the answer to everything that has gone wrong in chapters 56–59: the people have failed to live rightly because they lack the Spirit and the internalised word. The Lord promises to supply both.

exegetical.body:
The true fast (58:6‑7). The Lord’s description of the fast he wants is highly specific: loose the chains of injustice, untie the cords of the yoke, set the oppressed free, break every yoke (v6); share your food with the hungry, provide shelter for the wanderer, clothe the naked, not turn away from your own flesh and blood (v7). This is not a generic call to be nice but a precise diagnosis of the specific injustices being perpetrated in the community.

The blessings of the true fast (58:8–9, 10–12, 13–14). The blessings are described in three sections, each conditional on a different aspect of the true fast. The language of light, healing, righteousness, glory, answered prayer, the Lord’s presence, guidance, satisfaction, strength, fruitfulness, and inheritance goes far beyond mere earthly blessings — it points toward the eschatological flourishing described in chapters 60–66.

The comprehensive catalogue of sin (59:3–15a). The list in 59:3‑8 is remarkable for its comprehensiveness: bloodshed, lies, injustice, evil thoughts, violence, crooked schemes. Verses 9–15a shift to a corporate lament in the first person plural: ‘we’ have done all these things. The community confesses its sin collectively. This is rare in Isaiah and important — the chapter is not just a diagnosis from outside but a communal acknowledgment of guilt.

The warrior of 59:15b–17. The Lord’s armour is described in detail: righteousness as a breastplate, salvation as a helmet, garments of vengeance, and a cloak of zeal. Compare Isaiah 11:5 (the Branch clothed with righteousness and faithfulness) and Ephesians 6:14–17 (the believer clothed in the Lord’s own armour). The imagery is consistent throughout Isaiah and into the NT.

‘The Redeemer will come to Zion’ (59:20). Paul quotes this verse in Romans 11:26 (‘and so all Israel will be saved; as it is written: “The deliverer will come from Zion”’) in the context of his discussion of Israel’s future salvation. The coming Redeemer of Isaiah 59 is, in Paul’s reading, Jesus — who will ultimately accomplish the full redemption of Israel and the nations.

pastorals.body:
The diagnosis of chapter 58 — religious practice without ethical content — is one of the most personally challenging passages in the whole study. It is very easy for a Bible study group to practise exactly what is being condemned: gathering to study the word, singing and praying, while the practical demands of justice, care for the hungry, and concern for the oppressed remain unaddressed. Help the group take the specific language of vv6‑7 seriously.

The communal lament of 59:9–15a is unusual and important. The first-person plural ‘we’ invites the group to identify with the confession rather than viewing it as someone else’s problem. This is an opportunity for genuine corporate self-examination, not just theological discussion about other people’s sin.

watch.body: Watch for the group treating the ‘true fast’ passage of 58:6‑7 as applying primarily to society or government rather than to themselves and their community. The Lord is addressing the people of God, not the surrounding nations. The call to loose chains of injustice, share food with the hungry, and provide shelter for the wanderer is addressed to those who are already seeking the Lord — the very people in your Bible study group.

questions.header: 58:1–14: Fasting from sin, not just food
questions.question: What is God’s people’s religious observance like? (vv1‑5) Why does the Lord describe it as ‘rebellion’? (v1)
questions.answer: They seem eager when they fast. They appear to be seeking the Lord, asking for his ways, and delighting in knowing him. But it is meaningless because the rest of their behaviour shows the true state of their heart — they exploit their workers, they quarrel and fight on fast days (v3–4). Their outward humility is not matched by inward transformation. It is rebellion because they are only outwardly humble — they cannot behave as they do and expect the Lord to hear them.

questions.header: 58:1–14: Fasting from sin, not just food
questions.question: What kind of fasting does the Lord want? Look at the three descriptions in vv6‑7, v9b–10a, and v13. What do they fast from and how should their religious observance look?
questions.answer:
vv6‑7: End oppression and injustice; not just wearing sackcloth and abstaining from food but feeding the hungry, giving shelter to the homeless, clothing the naked. It’s about what they do, not just what they avoid.

vv9b–10a: Doing away with oppression, doing away with malicious talk, and spending themselves for others. How they treat their brothers matters, not just how religious they are.

v13: Doing what God pleases on his day — not going their own way or speaking idle words. True Sabbath keeping is not rigid observance without meaning but genuine reorientation toward the Lord.

questions.header: 58:1–14: Fasting from sin, not just food
questions.question: What blessing does the Lord promise to those who truly ‘fast’?
questions.answer:
vv8‑9: Light, healing, righteousness, glory, answered prayer, the Lord’s presence.
vv10–12: Light in darkness, guidance, satisfaction, strength, fruitfulness, provision, restoration — they will be called ‘Repairer of Broken Walls, Restorer of Streets with Dwellings.’
v14: Joy in the Lord, triumph, inheritance. These blessings go far beyond mere earthly prosperity — they use language that points toward the eschatological flourishing of chapters 60–66.

questions.header: 59:1–15: Sin upon sin, stumbling in the darkness
questions.question: As the people face ongoing hardship, what is not their problem and what is their problem? (v1‑2) How many references to their sin can you find? How is it described? (vv1‑8) What is the result of their sin? (vv9–15a)
questions.answer:
The problem is not the Lord’s inability — his arm is not too short to save, his ear is not too dull to hear (v1). The problem is that their iniquities have separated them from their God and their sins have hidden his face from them (v2).

Sin catalogue (vv3–8): bloodshed, lies, injustice, evil imaginations, reliance on empty arguments, thinking up evil, evil deeds, violence, shed innocent blood, evil schemes, no justice, crooked paths. The list is exhaustive and overwhelming.

The result (vv9–15a): separated from justice and righteousness. Stumbling around blind in the darkness, mourning. Truth and honesty have fallen in the street. The only hope is that they acknowledge their sin — which they do in vv12–15a in a corporate first-person confession.

questions.header: 59:15–21: The Lord himself is the answer
questions.question: What is the imagery in 59:15b–17 and what truth about the Lord does it emphasise? What is the connection here between salvation and judgment? What will the Lord achieve when he acts? (59:21)
questions.answer:
The Lord is watching the situation and looking for someone to act — and there is no one. He is appalled and takes on the job himself. He dresses like a warrior — righteousness as a breastplate, salvation as a helmet, garments of vengeance, a cloak of zeal. The Lord is the only one who can and will bring salvation (v16). This is both pastoral (no human saviour is needed or sufficient) and challenging (the Lord’s salvation is also his judgment — he is bringing vengeance on his enemies).

Salvation and judgment are inseparable: the Lord wants to achieve salvation, but sin will be done away with for ever. The result will be fear of the Lord across the whole world. The Redeemer will come to Zion for those who repent.

The Lord will achieve: an everlasting covenant — his Spirit and his word with his people and their descendants for ever. This is the new covenant in seed form: the Spirit internalisng the word so that obedience comes from within rather than being imposed from without.
questions.leaderNote: The armour of God in Ephesians 6:14–17 draws directly on this passage (and Isaiah 11:5). It is worth pointing the group to this connection: Paul is telling believers to put on the Lord’s own armour — the armour the Lord himself wears when he comes as warrior to save. This is why fighting the Christian battle requires the Lord’s resources, not our own.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: How do we see God’s people practising empty religion in Isaiah? How do we see that now? How are we tempted to go through the motions? What did true faith/fasting look like in Isaiah’s time? What does the NT have to say about that? How do we see the Lord taking matters into his own hands regarding salvation? How did we see 59:15b–21 fulfilled in the life of Israel, in Jesus, and still to come?
questions.answer:
Empty religion: gathering for worship, prayer, and Bible study while the practical demands of justice, care for the hungry, and concern for the oppressed remain unaddressed. The NT parallel: James 1:26–27 (‘Religion that God our Father accepts as pure and faultless is this: to look after orphans and widows in their distress and to keep oneself from being polluted by the world.’)

The Lord taking matters into his own hands: partially fulfilled in the return from exile (when the Lord acted despite human failure); fully fulfilled in Christ (who is the Lord’s own arm coming to save, cf. John 12:38; Isaiah 53:1) and his atoning death; still to come in the final coming of the Redeemer-warrior of Revelation 19:11–16 (which draws directly on this passage).

The everlasting covenant of 59:21 is fulfilled in the new covenant in Christ’s blood (Luke 22:20; Hebrews 8–9) and the pouring out of the Spirit at Pentecost (Acts 2). The Spirit and the word dwelling with the people and their descendants for ever is the life of the Spirit-indwelt church.
questions.leaderNote: James 1:26–27 is particularly helpful for applying the ‘true fasting’ passage. Note also the Sermon on the Mount — the Beatitudes and the teaching on fasting (Matthew 6:16–18) both engage with the diagnosis of Isaiah 58 and show Jesus continuing the same prophetic tradition.

chapters.title: Isaiah 60–61 — Through His Anointed One the Lord Will Shine Through His People
chapters.bigIdea: Through his anointed one the Lord will shine his everlasting splendour through his people, overturn the effects of the fall, and draw the nations to himself.

background.body:
Chapters 60–62 form the centre of the closing section of Isaiah and describe the glorious future of the people of God in luminous, almost overwhelming detail. Chapter 60 describes the nations streaming to the light of God’s people; chapter 61 describes the anointed one who makes this possible; chapter 62 describes the new names and new identity of the restored people.

The structure of chapter 60. The chapter opens with the light of the Lord rising on his people (vv1–3), describes the nations streaming to that light (vv4‑9), continues with the transformation of the people’s relationship with the nations (vv10–14), and concludes with the Lord being their everlasting light (vv15–22). The repeated use of ‘everlasting’ (vv15, 19, 20) signals that this is not a temporary restoration but the final and eternal state of God’s people.

The connections to Revelation 21. The parallels between Isaiah 60 and Revelation 21 are striking and extensive. The city of God shining with the splendour of God (60:1–3 and Revelation 21:11); the gates never shut (60:11 and Revelation 21:25); the Lord as their everlasting light with no need for sun or moon (60:19–20 and Revelation 21:23–24); the nations bringing their splendour into the city (60:3, 9, 11 and Revelation 21:24, 26). John is deliberately drawing on Isaiah 60 to describe the new Jerusalem.

The anointed one of chapter 61. The speaker of 61:1‑3 is a figure we have met before in Isaiah: someone specifically sent by God and given the Spirit of the Lord, whose mission is focused on justice and freedom for the poor and marginalised (cf. 11:1‑5; 42:1–4). Jesus identifies himself as this figure when he reads these verses in the Nazareth synagogue and says ‘today this scripture is fulfilled in your hearing’ (Luke 4:18–21). This is one of the most explicit self-identifications in Jesus’s ministry.

keyPoints.body:
The people of God are the light that draws the nations. The vision of chapter 60 is not the people of God going out to the nations but the nations coming to them, attracted by the light of the Lord that shines on his people. This is the fulfilment of the original Abrahamic calling — to be a blessing to all nations (Genesis 12:3). The people of God are not a tribal enclave but a light in the darkness that the whole world is meant to see.

The Lord himself will be the everlasting light. The climax of chapter 60 (vv19–20) is the replacement of sun and moon by the Lord himself: ‘The Lord will be your everlasting light, and your God will be your glory.’ No more need for external sources of light — the Lord’s own presence will illuminate everything. Revelation 21:23–24 applies this directly to the new Jerusalem.

The anointed one overturns the effects of the fall. The mission of the figure in chapter 61 — good news to the poor, binding up the broken-hearted, freedom for captives, release from darkness, comfort for all who mourn, a crown of beauty instead of ashes — is a systematic reversal of the consequences of human sin and suffering. Every item in the list addresses something that is wrong in the fallen world.

Jesus identifies himself as this anointed one. Luke 4:18–21 records Jesus reading Isaiah 61:1–2 in the Nazareth synagogue and saying ‘today this scripture is fulfilled in your hearing.’ This is one of the most explicit and significant moments of Messianic self-identification in the Gospels. Jesus is claiming to be the figure of Isaiah 61 — the one anointed by the Spirit to overturn the fall.

The fulfilment is already begun but not yet complete. Jesus has come; the Spirit has been poured out; people from every nation are being drawn to the Lord’s light. But the full vision of chapters 60–61 — the nations streaming to Zion, the Lord as everlasting light, all mourning replaced with joy — awaits the new creation. We live between the dawning and the full brightness.

exegetical.body:
‘Arise, shine’ (60:1). The Hebrew imperatives are feminine singular, addressing Zion/Jerusalem as the people of God. The call to arise and shine echoes the earlier wake-up calls of 51:9, 17 and 52:1. But now there is no longer a call to wake from sleep or from the cup of wrath — the light has come and the people’s task is to reflect it.

‘Gross darkness covers the peoples’ (60:2). The darkness that covers the peoples is not physical but spiritual — the darkness of life without the knowledge of the Lord. The contrast with the Lord’s glory rising on his people is total. The people of God are not told to retreat from the darkness but to let the Lord’s light shine through them so that the nations are drawn to it.

The Spirit of the Sovereign Lord (61:1). The anointed one is characterised by the Spirit of the Lord — the same Spirit that rests on the Branch (11:2) and the Servant (42:1). The Spirit’s presence is the mark of genuine divine commissioning. At Jesus’s baptism the Spirit descends on him (Matthew 3:16–17), and Luke 4:18 records Jesus quoting Isaiah 61:1 with ‘the Spirit of the Lord is on me’ as the ground of his ministry.

‘The year of the Lord’s favour’ (61:2). This phrase echoes the Jubilee legislation of Leviticus 25 — the year when debts were cancelled, slaves freed, and land returned. The anointed one is proclaiming the ultimate Jubilee: the comprehensive cancellation of the debt of sin and the restoration of everything that was lost. Jesus’s reading of these verses in Luke 4 implies that his ministry is the fulfilment of the Jubilee.

The Beatitudes and Isaiah 61. The Sermon on the Mount, particularly the Beatitudes (Matthew 5:1–12), is deeply shaped by Isaiah 61. The poor in spirit (v3a), those who mourn (v4), the meek who will inherit the earth (v5), those who hunger and thirst for righteousness (v6) — all echo the concerns of Isaiah 61. Jesus is proclaiming the kingdom by describing those who are the beneficiaries of the anointed one’s ministry.

pastorals.body:
Chapter 60 is one of the most luminous passages in Isaiah and should be read aloud in the session — perhaps the leader reading it while the group listens. After the darkness and confession of chapters 58–59, the opening ‘Arise, shine, for your light has come!’ should arrive with something of the force of dawn after a long night. Give the group time to receive it before moving to the questions.

The identification of Jesus as the anointed one of Isaiah 61 (Luke 4:18–21) is one of the most important moments in the whole study. Help the group understand what Jesus is claiming and what it means. He is not merely reading a passage about compassion — he is claiming to be the one through whom the Lord is fulfilling his ancient promises to restore everything that is broken. This is a staggering claim, and it should be received as such.

watch.body: Watch for the group treating chapter 60 as purely futuristic (about the new creation, not about now) or purely historical (about the return from exile, not about us). The vision of chapter 60 is already being fulfilled as people from every nation are drawn to the Lord through the proclamation of the gospel. The group itself is part of the fulfilment — people from the Gentile nations who have been drawn to the Lord’s light. Help them see themselves in the vision.

questions.header: 60:1–22: The Lord’s everlasting light through his people draws the nations
questions.question: What is the state of the world in v2? Where have we seen this imagery before in Isaiah? What changes? (vv1–2) Where does the light begin and what is the result? (v3)
questions.answer:
The world is covered in darkness — all the people who are in darkness. We have seen this imagery in chapter 9 (‘the people walking in darkness have seen a great light’) and in 58:10 (light rising in the darkness for those who practise the true fast).

Dawn after the darkness of night. The glory of the Lord rises upon his people. The nations who are in darkness see the light shining on the people of God and begin to come to them. The result: kings and nations streaming to the light.

questions.header: 60:1–22: The Lord’s everlasting light through his people draws the nations
questions.question: Look at the imagery in vv4‑9. What picture is being painted here and what point is it making?
questions.answer: Standing and looking out, seeing lost loved ones returning. Everyone flocks to the people of God and they bring their wealth with them — gold, incense, flocks, ships carrying people from afar. They come to the temple of God bringing offerings. The idea is that the people of God shine with the splendour of the Lord and attract the world out of the darkness they are in. The nations are praising God, worshipping God, looking to the Lord. It is a picture of worldwide coming to the Lord.

questions.header: 60:1–22: The Lord’s everlasting light through his people draws the nations
questions.question: What is happening in vv10–14?
questions.answer: Foreigners will rebuild the walls (v10). The gates will always be open — no more closing at night for fear (v11). The nations will bring their wealth and kings will serve God’s people (vv11–13). The people of God are the city of God where the Lord in his glory dwells (v14). Everything that was hostile and threatening has been transformed — enemies become builders, oppressors become servants.

questions.header: 60:1–22: The Lord’s everlasting light through his people draws the nations
questions.question: How will the Lord transform the fortunes of his people in vv15–22? Look at Revelation 21. What similarities do you notice? How does that help us understand Isaiah 60 better?
questions.answer:
He will restore their reputation; they will know him as Saviour and Redeemer; outrageous wealth and blessing; peace and well-being; violence, ruin, and destruction gone; the Lord will be their light and glory for ever; their days of sorrow will end; they will be righteous and possess the land for ever.

Revelation 21 parallels: the city of God shining with God’s splendour (21:11 and 60:1–3); gates never shut (21:25 and 60:11); the Lord as their light with no need for sun or moon (21:23–24 and 60:19–20); the nations bringing their splendour (21:24, 26 and 60:3, 9, 11). Isaiah 60 is one of the primary sources John draws on in Revelation 21. The full vision begins in Christ and will be completed in the new creation.
questions.leaderNote: The parallels between Isaiah 60 and Revelation 21 are extensive enough to be worth working through systematically with the group — or at least pointing to several specific examples. John is not merely using Isaiah as illustration but claiming that Revelation 21 is the fulfilment of what Isaiah 60 promised.

questions.header: 61:1‑11: The Lord’s anointed one overturns the fall and restores God’s people
questions.question: How can we tell that the person speaking in chapter 61 is someone we have met before in Isaiah? Look at chapters 11 and 42. What similarities do you notice in description and mission? What is the mission of the person speaking in chapter 61?
questions.answer:
Someone specifically sent by God and given the Spirit of the Lord (cf. 11:2; 42:1). In all three cases the mission is focused on justice and freedom for the poor and marginalised.

Mission of the anointed one (ch 61): good news to the poor, binding up the broken-hearted, freedom for captives, release from darkness, proclaiming the year of the Lord’s favour and the day of vengeance, comforting all who mourn, giving a crown of beauty instead of ashes, the oil of gladness instead of mourning, a garment of praise instead of a spirit of despair. He overturns the effects of sin and the fall — every item in the list addresses something that is wrong in a fallen world.

questions.header: 61:1‑11: The Lord’s anointed one overturns the fall and restores God’s people
questions.question: Look at Luke 4:16–21. What did Jesus think Isaiah 61 was about? How do we see these chapters fulfilled in his ministry? (See also Matthew 5:1–12.)
questions.answer: Jesus said that these verses applied to him: ‘Today this scripture is fulfilled in your hearing.’ In his coming, he was fulfilling Isaiah 61 — he was the person anointed with the Spirit of the Lord. Jesus overturned all the effects of the fall in his earthly ministry: sickness, death, blindness, captivity to sin and demons, the sorrow of living in a fallen world were all addressed by him. The Beatitudes (Matthew 5:1–12) pick up on Isaiah 61 and show how the Kingdom of God will be characterised by what we see in chapter 61. In the earthly ministry of Jesus we see it begin; it will reach its full fulfilment in the new creation.
questions.leaderNote: Jesus’s reading of Isaiah 61:1–2 in Luke 4:18–21 is one of the most significant moments of Messianic self-identification in the Gospels. He stops reading partway through v2 — before ‘the day of vengeance’. This has been interpreted as significant: at his first coming he proclaims the year of favour; the day of vengeance awaits his return.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: What difference would these chapters have made to the original hearers in their situation? How do we see these chapters already being fulfilled in the people of God and in the work of Christ in his church? What are we still waiting for? What difference does it make to us as exiles and strangers on earth now? (1 Peter 2:11)
questions.answer:
For original hearers: promise of restoration, peace, forgiveness, and renewal after exile. The Lord will once again show compassion on his people and redeem and save them. Enemies will become either friends, their servants, or will be destroyed. They will experience peace and glory.

Already fulfilled: the dawn of the Lord’s light in Jesus (John 1:9; 8:12); the nations streaming to the Lord through the proclamation of the gospel; the anointed one bringing good news to the poor (Luke 4; Luke 7:22); the church as the community where the effects of the fall are being reversed.

Still waiting for: the full brightness of the Lord’s everlasting light; all mourning replaced with joy; the complete transformation of all creation; the final gathering of all nations. As exiles and strangers we live in the tension — the light has come but the darkness is still real; the effects of the fall are being addressed but not yet fully reversed. The vision of chapters 60–61 keeps our hope oriented toward the completion that is coming.

chapters.title: Isaiah 62–64 — Praise the Lord Who Comes to Judge and Redeem
chapters.bigIdea: Praise the Lord who comes to judge and redeem.

background.body:
Chapters 62–64 move from the glorious vision of chapters 60–61 to its practical and prayerful appropriation. Chapter 62 describes the Lord’s intercession for and transformation of Zion; chapter 63:1–6 presents a dramatic picture of the Lord as warrior-judge; chapters 63:7–64:12 contain one of the most powerful communal prayers in the whole OT.

The new names of Zion (62:2–5). The people of God will be called by a new name (v2): no longer Deserted or Desolate but Hephzibah (‘my delight is in her’) and Beulah (‘married’). These new names represent a complete transformation of identity — from rejected and abandoned to delighted in and beloved. The name-change imagery recurs throughout Revelation (2:17; 3:12).

The intercessors (62:6–7). The Lord appoints watchmen on the walls of Jerusalem who are to give themselves no rest and give the Lord no rest until he establishes Jerusalem. This is a remarkable picture of intercession: the people of God are called to pray persistently, reminding the Lord of his promises and holding him to his word. This is not impertinence but the kind of bold, persistent prayer Jesus commends (Luke 18:1–8).

The warrior-judge of 63:1–6. The dramatic vision of the Lord coming from Edom with garments stained red is one of the most arresting images in Isaiah. The questions — ‘who is this?’ — and the Lord’s answer — ‘it is I, proclaiming victory, mighty to save’ — identify the coming one as the Lord himself. His garments are stained with the blood of the nations he has judged in his wrath. Revelation 19:11–16 applies this imagery to the second coming of Jesus.

The communal prayer of 63:7–64:12. This is the longest sustained prayer in Isaiah and one of the most theologically profound in the whole OT. It moves from praise (63:7–9), through historical reflection on the Exodus and the people’s subsequent rebellion (63:10–14), to a desperate plea for God to act again (63:15–19), to the great petition that the Lord would ‘rend the heavens and come down’ (64:1‒5), to a confession of sin (64:5‑7), to a final plea for mercy (64:8–12). This prayer models what genuine communal intercession looks like.

keyPoints.body:
The Lord’s restoration of his people involves giving them a new identity. The new names of chapter 62 — Hephzibah, Beulah, the Holy People, the Redeemed of the Lord, Sought After, City No Longer Deserted — are not merely cosmetic. They describe a fundamental transformation of the people’s relationship with the Lord and their standing before the world. The people who were abandoned are now the object of the Lord’s delight.

The people of God are called to persistent intercession. The watchmen of 62:6–7 are not passive observers of the Lord’s purposes but active participants in them, praying without rest until the Lord acts. This is the most specific call to intercession in the book of Isaiah and one of the most direct in the whole OT.

The Lord’s judgment of his enemies is part of his salvation of his people. The warrior of 63:1–6 is the same one who comes to save (v5 echoes 59:16). His judgment on Edom/the nations is not opposed to his salvation of his people but is the other side of the same act. You cannot have the salvation without the judgment.

The communal prayer models honest intercession. The prayer of 63:7–64:12 does not avoid difficult truths: the people acknowledge the Lord’s past faithfulness (63:7–9), confess their own rebellion (63:10; 64:5‑7), cry out in urgent need (64:1‑5), and end in a plea for mercy that does not minimise the Lord’s anger (64:9–12). This is intercession that takes both the Lord’s holiness and his love with full seriousness.

We are the clay; he is the potter. The great affirmation of 64:8 — ‘Yet you, Lord, are our Father. We are the clay, you are the potter; we are all the work of your hand’ — is both an act of submission and a basis for appeal. If we are his work, he will not abandon us. The potter does not destroy what his hands have made.

exegetical.body:
Hephzibah and Beulah (62:4). These Hebrew names are preserved untranslated in many Bible versions. Hephzibah means ‘my delight is in her’ — expressing the Lord’s delight in his restored people. Beulah means ‘married’ — expressing the restoration of the covenant relationship after the period of apparent abandonment.

The watchmen on the walls (62:6‒7). The image of watchmen is used elsewhere in the OT for prophets (Ezekiel 3:17; 33:7) and for those called to pray (Nehemiah 7:3). Here they are specifically intercessors — appointed by the Lord himself to remind him of his promises without ceasing. The call to ‘give him no rest till he establishes Jerusalem’ is deliberately bold. It is modelled on Abraham’s intercession for Sodom (Genesis 18) and anticipates the parable of the persistent widow (Luke 18:1–8).

The Lord coming from Edom (63:1–6). Edom was Israel’s neighbour and persistent enemy. In Isaiah’s prophetic vision, Edom represents the hostile nations in general — and the Lord’s coming from Edom represents his decisive judgment of all the enemies of his people. The imagery of the winepress (v3) is the same as Revelation 14:19–20 and 19:15, which apply it to the final judgment executed by Christ.

‘Rend the heavens and come down’ (64:1). This is one of the most urgent petitions in the OT — a cry for the Lord to break through the apparent barrier between heaven and earth and act decisively. The image of the mountains trembling and the nations quaking at his presence echoes the theophany of Sinai (Exodus 19:18) and points forward to the coming of the Lord in power at the end of history.

The potter and the clay (64:8). Compare Isaiah 29:16 and 45:9, where the same image is used to rebuke those who question the Lord’s sovereignty. Here the same image is used not as rebuke but as appeal: because we are the clay and you are the potter, therefore do not be angry beyond measure (v9). The potter’s investment in his work becomes the basis for the plea for mercy.

pastorals.body:
The communal prayer of 63:7–64:12 is rare and important — it is a model of corporate intercession that takes both the Lord’s holiness and his love with full seriousness. If the group has the capacity, it would be worth reading this prayer aloud together — the leader leading and the group responding, or reading it in sections. It is designed to be prayed, not just studied.

The warrior imagery of 63:1–6 may be uncomfortable for some in the group. The image of the Lord with blood-stained garments is deliberately stark. Help the group hold the discomfort rather than resolving it prematurely: the same God who promises comfort (40:1), who calls with maternal tenderness (49:15), who gently leads the nursing ewes (40:11), is also the warrior who judges with implacable severity. Both are essential to who the Lord is. The judgment of the warrior is the salvation of his people.

watch.body: Watch for the group treating the prayer of 63:7–64:12 as primarily a historical document about Israel’s relationship with the Lord rather than as a model for their own intercession. This prayer is meant to be prayed — its structure, its honesty, its boldness, and its theological grounding are all available to the group as a pattern for their own communal prayer. Help the group receive it as such.

questions.header: 62:1–12: The Lord will restore Zion
questions.question: What are the Lord’s plans for Zion in this chapter? We are told that the people of God ‘will be called by a new name’ (v2). What new names are given here and what do these new identities say about their new experience?
questions.answer:
The Lord’s plans: restoration, renewal, salvation, glory, vindication, relationship with the Lord, blessing, protection, establishment, abundance, praise of the nations, gathering of the nations, redemption.

New names: no longer called Deserted and Desolate but Hephzibah (‘my delight is in her’) and Beulah (‘married’). They will be called The Holy People, the Redeemed of the Lord, Sought After, City No Longer Deserted. These names reverse everything the people have experienced in their history of rejection and exile. They have been like an adulterous wife, then like a widow — but the Lord is going to bring them back into blessing and the most intimate covenant relationship. All previous experiences will be overturned.

questions.header: 62:1–12: The Lord will restore Zion
questions.question: Is there any hint in the passage about how the Lord will bring about this restoration? (62:6‑9)
questions.answer: Through the constant intercession of the watchmen he has appointed on the walls of Jerusalem (vv6–7) — they are to give themselves no rest and give the Lord no rest until he establishes Jerusalem. The Lord’s own word and oath are also the ground of the certainty (vv8‑9). The restoration is certain because the Lord has sworn by his own right hand and his mighty arm.

questions.header: 63:1‑6: Praise the Lord for his judgment and salvation
questions.question: What questions are asked about the person in Isaiah 63:1‑6? How are they answered? Now compare 63:1‑6 with 59:15–19. How can we tell who is speaking in 63:1‑6? Read Revelation 19:11–16. How does the NT apply these verses from Isaiah? What does this tell us about the ministry of Jesus?
questions.answer:
Who is this? ‘I, proclaiming victory, mighty to save.’ Why are your garments red? ‘I have trodden the winepress of the nations and their blood spattered my garments.’

Comparing with 59:15–19: the same pattern — no one else to act; he does it himself; acts in judgment and vengeance but also saves and redeems. In chapter 59 we are told it is the Lord. Therefore the warrior of 63:1‑6 is the Lord himself coming in judgment.

Revelation 19:11–16 applies this to Jesus: the rider on the white horse, whose robe is dipped in blood, who treads the winepress of the fury of the wrath of God Almighty. Jesus is the agent of both the salvation and the judgment of the Lord. His first coming brought the salvation; his second coming will bring the full judgment.

questions.header: 63:7–64:12: Look down, come down
questions.question: How does chapter 63 follow from chapter 62? What’s the connection? Isaiah 63:7–14 seems a surprising response to what we read in vv1‑6. Why is it an appropriate response? What is Isaiah praising the Lord for?
questions.answer:
The Lord’s judgment on sin (ch 63:1‑6) is part of his salvation. He judges the enemies of his people to save his people. He judges the nations and gathers the nations. His judgment and salvation are always inextricably linked — chapter 62 promised restoration and chapter 63 shows the means: the warrior-judge who acts to save.

Isaiah responds to vv1‑6 by recounting the history of the Lord’s dealings with his people — a pattern of compassion, kindness, and salvation; of rebellion, judgment, and restoration. This is how the Lord deals with his people, so it is what they can expect. The mighty Lord who judges is the mighty Lord who saves (vv11–14). In the Exodus, judgment and salvation went together — this is cause for praise.

questions.header: 63:7–64:12: Look down, come down
questions.question: What does Isaiah remind the Lord of in 63:15–19? What does he call on the Lord to do? (64:1‑5) What reasoning is given? What does the Lord’s people confess? (64:5‑7) What is their final plea? (64:8–12)
questions.answer:
Isaiah reminds the Lord of: his character (holy, glorious, mighty, compassionate); that he is their Father and Redeemer; that they belong to him (63:16, 19).

He calls on the Lord to come down in judgment (64:1–3) so that even the mountains will tremble. But also in mercy, so they can be saved (vv5, 9). There is no other God who can act — he acts for those who wait for him. The reasoning: the Lord’s own name and reputation are bound up with his people’s situation.

Confession (64:5‑7): all our righteous acts are like filthy rags; we all wither like leaves; our sins sweep us away; we have turned away from the Lord. This is one of the most comprehensive confessions of human sinfulness in the OT. They cannot save themselves.

Final plea (64:8–12): you are our Father; we are the clay, you are the potter; we are all the work of your hand. Do not be angry beyond measure. Do not remember our sins for ever. Look at your people and their desolation. Will you remain silent and punish us beyond measure?
questions.leaderNote: The plea ‘rend the heavens and come down’ (64:1) is the desperate cry of those who feel that the Lord is hidden. It is answered in the NT by the incarnation: God does rend the heavens and come down, in the person of his Son. Chapters 65–66 are the Lord’s answer to this prayer.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: How do we see this pattern of the Lord’s work of judgment, compassion, and salvation throughout history and particularly in Jesus (at his first and second comings)? The judgment of the Lord is terrifying, but how does this passage help us see that it is also a cause for praise?
questions.answer:
Throughout history: the pattern of the Lord coming to judge and save — at the Exodus, in the Exile and return, in the first coming of Jesus (salvation), and at his second coming (judgment). Each act of judgment is in the service of salvation; each salvation involves the judgment of what opposes God’s people.

The judgment of the Lord as cause for praise: because the Lord’s judgment means that evil does not have the last word; that oppressors will not continue unopposed for ever; that the restoration promised in chapters 60–61 will come about because the warrior will ensure it. The same God who ‘rends the heavens and comes down’ in terrifying judgment is the Father, the potter, who will not abandon the work of his hands (64:8). Judgment and love are not contradictions in the Lord’s character — they are two expressions of the same absolute commitment to righteousness and to his people.
questions.leaderNote: The prayer of 63:7–64:12 could be used as a model for the group’s own corporate prayer at the end of the session. Consider reading vv8–12 together as a communal prayer — confessing, appealing, and trusting in the Lord as Father and potter.

chapters.title: Isaiah 65–66 — The Grand Finale: Sin Judged, Eden Restored, God’s People Saved for Ever
chapters.bigIdea: The grand finale is coming, where sin and the enemies of God will be destroyed, Eden restored, and God’s people saved and blessed in his presence for ever.

background.body:
Chapters 65–66 are the Lord’s answer to the prayer of 63:7–64:12 — specifically to the plea to ‘rend the heavens and come down’ (64:1). He will come down, and this is what he will do. These chapters bring the whole book of Isaiah to its conclusion, gathering up its major themes and driving them to their ultimate destination.

The Gentiles and the obstinate Israel (65:1–16). The Lord opens by addressing two contrasting groups: those who sought him without being asked (the Gentiles, v1 — confirmed by Paul in Romans 9:30 and 10:20) and his own people who have been obstinately rebellious (v2 — confirmed by Paul in Romans 10:21). The contrast that runs through chapters 40–66 — between the faithful remnant and the obstinate majority — reaches its climax here.

The new creation (65:17–25). This is the most direct and sustained description of the new creation in the whole OT. The Lord announces: ‘See, I will create new heavens and a new earth.’ Everything that characterises the Fall will be undone: no weeping or crying (v19), no untimely death (v20), no wasted labour (vv21–23), no misfortune (v23), no harm or destruction (v25). Instead: delight, joy, blessing, life, joyful labour and purpose, peace, and unity with God (v24). Isaiah 65:17–25 is the direct source for Revelation 21–22.

The final judgment and the new creation in chapter 66. Chapter 66 brings the whole book to its conclusion. The Lord who is too great to be contained (vv1–2) will welcome the humble and contrite (v2) but judge those who go through religious motions with hearts far from him (vv3–4). The new creation of chapter 65 is restated (vv22–23), and the book closes with the eternal destiny of the wicked (v24) — a verse Jesus quotes in Mark 9:43, 48 in his warnings about hell.

The closing refrain: ‘No peace for the wicked’ (48:22; 57:21; 66:24). This refrain has appeared three times in the second half of Isaiah (48:22; 57:21) and appears in its final and most solemn form in 66:24. The book ends not with universal salvation but with a clear distinction between those who belong to the Lord and those who do not. The eternal life of the redeemed is contrasted with the eternal death of the wicked. Both are real.

keyPoints.body:
The Lord has been actively seeking those who did not seek him. The astonishing opening of chapter 65 — ‘I revealed myself to those who did not ask for me; I was found by those who did not seek me’ — is the ultimate statement of divine initiative in salvation. The Gentiles, who had no prior relationship with the Lord, are found by him before they seek him. Paul reads this as the basis of the Gentile mission.

The Lord will save a remnant even from the obstinate. Despite Israel’s persistent rebellion (65:2–7), the Lord promises to preserve a remnant: ‘so will I do on behalf of my servants; I will not destroy them all’ (v8). The remnant theme that has run throughout Isaiah reaches its conclusion here: the Lord will not abandon his purposes, even when the majority reject him.

The new creation is the ultimate answer to the Fall. Isaiah 65:17–25 describes not the restoration of the old order but the creation of something new and better. The thorns and thistles of Genesis 3 are replaced by trees (55:13); the enmity and danger of the animal kingdom is resolved (65:25; cf. 11:6–9); the labour that was cursed is now joyful (65:21–23); the presence of God that was lost is restored (65:24). This is not a return to Eden but an advance beyond it.

The humble and contrite are welcomed; the formally religious are judged. The contrast of 66:2–4 is one of the most arresting in Isaiah: the Lord looks with favour on the humble and contrite who tremble at his word (v2), but those who go through religious motions while their hearts are far from him — the Lord treats their offerings as if they were pagan sacrifices (v3). This echoes the diagnosis of chapters 1 and 58 and brings the whole book’s concern with genuine vs formal religion to its conclusion.

The Lord’s plan is for the nations to be gathered to worship him. The closing vision of 66:18–21 is staggeringly inclusive: the Lord will gather people from all nations, and some of them he will send as missionaries to proclaim his glory to the nations that have not yet heard. He will even bring some as priests and Levites — roles previously restricted to Israelites. The book of Isaiah ends with a vision of universal worship that encompasses every nation under heaven.

exegetical.body:
‘I was found by those who did not seek me’ (65:1). Paul quotes this verse in Romans 10:20 and explicitly identifies it as referring to the Gentiles: ‘And Isaiah boldly says, “I was found by those who did not seek me; I revealed myself to those who did not ask for me.”’ The Lord’s reaching to the Gentiles — before they seek him, before they have any prior relationship with him — is the theological foundation of the Gentile mission.

The new heavens and new earth (65:17). This is the first explicit use of the phrase ‘new heavens and new earth’ in the Bible (though the concept underlies Isaiah 24–27 and chapter 35). The emphasis is on the word ‘create’ — this is not restoration or renovation of the existing order but a new act of creation. 2 Peter 3:13 and Revelation 21:1 both use this language from Isaiah 65.

The wolf and the snake (65:25). The echoing of Isaiah 11:6–9 is deliberate. In chapter 11 it was the Branch who brought about this state of peace; here it is the Lord’s new creation that establishes it. The two passages are deeply connected — the Branch’s work (chapter 11) is the means by which the new creation (chapter 65) comes about.

‘My people Egypt’... ‘a house of prayer for all nations’ (66:21). The vision of foreigners being brought as priests and Levites (66:21) is the ultimate fulfilment of the promise that the Lord’s house will be a house of prayer for all nations (56:7). The priestly function that was restricted to one tribe in Israel will, in the new creation, be exercised by people from every nation.

The closing verse (66:24). The book of Isaiah ends with a verse Jesus quotes three times in Mark 9:43–48 (‘where the worms do not die and the fire is not quenched’) as his warning about hell. The eternal consequence of rebellion against the Lord is not annihilation but ongoing exclusion from the Lord’s presence. This is the solemn note on which the book closes — not as the dominant note (the dominant note is the new creation and universal worship) but as the essential final word of warning. The book of Isaiah ends, as it began, with a call to choose.

pastorals.body:
This is the final session of the study and deserves to be handled with the weight it carries. The group has journeyed through the whole of Isaiah — from the devastating indictment of chapter 1 to the glorious new creation of chapters 65–66. Help them feel the arc before pressing into the details of these final chapters.

The description of the new creation (65:17–25) should be given extended time. It is not a footnote but the destination toward which the whole book has been pointing. Read it slowly, ask the group to notice specific details, and give space for wonder. The vision of vv17–25 is one of the most hopeful passages in all of Scripture — help the group receive it as such.

The closing verse (66:24) is uncomfortable and should be. The book of Isaiah does not end with universal salvation. The eternal consequence of persistent rebellion against the Lord is real and solemn. Help the group hold this honestly, without either minimising it or dwelling on it disproportionately. It is the final warning within a book that has been primarily about grace.

The final application questions for the whole study are an opportunity for genuine reflection and thanksgiving. What has the study meant? What has changed in how the group reads the OT, understands the Lord, or grasps the gospel? Allow significant time for this — it is the pastoral destination of the whole journey.

watch.body: Watch for the group treating the new creation of 65:17–25 as purely future and irrelevant to the present. The vision is future — but it shapes the present. Those who know where history is going live differently in the present. The new creation is not an escape from reality but the transformation of it: the work of our hands, the relationships we have, the creation we inhabit — all of it will be renewed, not discarded. That changes how we relate to the present world.

questions.header: 65:1–16: Those who seek the Lord will be blessed, those who ignore him will be judged
questions.question: Look at 65:1. Who is the Lord revealing himself to here? How do we know? Look at Romans 9:30 and 10:20 to help.
questions.answer: The Lord is revealing himself and being found by the Gentiles — those who had neither asked for the Lord nor sought him nor called on him. Paul makes this explicit in Romans 10:20: ‘And Isaiah boldly says, “I was found by those who did not seek me; I revealed myself to those who did not ask for me.”’ We know it refers to Gentiles because they have neither asked for the Lord, sought him, nor called on him — whereas Israel had at various times done all three.

questions.header: 65:1–16: Those who seek the Lord will be blessed, those who ignore him will be judged
questions.question: By contrast, how does Israel respond to him? (Cf. Romans 10:21.) What are the signs of Israel’s obstinacy and wickedness? (vv3‑5) What is the Lord promising in v8?
questions.answer:
Israel is obstinate — the image is of someone reaching out and pleading but being ignored (v2, quoted by Paul in Romans 10:21). They want to follow wicked ways they have dreamt up rather than following the Lord.

Signs of obstinacy and wickedness (vv3‑5): false worship, idolatry, focus on the dead (worshipping at graves), eating impure things, self-confidently believing themselves to be sacred. r>
The promise of v8: the Lord will save a remnant. Despite the widespread rebellion, he will not destroy them all — ‘as when juice is still found in a cluster of grapes and people say, “Don’t destroy it, there is still a blessing in it,” so will I do on behalf of my servants; I will not destroy them all.’

questions.header: 65:1–16: Those who seek the Lord will be blessed, those who ignore him will be judged
questions.question: What are the fundamental differences between those the Lord will spare and those he will judge? (vv9–12) What will the blessing and judgment of the Lord look like for these two groups? (vv13–16)
questions.answer:
Those the Lord will spare: chosen, his servants, they seek him. Those he will judge: forsake him, forget his place of blessing, turn to paganism (Fortune and Destiny), do not listen to the Lord, chose to do what is evil.

Blessing (vv13–15a): food, drink, joy, a new name, the memory of past trouble forgotten. Those who seek the Lord will be blessed with provision, joy, a new identity, and a new beginning.

Judgment (vv15b–16): want, shame, anguish, brokenness, their name associated with cursing, death. The Lord will judge both the community and the individual (‘you will be put to death’ in v15 is singular while the other ‘yous’ are plural).

questions.header: 65:17–25: The Lord will create a new heavens and new earth
questions.question: What will the Lord do to erase the memory of past troubles? (v17) What will be absent from the new creation? What do these absences suggest about what characterises the new creation? What does this passage remind you of? Where have we seen these ideas elsewhere in Isaiah and in the rest of the Bible?
questions.answer:
He will create new heavens and a new earth — a complete recreation, not merely renovation. It will be so glorious that past troubles will not be remembered or come to mind (v17).

What will be absent: no weeping or crying (v19); no untimely death (v20); no wasted labour (vv21–23); no misfortune (v23); no prey/predator (v25); no harm or destruction (v25). In short, everything that characterises the Fall will be undone.

The new creation will therefore be characterised by: delight, joy, blessing, life, joyful labour and purpose, peace, unity with God (v24), and the full knowledge of the Lord filling the earth (cf. 11:9).

Isaiah 11 — the Branch’s rule bringing the same animal peace (11:6–9); Isaiah 35 — the desert blooming and the redeemed returning; Revelation 21–22 — new heavens and new earth, no more death, crying, or pain; the presence of God filling everything. Isaiah 65:17–25 is one of the primary sources for Revelation 21.
questions.leaderNote: The question of whether the description in vv20–24 refers to the new creation or a Millennium is often raised. The notes suggest not getting into that debate. The main point is clear regardless: the Lord will create something new and better than the fallen world, where everything that went wrong will be set right. Help the group receive that vision in its fullness.

questions.header: 66:1–24: Wrath or blessing, which will it be?
questions.question: Isaiah 66 begins and ends with the dwelling place of God. He is too great to be contained (vv1‒2), yet welcomes humanity into his presence (vv18–23). What is the main difference between those the Lord will welcome and those he will judge? (vv2‒4)
questions.answer: The Lord looks with favour on those who are humble and contrite and tremble at his word (v2). Note: this does not imply they are sinless but that they recognise their sin before the Lord and respond to his word with genuine reverence. The repetition of ‘those who tremble at his word’ (v2 and v5) is significant. The Lord will punish harshly those who have chosen their own ways while refusing to listen to him (v4). The main difference between them is how they respond to the Lord: with pride, stubbornness, and refusal to listen — or with repentance, fear, and listening.

questions.header: 66:1–24: Wrath or blessing, which will it be?
questions.question: What is the Lord’s assessment of those who refuse to listen to him? (vv3‑4) How do these people behave toward those who do listen to the Lord? (vv5)
questions.answer:
They may as well be pagans. The Lord regards their offerings in the same way as unclean and idolatrous sacrifices. They delight in themselves and their empty religion — echoing the diagnosis of chapters 1 and 58 one final time.

They hate those who listen to the Lord. They are ‘brothers’ in the same religious community but they have no time for the true people of God. This is sobering: God’s people can expect persecution not only from unbelievers but also from those in their own religious community who value their own ways more than the word of the Lord.

questions.header: 66:1–24: Wrath or blessing, which will it be?
questions.question: How will the Lord bless those who listen to him and tremble at his word? (vv7–14) What will happen to the wicked and why? (vv14–17) What is the Lord’s plan for the world and how are his people part of it? (vv18–21)
questions.answer:
Blessing (vv7–14): the Lord will bless them by increasing them, giving them joy, and overflowing provision. The comfort the Lord offers (cf. Isaiah 40) can only be experienced as part of Zion. He will flourish his people and make himself known to them. The nations will stream in to be part of the people of God (cf. Revelation 21:24).

The wicked (vv14–17): the Lord will punish his enemies and destroy them — those who have tried to save themselves through their own rituals and have followed other powers rather than the Lord. The one they follow will also meet their end.

The Lord’s plan (vv18–21): he will gather his people from every language and nation (cf. Revelation 7:9). His people will go out to proclaim his glory to the ends of the earth. The scattered people of God will be gathered in (cf. John 11:52). They will worship the Lord and be a priestly people. Even those from distant nations will serve as priests and Levites.
questions.leaderNote: The detail of vv18–21 is remarkable: the Lord will bring people from Tarshish, Libya, Lydia, Tubal, Greece, and the distant islands — the furthest reaches of the known world in Isaiah’s day. And some of them he will bring as priests and Levites. This is the ultimate extension of the promise of 56:3‑8 — the foreigner and the eunuch welcomed into the temple — to its fullest possible conclusion.

questions.header: 66:1–24: Wrath or blessing, which will it be?
questions.question: What is the grand finale of all history? (vv22–24)
questions.answer: The Lord’s people will be as enduring as the eternal new heavens and earth he will create. They will enjoy worshipping him for ever — every Sabbath and every new moon, all flesh will come to worship before the Lord (v23). But sin and the enemies of God will be destroyed by eternal death — ‘their worm will not die, nor will their fire be quenched, and they will be loathsome to all mankind’ (v24). Jesus quotes this verse in Mark 9:43–48 as his warning about hell. The book of Isaiah ends not with a comfort but with a warning — calling the reader to choose, as it did at the very beginning.

questions.header: Application
questions.question: How can we be people who tremble at the Lord’s word? How can this passage give us confidence in spite of our sin? What difference does it make to know that the new heavens and earth are coming?
questions.answer:
Trembling at the Lord’s word: genuine engagement with Scripture that takes it personally, as the word of the living God addressed to us — not merely intellectual study or religious routine. The posture of humility and contrition (v2) is the mark of those who truly belong to the Lord, more than any outward religious performance.

Confidence despite sin: the Lord saves a remnant even from the obstinate (65:8); he revealed himself to those who did not seek him (65:1); he is Father and potter who does not abandon the work of his hands (64:8). The new creation does not depend on our merit but on the Lord’s creative power and faithful love.

The difference the new creation makes: everything we do — our work, our relationships, our care for creation — is not ultimately futile (65:23). The renewal that is coming is not the destruction of this world but its transformation. Our labours are not in vain in the Lord (1 Corinthians 15:58). The new creation gives the present meaning rather than making it irrelevant.
questions.leaderNote: Allow significant time for this final application question — and for broader reflection on the whole study. What has studying Isaiah meant for the group? What has changed in their understanding of the Lord, of sin, of grace, of the gospel? What passage has struck them most? How has the study affected their prayer and their daily lives? The whole study has been building to this point — give it the time it deserves.

questions.header: Closing reflection: The whole of Isaiah
questions.question: The book of Isaiah has taken us from the devastating indictment of chapter 1 to the vision of new creation in chapters 65–66. Looking back over the whole study: what are your big takeaways? What has changed in how you read the Old Testament? How has your understanding of Jesus deepened? What aspects of the Lord’s character have struck you most? And what does it mean to live in light of everything Isaiah has shown us?
questions.answer: Open reflection question for the whole group. No single answer — this is a moment for each person to share what has been most significant for them personally. The leader may want to prepare a brief summary of the arc of the whole book (from ‘your hands are full of blood’ in 1:15 to ‘come, all you who are thirsty’ in 55:1 to ‘I will create new heavens and a new earth’ in 65:17) as a framework for the discussion. Then give generous time for the group to reflect, share, and give thanks.
questions.leaderNote: Consider ending this final session with a time of prayer and worship. Perhaps read Isaiah 12 (the song from session 6) together as a final act of praise — the right response to everything Isaiah has shown us. Or read Revelation 21:1‡5 together as the ultimate destination to which the whole study has been pointing. The goal of the study has been not just knowledge but transformation — the kind that begins with encountering the Lord as he truly is: holy, sovereign, gracious, and faithful.

paras: Julie Rowbory grew up in Northern Ireland and was educated at the University of Cambridge, where she obtained an MA and then an MPhil in history. She has worked as a Latin and history teacher, taught English in Asia and lived in East Africa. She is married to David, with whom she has four daughters. They divide their time between Scotland and West Africa.
